The bill explicitly subjects the Council's board members and employees to the Public Ethics Law, which is administered and enforced by the Commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the statutory requirement that local tax collectors in Maryland (with the exception of Baltimore City, which was already exempt) sell property with taxes in arrears within a specific two-year timeframe, or one year in Calvert County. Instead, it grants local governments the authority to establish their own timelines for tax sales through local law.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland local government tax collectors and county or municipal governing bodies
qa_results.stakeholders: Property owners with unpaid taxes, local government authorities, and small businesses that may be subject to tax sales or involved in the tax sale process.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill may result in a delay of State Annuity Bond Fund revenues and local government revenues beginning in fiscal 2024 if local governments choose to allow longer periods of tax arrears before conducting tax sales. Because the extent of any local timing changes is unknown, specific dollar estimates are unavailable and the impact is considered indeterminate.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the implementation of state laws and proposed the legislation. The Governor signed this bill into law, affecting how local jurisdictions manage property tax arrears.

The Comptroller is the state's principal tax collector and chief financial officer. While property taxes are primarily local, the state receives a portion that flows into the Annuity Bond Fund; the bill's impact on the timing of tax sales could delay these state revenues.

The Treasurer manages the state's financial assets and the Annuity Bond Fund, which is used to pay debt service on state bonds. The fiscal note explicitly mentions that revenues for this fund may be delayed due to the changes in tax sale requirements.

DBM is responsible for revenue estimation and fiscal planning. Changes in the timing of property tax collections impact the state's long-term fiscal projections and the management of the Annuity Bond Fund.

The judicial branch oversees the legal proceedings associated with tax sales. The Administrative Office of the Courts was a consulted party for the fiscal note, reflecting the bill's relevance to court-ordered property sales.

The OAG serves as legal counsel to the state and its agencies. It would be responsible for advising state entities on the legal implications of changes to the Tax-Property Article and any subsequent litigation involving tax sale timing.

The fiscal note highlights a potential meaningful impact on small businesses, either by providing them more time to pay taxes in arrears or by impacting businesses that generate revenue from tax sales.

The Tax Court adjudicates disputes regarding tax assessments and collections. Changes to the mandatory timeline for tax sales could result in new categories of administrative disputes or appeals falling under the court's jurisdiction.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill adds the State Ethics Commission to the list of public bodies subject to enhanced requirements under the Open Meetings Act. It requires the commission to livestream its open meetings, post agendas and meeting materials online at least 48 hours in advance, and maintain archived video recordings and minutes on its website. The Department of Information Technology is tasked with providing the necessary technical staff and equipment for these livestreams.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Ethics Commission and the Department of Information Technology
qa_results.stakeholders: State Ethics Commission, Department of Information Technology, and members of the public seeking access to commission meetings.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, State general fund expenditures increase by $10,000 in fiscal 2024 only for the State Ethics Commission to reimburse the Department of Information Technology (DoIT) for procuring video streaming equipment and support services. Reimbursable revenues and expenditures for DoIT increase by the same amount in fiscal 2024. Ongoing technical support costs are expected to be minimal and absorbable. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill is the primary subject of the legislation, which adds the Commission to the list of agencies subject to the enhanced transparency requirements of the Open Meetings Act, including livestreaming and online documentation.
The bill explicitly mandates that DoIT provide the technical staff, support, and equipment necessary to livestream the open meetings of the State Ethics Commission.
The OAG provides legal guidance on the Open Meetings Act and is mentioned as an information source regarding the bill's implementation.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note identifies that the Open Meetings Law Compliance Board, which falls under this category, would be responsible for handling complaints regarding the bill's requirements.

The Board of Elections is specifically named in the section of the bill (Section 3-307(h)) concerning the Department of Information Technology's requirement to provide technical support for livestreaming.
The MDTA is explicitly named in the technical support mandate (Section 3-307(h)) and has unique provisions within the act (Section 3-307(g)) that are reaffirmed in the bill text.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits local jurisdictions from imposing unreasonable limitations or land use restrictions on utility-designated pollinator areas managed by public service companies. It defines requirements for pollinator-friendly vegetation management, including specific mowing schedules—typically limited to once every other year during winter months—to protect and maintain habitats for native pollinators and wildlife on utility rights-of-way and easements.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Public Service Commission and local jurisdictions
qa_results.stakeholders: Public service companies, local governments, native pollinators, and wildlife
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not materially affect State or local government finances or operations. Specific dollar estimates are unavailable because no material fiscal impact was identified by the Department of Legislative Services or responding local governments. Small business impact is expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill governs the vegetation management practices of public service companies, which are primarily regulated by the Public Service Commission. The Commission already sets service quality and reliability standards that include mandatory vegetation management.

The bill directly amends the Land Use Article and prohibits local jurisdictions from imposing certain land use restrictions on utility-designated pollinator areas. The Department of Planning is the principal agency for promoting sustainable growth and providing technical assistance to local governments on land use policy.

The bill specifically references the Department of Agriculture as a source for defining native plants and states that the act does not modify existing state or local laws relating to weed control under the Agriculture Article.

The bill focuses on creating and maintaining habitats for native pollinators and wildlife. The Department of Natural Resources is responsible for conserving and managing the state's natural resources and biodiversity.
The bill relates to the management of utility-controlled land and rights-of-way, impacting energy infrastructure and the promotion of environmentally sound energy delivery practices.

Public service companies often maintain easements and rights-of-way on land managed by the Department of Transportation. The bill’s restrictions on mowing and land use could affect the management of state-owned transportation networks.

As an agency that manages extensive lane miles and rights-of-way, SHA is directly impacted by changes to how utility companies manage vegetation within those corridors, particularly regarding mowing schedules and habitat protection.

The bill defines 'Integrated Vegetation Management,' which includes the use of chemical methods/herbicides. The Department of the Environment enforces state laws regarding pollution control and environmental protection related to these activities.

Vegetation management is a significant operational expense for utilities. Changes in these requirements can impact utility reliability and the rates paid by residential consumers, whom the Office of the People's Counsel represents.
This multi-agency initiative focuses on sustainable development and resource conservation. The bill's promotion of pollinator-friendly land use aligns with the initiative's planning visions.
The bill affects land use in rural counties where many large-scale utility rights-of-way are located, impacting rural environmental stewardship and agriculture.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the General Assembly and state agencies regarding the interpretation and enforcement of land use and public utility laws.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill renames the Certified Local Farm Enterprise Program to the Certified Local Farm and Fish Program, expanding its purpose to include food from certified Chesapeake invasive species providers within the 20% procurement goal for State agencies. It also renames the associated grant fund, establishes certification requirements for invasive species providers, and mandates an annual report on program implementation and participation.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland State agencies, local farms, seafood dealers, Chesapeake Bay watermen, and the Maryland Agricultural and Resource-Based Industry Development Corporation.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Agriculture can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. The bill mandates a general fund appropriation of $2,735,000 in fiscal 2025 to the Maryland Agricultural and Resource-Based Industry Development Corporation (MARBIDCO), of which $435,000 is specifically designated for grants and near-equity investments to support agricultural aggregation and participation in the Certified Local Farm and Fish Program. There is no fiscal impact on local governments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Agriculture is the primary implementing agency for the bill. It houses the Office for the Certified Local Farm and Fish Program, is responsible for certifying providers, developing a directory of certified entities, and adopting regulations to implement the program.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Agricultural & Resource-Based Industry Development Corp.
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MARBIDCO is explicitly named as the administrator of the Certified Local Farm and Fish Food Aggregation Grant Fund, which supports food aggregation, storage, and processing sites through grants and investments.

The bill defines invasive species based on the Maryland Aquatic Nuisance Species Management Plan and refers to seafood dealer licensing under the Natural Resources Article. DNR oversees the conservation and management of these fisheries.
The Board of Public Works is responsible for receiving annual reports of procurement waivers from the Office and maintaining records of these waivers for legislative committees.
As the state's central procurement authority, DGS is impacted by changes to procurement procedures and the expansion of the 20% procurement goal to include invasive species fish for all state units.
The bill explicitly includes the University System of Maryland as a university subject to the procurement goals and regulations of the Certified Local Farm and Fish Program.
The bill explicitly includes Morgan State University as a university subject to the procurement goals and regulations of the Certified Local Farm and Fish Program.
The bill explicitly includes St. Mary's College of Maryland as a university subject to the procurement goals and regulations of the Certified Local Farm and Fish Program.
As an agency overseeing public education and school meal programs, MSDE and local school systems are key 'units' impacted by food procurement goals.
The Maryland Department of Health operates various facilities and programs that require significant food procurement, making it a key participant in reaching the 20% local procurement goal.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DPSCS is a major state procurer of food for its correctional facilities and is directly impacted by the requirement to meet local farm and fish procurement goals.
DJS operates residential facilities for youth that require food procurement, making it a relevant unit under the bill's expanded program goals.
The school operates dining facilities for its students and is specifically identified in the fiscal note as an agency relevant to food procurement goals.
The department oversees the Charlotte Hall Veterans Home, which procures food for residents and is subject to the bill's procurement preferences.
The MFCA manages wholesale produce and seafood markets which serve as critical infrastructure for the food aggregation and distribution goals supported by the bill.
The Council advocates for the economic interests of Maryland's rural counties, including the agricultural and seafood industries supported by this procurement program.
As a member of the Board of Public Works, the Comptroller oversees significant state contracts and receives reports regarding procurement waivers established by this program.
As a member of the Board of Public Works, the Treasurer is involved in the oversight of the state's fiscal integrity and procurement processes relevant to the program.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Department of Agriculture, regarding the development of regulations and the legality of procurement preferences.
MDE is responsible for environmental restoration and water quality; the management of invasive species finfish helps protect the ecological balance of the Chesapeake Bay.
OLA conducts audits of state agencies and may evaluate compliance with the 20% procurement goal and the management of the food aggregation grant fund.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland State Board of Contract Appeals
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MSBCA adjudicates bid protests and contract disputes; new procurement goals and preferences for invasive species providers may lead to disputes requiring the board's review.
OAH provides impartial hearings for state agencies; it may be involved in cases regarding the decertification of local farms or invasive species providers.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: GOSMWBA oversees socioeconomic procurement programs; the 20% local farm and fish goal functions similarly to the MBE and SBR programs it manages.
DBM is responsible for the state's budget development and long-term fiscal planning, including oversight of agency spending on food procurement.
DHS administers social service programs that may involve food procurement or distribution, making it a relevant unit for reaching the 20% procurement goal.
As a major state agency with various administrative arms, MDOT is a 'unit' that must consider local farm and fish goals in its applicable food procurements.
The Department of Aging oversees nutrition programs for older adults and is a relevant unit for implementing state procurement goals for local food products.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the service charge that a circuit court or the District Court may impose for a dishonored check from $10 to $30.
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals and entities who issue checks to Maryland courts that are returned for insufficient funds or other reasons.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: General fund revenues are estimated to increase by a minimum of $7,600 in fiscal 2024 and by a minimum of $10,100 annually thereafter. There may be a potential minimal increase in special fund revenues for the Central Collection Unit. No material effect on expenditures or local finances is anticipated.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Administrative Office of the Courts provides operational and financial support to the Maryland Judicial Branch. This bill directly affects the fees that the Circuit Courts and the District Court are authorized to collect for dishonored checks.

The Department of Budget and Management houses the Central Collection Unit (CCU), which is responsible for collecting delinquent debts owed to the state. The bill's increase of the service charge to $30 reaches the minimum threshold required for the CCU to take collection action.

As the state's chief financial officer, the Comptroller is responsible for overseeing the collection of state revenues. The increased fees authorized by this bill will contribute to a projected increase in General Fund revenues.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, including the Department of Budget and Management and its collection units, and is responsible for the state's annual budget, which will be minimally impacted by the increase in general fund revenues.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Safe Access for All (SAFE) Roads Act of 2023 requires the Maryland Department of Transportation to implement immediate, near-term safety improvements for any approved pedestrian or bicycle safety project expected to take longer than 12 months to complete. Additionally, the State Highway Administration must submit reports to the General Assembly regarding staffing levels, turnover, signalized crosswalk approvals, and installation plans for safe pedestrian crossings in suburban activity centers and traditional town centers.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Transportation (MDOT) and the State Highway Administration (SHA)
qa_results.stakeholders: Pedestrians, bicyclists, and employees of the State Highway Administration
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Transportation (MDOT) may be required to redirect funding from other capital projects beginning in FY 2023 to implement near-term safety improvements. However, the fiscal note indicates this impact is expected to be minimal because the capital program is fully subscribed. There is no projected impact on state revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Transportation (MDOT) is the primary agency responsible for implementing the bill's requirements. Specifically, it must implement incremental safety improvements for projects taking over 12 months and the State Highway Administration (SHA), a modal unit of MDOT, must submit detailed staffing, signalization, and infrastructure reports.

The bill requires pedestrian crossings to be placed at specific intervals in areas with 'significant transit dependency.' The MTA provides the transit services and data that define these areas, and the infrastructure improvements directly impact access to transit systems.

The bill requires SHA to report on wage, benefit, and compensation levels, as well as turnover rates and open positions. DBM is the principal personnel agency for the state and oversees the state budget, which would be impacted by potential funding redirections noted in the fiscal note.

The MDTA manages toll facilities, which are part of the state's highway network. While the bill focuses largely on SHA roads, the safety design standards and incremental improvement requirements for bicycle and pedestrian access could apply to MDTA-managed state highways.
The MVA houses the Highway Safety Office, which is involved in traffic safety initiatives and pedestrian safety programs, aligning with the bill's focus on 'SAFE' roads.

The bill mandates safety analysis and plans for state highways in 'suburban activity centers' and 'traditional town centers,' which are planning designations. MDP provides the land use and planning expertise that intersects with these transportation infrastructure requirements.

The bill requires detailed reporting on staff turnover and its effect on implementation speed. GOPI utilizes performance metrics to improve the efficiency and responsiveness of state government operations, making these reports directly relevant to their mission.

OLA is responsible for fiscal compliance and performance audits of executive agencies. They may be required to verify MDOT's compliance with the reporting mandates and the implementation of incremental safety improvements.

This multi-agency initiative focuses on sustainable development and community revitalization. Pedestrian and bicycle safety infrastructure is a core component of creating walkable, sustainable communities.

As the executive office overseeing the Department of Transportation and the annual state budget, the Governor's Office is responsible for the high-level coordination and policy implementation of the SAFE Roads Act.
The OAG provides legal counsel to MDOT and SHA regarding the implementation of new transportation mandates and regulatory compliance.
The bill requires SHA to report on staffing vacancies and turnover, which are indicators of labor conditions and management efficiency overseen by the PERB for the public workforce.
As the primary law enforcement agency for state highways, the MSP has a stake in traffic safety improvements, though the bill focuses on engineering and administration rather than enforcement.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill revises the definition of 'charitable contribution' to exclude certain in-kind donations and discounts on services or materials. It also allows the Secretary of State to accept alternative documentation instead of a required audit or review for the registration of specific charitable organizations, primarily those that solicit in-kind contributions and have limited cash receipts.
qa_results.stakeholders: Charitable organizations operating in Maryland and the Maryland Secretary of State's office.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Secretary of State may experience an increase in workload and potential temporary staffing costs to process an influx of requests for registration and reporting waivers. State revenues are not expected to be materially affected. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Secretary of State is the primary agency responsible for the registration and oversight of charitable organizations in Maryland. The bill directly alters its regulatory processes by changing the definition of 'charitable contribution' and authorizing the SOS to accept alternative documentation in place of mandatory audits or reviews for certain organizations.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the legal representative for the Secretary of State and provides counsel on the implementation of state regulations. Additionally, it enforces consumer protection laws related to charitable solicitations and organizations, which are impacted by the definition changes in the bill.

The Department of Budget and Management is the state's principal fiscal and personnel agency. The bill's fiscal note indicates that the Secretary of State may require additional staffing resources to manage the potential increase in requests for documentation waivers, which falls under DBM's oversight.

This office facilitates partnerships between state government and nonprofit organizations. Changes to the regulatory and reporting requirements for charitable organizations affect the fiscal and administrative landscape for the nonprofit entities that the office coordinates and supports.

The bill explicitly excludes discounts on broadcast airtime and public service announcements from the definition of charitable contributions. This change affects how charitable organizations report their partnerships with the Commission, which provides these services via Maryland Public Television (MPT).

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) and the Maryland Department of Health (MDH) to update the Maryland State School Health Service guidelines regarding anaphylactic food allergies. Local boards of education are required to adopt and implement guidelines to reduce the risk of allergen exposure in schools by the 2024-2025 school year. Additionally, public schools must establish a system to disclose major food allergens in the meals they serve and implement safety strategies such as designating food-free areas and allergen-free tables in cafeterias.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE), Maryland Department of Health (MDH), and local county boards of education
qa_results.stakeholders: Public school students with anaphylactic food allergies, parents, school administrators, teachers, and school food service employees
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the Maryland State Department of Education and the Maryland Department of Health can update the required guidelines using existing budgeted resources. Local boards of education and public schools are also expected to handle the adoption of guidelines and the development of allergen disclosure systems with existing budgeted resources. The bill is not expected to affect state or local revenues.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MSDE is explicitly required by the bill to jointly update the Maryland State school health service guidelines for reducing the risk of exposure to food allergens. The department also provides oversight and leadership for the local boards of education and public schools that must implement and publish these guidelines.

MDH is explicitly required to jointly update the Maryland State school health service guidelines alongside MSDE. Furthermore, the department oversees school health services and local health departments, whose staff (including school nurses) are responsible for implementing allergy assessments and emergency plans for students.

The bill requires school guidelines to provide information about Section 504 plans, which are used to provide accommodations for students with disabilities, including severe food allergies. This aligns with the department's role in coordinating services and ensuring inclusive policies for Maryland residents with disabilities.
As the chief legal counsel for state agencies, the OAG is responsible for reviewing the updated health service guidelines for legal sufficiency, as noted in the fiscal note analysis.

The agency is tasked with fostering safe and supportive learning environments. The bill's requirements to establish strategies for reducing bullying and harassment of students with allergies and implementing exposure risk reduction strategies fall within the agency's broad mandate for school safety.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires a tax collector to attempt to contact a tax sale certificate holder via phone and email if they fail to provide a property owner or mortgagee with the amount of expenses required for property redemption. If the certificate holder does not respond within five business days of the collector's attempt, the collector is authorized to redeem the property without a satisfaction letter that would otherwise verify the holder's expenses have been paid.
qa_results.stakeholders: Property owners, mortgagees, tax sale certificate holders, and local tax collection offices
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not directly affect State finances and is not anticipated to materially affect local government finances. Small business impact is expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill modifies the procedural requirements for property tax sale redemptions and the reimbursement of legal expenses incurred in actions to foreclose the right of redemption. Since these actions are civil proceedings filed in Maryland's circuit courts, the Administrative Office of the Courts is impacted as it provides operational and legal support for judicial proceedings.

As the chief legal representative for the state, the Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel on the interpretation of the Tax-Property Article and has a policy interest in the legal integrity of tax sale foreclosure and redemption processes.

The Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development is responsible for ensuring access to secure housing and supporting community revitalization. Procedural changes that facilitate property redemption and prevent the final foreclosure of the right of redemption directly align with the agency's mission to promote homeownership and stable communities.

As the state's principal tax collector, the Comptroller has a general interest in laws governing the collection of revenues and the administration of tax sales, although property taxes are primarily collected at the local level.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives. This bill relates to the Governor's role in enforcing state laws and his administration's broader policy priorities regarding property rights and housing.

The Maryland Department of Aging advocates for the rights of older adults. Because seniors are a population significantly affected by property tax sales and the loss of their homes, the department has a relevant interest in policies that streamline the redemption process.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Maryland Comptroller to disclose specific tax information of business entities to the Maryland Small Business Retirement Savings Board and its authorized contractors. The information shared is limited to federal employer identification numbers, business names, addresses, and contact details for the purpose of administering the Maryland Small Business Retirement Savings Program and Trust.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Comptroller and the Maryland Small Business Retirement Savings Board
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland small business entities, the Maryland Small Business Retirement Savings Board, and authorized program contractors.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on State or local finances. According to the fiscal note, the Comptroller's Office can handle the additional workload associated with the expanded disclosure authorization using existing resources, and there are no changes to State or local revenues or expenditures.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the state's principal tax collector, the Comptroller is the agency authorized and directed by the bill to disclose specific business tax information to the Maryland Small Business Retirement Savings Board.

MarylandSaves is the state-sponsored retirement program administered by the Maryland Small Business Retirement Savings Board, which is the entity explicitly authorized by the bill to receive tax information to facilitate the administration of the program.

The State Treasurer's Office manages several state financial and savings programs; the fiscal note indicates the Treasurer's Office provided analysis on how this bill would streamline registration and tracking for the MarylandSaves program.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Comptroller and the Board, regarding the legality, implementation, and privacy safeguards required for the disclosure of tax information.

The Maryland Small Business Retirement Savings Program is authorized under the Labor and Employment Article of the Maryland Code, and its focus on private-sector worker benefits aligns with the Department's general mission to protect workers and support businesses.

DoIT is responsible for state data governance and cybersecurity; the bill's provisions regarding the disclosure of tax data to external boards and contractors fall under its strategic and security oversight.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office coordinates state policies and programs for small businesses, and this bill directly impacts the administrative efficiency of a state retirement program designed specifically for the small business community.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes Maryland counties and municipalities to adopt local ordinances regulating invasive bamboo. Local governments may prohibit the sale and planting of invasive bamboo and require property owners to use containment measures, such as barriers or trenches, to prevent bamboo from spreading to neighboring properties or public rights-of-way. The bill also establishes civil fine structures for local enforcement.
qa_results.responsible_party: Governing bodies of Maryland counties and municipalities
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland property owners, local government authorities, landscaping companies, and retail plant nurseries.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no impact on state finances. Local governments can implement the bill's provisions using existing resources. There is a potential increase in local revenue from civil fines, which are limited to $50 for a first offense and $200 for subsequent offenses within a 12-month period, with a total cap of $3,000 for violations arising from the same set of facts. Small businesses, particularly nurseries and landscapers, may see meaningful financial impacts depending on local regulations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Agriculture is responsible for the classification of Tier 1 and Tier 2 invasive plants. While the bill authorizes local regulation, it operates within the context of the invasive species regulatory framework managed by the Secretary of Agriculture.
DNR is cited as an information source for the bill and is responsible for publishing the list of native plants that serve as alternatives to invasive species like bamboo.

The bill has a potentially meaningful impact on small businesses, including retail nurseries and landscaping services, which fall under the economic oversight and support domain of the Department of Commerce.
The Governor signed the bill into law and oversees the executive agencies (MDA and DNR) impacted by invasive species policy.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the state agencies involved in environmental and agricultural regulation, and would oversee the legal sufficiency of the intersection between state and local invasive plant laws.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill mandates that ground leases and amendments are not legally registered until they are posted on the State Department of Assessments and Taxation’s (SDAT) online registry. It prohibits ground lease holders from collecting or attempting to collect rent, late fees, interest, or collection costs if the lease is not registered. Additionally, the bill establishes an administrative process for leasehold tenants to challenge collection attempts through affidavits submitted to SDAT and provides a mechanism for judicial review of SDAT's decisions in circuit court.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT)
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland ground lease holders (grantors) and leasehold tenants (grantees).
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the State Department of Assessments and Taxation and the Judiciary can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. There is no impact on State or local revenues, as SDAT is prohibited from charging fees to carry out the registration and dispute resolution provisions established by the bill.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates a new cause of action and specific judicial procedures for ground leaseholders and leasehold tenants to challenge decisions made by the state regarding the legality of rent collection. It dictates court venues, timelines for filing, and requirements for the court to enter specific orders directing the state to void or uphold registrations.

As the chief legal representative for state agencies, the OAG would be responsible for providing legal counsel to the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT) during the development of the new administrative process, the drafting of regulations, and the creation of form affidavits. They may also represent the state's interest if administrative processes are legally challenged.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, which includes the Department of Assessments and Taxation (the primary agency implementing the bill). The Governor also signed this bill into law and is responsible for overall executive policy regarding property and registration regulation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill removes a restriction that prohibited leasehold tenants from submitting documentation and payments to the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT) for ground rent redemption or extinguishment until at least 90 days after their application was posted online. Under the bill, tenants may submit these materials earlier, though SDAT will still wait until the 90-day posting period is complete before issuing the redemption or extinguishment certificate.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Department of Assessments and Taxation
qa_results.stakeholders: Leasehold tenants, ground lease holders, and the Mayor and City Council of Baltimore City
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note indicates that the bill will likely result in operational efficiencies for the State Department of Assessments and Taxation but is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government finances. Specific dollar estimates for revenue or expenditure changes are unavailable as the bill primarily modifies the administrative timeline for existing processes.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes specific judicial procedures, including authorizing leasehold tenants to file interpleader actions in circuit court and allowing ground lease holders to appeal valuation disputes in the Circuit Court of Baltimore City.

As the chief legal officer for the state, the OAG provides legal counsel to the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT) regarding the implementation of the bill's new documentation requirements and the adoption of necessary regulations.

The bill streamlines the redemption and extinguishment of ground rents, which are identified in the bill as impediments to the redevelopment of abandoned and distressed property, directly supporting the department's mission of community revitalization and housing security.

The bill provides that uncollected ground rent redemption and extinguishment funds shall escheat to the State General Fund after 20 years, which falls under the Comptroller's duties as the state's chief financial officer and principal collector of revenues.

The Governor oversees the executive branch agencies responsible for implementing these changes, including the State Department of Assessments and Taxation, and is responsible for signing the legislation and overseeing its general enforcement.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies mandatory property insurance requirements for condominiums in Maryland. It distinguishes between attached or multifamily units, where the council of unit owners must maintain insurance on both common elements and the units themselves (excluding owner improvements), and detached units, where the council is only required to insure common elements. Additionally, it requires owners of detached residential units to carry their own homeowners insurance for the entire unit.
qa_results.responsible_party: Council of unit owners for condominiums
qa_results.stakeholders: Condominium unit owners, condominium councils of unit owners, and insurance providers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not affect State or local government operations or finances. The impact on small businesses is considered minimal, as the bill primarily redistributes insurance responsibilities between condominium councils and individual unit owners.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly alters mandatory property and casualty insurance coverage requirements for condominiums, including specific mandates for detached units. The Maryland Insurance Administration is responsible for regulating the insurance market and reviewing policy forms to ensure compliance with state law.

The Secretary of State is responsible for the registration and oversight of condominium regimes in Maryland. Since this bill changes the legal requirements for condominium insurance under the Maryland Condominium Act, it impacts the regulatory framework for the entities registered with this office.

The Office of the Attorney General's Consumer Protection Division has statutory authority over violations of common ownership community laws, including the Maryland Condominium Act. This agency would be responsible for legal guidance and potentially enforcing the consumer rights associated with these insurance changes.

The bill amends the Real Property Article regarding insurance liabilities and proceeds disbursement. As disputes over condominium insurance and deductibles are often litigated in the state's judicial system, the Administrative Office of the Courts was consulted for its impact on the judiciary.
As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the primary implementing agencies (MIA and SOS) and signed the legislation into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of Agriculture to develop a pet protection fact sheet for individuals facing eviction or property repossession. Landlords and the Maryland Judiciary must provide links to this fact sheet during lease execution or warrant issuance. During a repossession, officials must inspect the premises for pets, return them to the owner, or, if the owner is absent, arrange for an animal shelter or rescue to take custody and provide contact details to the owner. It prohibits abandoning pets on public property during such actions.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Agriculture, Maryland Judiciary, and law enforcement officials (sheriffs and constables)
qa_results.stakeholders: Tenants with pets, landlords, animal shelters, animal rescue organizations, and law enforcement officials
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the Maryland Department of Agriculture and the Maryland Judiciary can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. No impact on state revenues is expected, and the bill is not anticipated to materially affect local government operations or finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates the Department of Agriculture to develop, publish, and regularly update a fact sheet regarding the care and protection of pets during evictions or the loss of possession of real property.
The bill requires the Maryland Judiciary to include a link to the pet protection fact sheet with every warrant issued in failure to pay rent, breach of lease, and other eviction-related actions.

The bill places specific requirements on 'other officials' who deliver possession of property following an eviction, including inspecting the premises for pets and coordinating with animal shelters, a role that state law enforcement may fulfill in certain jurisdictions or circumstances.

The bill impacts eviction processes and requires landlord communication regarding pet protections, which intersects with the agency's mission to ensure access to secure housing and community revitalization.

The Governor's Office oversees the Department of Agriculture and signs such legislation into law; the Governor's administration is responsible for the overall execution of state policy regarding pet welfare and tenant protections.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Agriculture and the Judiciary regarding the implementation of the new statutory requirements and ensures compliance with real property laws.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the methods by which a municipal chief executive officer can provide public notice of a proposed charter amendment. It allows for an alternative to the four-week newspaper publication requirement, permitting a single newspaper publication combined with direct delivery (by hand, mail, or municipal newsletter) of the amendment summary to every residence. The bill also extends the required public posting period for certain amendments to 40 days and requires municipalities to maintain delivery records.
qa_results.responsible_party: Chief executive officers of Maryland municipalities
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of Maryland municipalities and municipal government officials
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on State finances. According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not materially affect municipal expenditures, and municipal revenues are not affected. Specific dollar estimates for the potential savings or costs of switching notice methods are not provided.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the central depository for government records of permanent value from municipal governments, the Maryland State Archives is responsible for preserving municipal charters and their amendments. The bill specifically references municipal records retention and document disposal schedules, which are overseen and approved by the State Archives.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel on the interpretation of the Local Government Article and ensures compliance with state laws such as the Maryland Public Information Act, which the bill explicitly requires municipalities to follow regarding charter amendment records.

The Maryland Department of Planning provides technical assistance and policy guidance to local governments. Charter amendments are fundamental to municipal governance and land use authority, areas where MDP supports local jurisdictions.

The bill modifies notice requirements and timelines (changing 4 weeks to 40 days for certain postings) for charter amendments submitted to referendums, which are local elections overseen by the regulatory framework managed by the State Board of Elections.
The Governor's Office manages intergovernmental relations with local entities and coordinates policy initiatives that affect municipal operations and governance across the state.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of Health to establish an Express Lane Eligibility Program by January 1, 2025, to enroll or renew individuals in the Maryland Medical Assistance Program (Medicaid) and the Maryland Children’s Health Program based on their eligibility for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP). It also extends the reporting deadline for the Workgroup on Low-Income Utility Assistance, requiring a final report on study findings and recommendations to be submitted by January 1, 2024.
qa_results.stakeholders: Low-income individuals and families eligible for SNAP, Medicaid, or the Maryland Children’s Health Program, and low-income utility customers seeking financial assistance.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the financial impact on State revenues and expenditures to implement the Express Lane Eligibility Program is indeterminate. Any administrative or program costs incurred by the Maryland Department of Health would be eligible for a 61% federal matching rate. The Workgroup on Low-Income Utility Assistance is expected to complete its final report using existing budgeted resources, and there is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is the primary agency responsible for establishing and administering the Express Lane Eligibility Program to enroll individuals in the Maryland Medical Assistance Program (Medicaid) and the Maryland Children’s Health Program (MCHP).

The Department of Human Services (DHS) is responsible for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP), which provides the eligibility data for the Express Lane Eligibility Program. Additionally, DHS is responsible for staffing the Workgroup on Low-Income Utility Assistance.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill mandates specific reporting requirements for the Workgroup on Low-Income Utility Assistance, a temporary body tasked with studying financial assistance for residential utility customers.

The bill involves a workgroup studying low-income utility assistance, which directly intersects with the Maryland Energy Administration's role in managing energy assistance programs and advising on state energy policy.

The Maryland Health Benefit Exchange manages the platform for health coverage enrollment; the implementation of Express Lane Eligibility for Medicaid and MCHP will likely require coordination with their systems and enrollment processes.

The Office of the People's Counsel represents the interests of residential utility consumers and is a critical stakeholder in the Workgroup on Low-Income Utility Assistance's findings and recommendations.

The Public Service Commission regulates public utilities and is a key participant in the Workgroup on Low-Income Utility Assistance, which examines financial assistance for electric and natural gas customers.

The establishment of the Express Lane Eligibility Program is subject to the limitations of the state budget, which is overseen by DBM. The fiscal note also indicates indeterminate impact on revenues and expenditures.

The Express Lane Eligibility Program requires data matching and coordination between MDH and DHS systems, which may require oversight or technical support regarding the state's IT infrastructure and data governance.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments (MDH, DHS) implementing these programs and coordinating the policy initiatives related to child poverty and utility assistance.
The bill focuses on enrolling children in health programs (MCHP) and addressing low-income assistance, which aligns with the office's mission to reduce child poverty and improve child well-being.
The team is tasked with addressing child poverty and economic mobility; the Express Lane Eligibility program for Medicaid/MCHP is a data-driven strategy directly aligned with these priorities.
The Workgroup on Low-Income Utility Assistance studies gaps in assistance for the elderly, and seniors are often beneficiaries of the utility assistance programs under review.
Medicaid enrollment and utility assistance programs are highly relevant to residents with disabilities, whose access to care and essential services may be improved by streamlined enrollment.
The implementation of 'Express Lane' eligibility is a performance-focused initiative designed to increase government efficiency and reduce administrative duplication.
The center's mission to lower energy costs for consumers and advance energy efficiency is relevant to the Workgroup on Low-Income Utility Assistance's focus on residential utility affordability.
The MHCC's role in planning for the state's health system and improving access to health care makes the eligibility and enrollment changes to Medicaid and MCHP relevant to their work.
The OAG provides legal counsel to MDH and DHS and would likely be involved in drafting or reviewing federal waivers (such as Section 1115 waivers) required by the bill.

Access to healthcare and utility affordability are critical issues in Maryland's rural counties, making the workgroup's recommendations and health program enrollment changes relevant to rural advocacy.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill creates an Ocean City promoter's license in Worcester County, authorizing the Board of License Commissioners to issue the license to for-profit organizations to sell and serve beer, wine, and liquor at entertainment events held on property owned by the Town of Ocean City.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of License Commissioners for Worcester County
qa_results.stakeholders: For-profit organizations, the Town of Ocean City, the Ocean City Police Department, and event attendees in Worcester County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, Worcester County revenues are expected to increase by $25,000 in fiscal 2024, based on the estimate that five Ocean City promoter's licenses will be issued at a fee of $5,000 each. There is no impact on State revenue or expenditures, and local enforcement can be handled with existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller is the state's principal official responsible for regulating the alcohol industry and collecting state revenues. This bill modifies the Alcoholic Beverages Article by establishing a new promoter's license in Worcester County, which falls under the regulatory purview described in the agency's summary.

The bill facilitates entertainment events in Ocean City, a premier tourism destination. The agency is responsible for driving the tourism economy and promoting such assets and events to domestic and international markets.

As the primary economic development agency overseeing tourism and the arts, the Department of Commerce is impacted by legislation that creates new frameworks for large-scale entertainment events designed to attract visitors and generate local economic activity.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the state and provides counsel to the General Assembly and state agencies on the implementation and enforcement of state laws, including changes to the Alcoholic Beverages Article.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and the enforcement of state laws, and the Governor is responsible for signing this bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the regulations surrounding ground leases in Maryland by prohibiting ground lease holders of unregistered leases from requiring tenants to hold more than three years of rent in escrow. It requires the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT) to create and host standardized forms for ground rent billing and notices of possession. Additionally, it establishes stricter mailing requirements for ground lease holders, prevents them from charging tenants for these mailing costs, and mandates that ground lease transferees notify SDAT of any transfers.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Department of Assessments and Taxation
qa_results.stakeholders: Ground lease holders, leasehold tenants, and the State Department of Assessments and Taxation
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT) can comply with the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government finances or operations. Small business impacts are expected to be minimal, though ground lease holders are prohibited from being reimbursed by tenants for specific mailing and notice costs.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes specific notice and mailing requirements that must be met before a ground lease holder can file an action for possession for nonpayment of ground rent in the state court system. The AOC provides operational and legal support to the Maryland Judicial Branch, which handles these cases.

The OAG serves as the primary legal counsel for state agencies, including the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT), which is tasked by this bill to develop new form notices and bills. It also has a mandate to enforce consumer protection laws, which are relevant to the rights of leasehold tenants.

The bill includes a formal declaration of intent by the General Assembly regarding the State Department of Assessments and Taxation's ground rent redemption program, which falls under the executive branch's oversight. The Governor's Office coordinates policy initiatives and oversees executive branch enforcement of laws.

The department's mission is to ensure safe and secure housing for Maryland residents. Ground lease regulations and redemption programs are central to property ownership and housing stability in several Maryland jurisdictions.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees budget execution and personnel for state agencies. While the fiscal note indicates the bill can be implemented with existing resources, DBM is responsible for the overall fiscal planning for the department implementing these changes.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires mental health treatment plans to include long-range discharge goals and inpatient stay estimates, mandates regular clinical reviews, and allows family participation in the treatment process with patient consent. It also establishes a grievance and appeals process for patients in State-operated facilities through the Resident Grievance System and requires the Maryland Department of Health to submit annual reports to the General Assembly.
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals with mental disorders admitted to health care facilities, family members of patients, and facility treatment staff
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures may increase to hire additional staff to manage treatment plan reviews, reassessments, and appeals, and to cover costs for patients requiring treatment at outside facilities. A specific dollar estimate is unavailable as the impact depends on the volume of requests. The Maryland Department of Health can handle regulatory and reporting requirements within existing budgeted resources. Local fiscal impact is negligible.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency overseeing state behavioral health facilities and the administration of the Resident Grievance System, MDH is responsible for implementing the new treatment plan requirements, adopting regulations for the appeals process, managing the increased staffing needs, and submitting the annual report to the General Assembly.

The bill text explicitly designates the Office of Administrative Hearings to conduct de novo hearings and issue decisions for individuals in State facilities who request a reconsideration of their treatment plan review and reassessment.

The fiscal note indicates that MDH will require additional general fund expenditures for personnel and potential treatment costs outside of state facilities to comply with the bill, which falls under DBM's oversight of state budget and personnel management.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Public Defender is responsible for representing indigent individuals in involuntary commitment and mental health proceedings; they are listed as an information source for the bill due to their role in safeguarding the rights of facility residents.
The bill provides for judicial review of administrative decisions in the Circuit Court, impacting the judicial branch's operations. The AOC is also listed as an information source in the fiscal note.
The OAG provides legal counsel to MDH and would be responsible for helping draft the required regulations and representing the state in administrative or judicial appeals.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DPSCS is listed as an information source in the fiscal note, as they manage individuals with mental health needs within correctional settings and may interact with the state's mental health facility and grievance systems.

The Department of Disabilities coordinates services for residents with disabilities, including mental health disorders, and ensures that policies are inclusive and integrated, making this bill relevant to their advocacy and coordination mission.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies involved (MDH) and the Governor signed the bill into law; the office also coordinates broad policy initiatives related to behavioral health.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill reestablishes the authorization for certain plug-in electric drive vehicles to use high occupancy vehicle (HOV) lanes regardless of the number of passengers, provided the vehicle has a maximum speed of at least 65 miles per hour and the owner obtains a permit from the Motor Vehicle Administration.
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners of plug-in electric drive vehicles in Maryland
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Transportation Trust Fund (TTF) revenues are estimated to increase by up to $380,000 through fiscal 2026, assuming the Motor Vehicle Administration (MVA) charges the maximum authorized fee of $20 for an estimated 19,000 permits. TTF expenditures are expected to increase by approximately $26,220 through September 30, 2025, to cover the cost of purchasing HOV decals. MVA can implement reporting and programming requirements using existing resources. There is no fiscal impact on local governments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MVA is the primary agency responsible for issuing the HOV permits, collecting the associated fees (up to $20), and co-authoring the annual report on HOV lane operation and permit usage.

SHA designates HOV lanes and is responsible for their management. Under the bill, SHA must consult on permit design, recommend limits on the number of permits to prevent lane degradation, and co-author annual reports to the Governor.

As the parent agency of both MVA and SHA, MDOT oversees the implementation of this policy. Additionally, the fiscal impact of permit fees and decal expenditures directly affects the Transportation Trust Fund (TTF), which MDOT manages.
The Department of State Police is specifically tasked with consulting on the design of the HOV permit and is responsible for enforcing traffic laws related to HOV lane restrictions.

The Governor's Office is responsible for overseeing the executive branch agencies (MVA, SHA, MSP) implementing the bill. Furthermore, the bill mandates that MVA and SHA provide an annual report directly to the Governor regarding the impact of the permits on HOV lane operations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill streamlines Maryland's government reporting requirements by repealing, consolidating, and rescheduling numerous mandated reports from various State agencies. The revisions target reports that are obsolete, duplicative, or unnecessary, aiming to improve administrative efficiency and ensure that only relevant data is collected and reported to the General Assembly.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland State agencies and departments, the Maryland General Assembly, local government entities, and local school systems.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is procedural in nature and has no direct effect on State or local finances. According to the Fiscal Note, some agencies may experience operational efficiencies due to the repeal, rescheduling, or consolidation of existing reporting requirements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is the recipient of numerous mandated reports addressed in this bill, and the Governor is responsible for appointing members to several boards whose reporting requirements are being modified or repealed.
The bill modifies the reporting dates for the Comptroller regarding beer production for various license holders and updates the Comptroller's role in auditing 9-1-1 fee collection and remittances.
The bill centralizes telework reporting requirements, making the Department responsible for submitting a single consolidated report for all state units rather than each unit submitting individually.
The bill repeals a reporting requirement for the Department regarding conditional loans or grants provided to companies for headquarters construction and retention.

The bill modifies reporting for the LYNX High School and affects reporting mechanisms for students who are foster care recipients or homeless youth, which involves coordination with the State Board of Education.
The bill modifies the annual reporting requirements for the 9-1-1 Board, which is overseen by the Department of Emergency Management, including updates on Next Generation 9-1-1 transitions.
The bill modifies reporting dates and requirements for climate change mitigation, the Bay Restoration Fund, and solid waste management/recycling programs administered by the Department.
The bill repeals the requirement for the Department (via the Records Management Division) to submit a consolidated annual report on forms management activities from all state departments.
The Department takes over the lead reporting role for home visiting programs and has reporting date adjustments for the Governor's Wellmobile Program and tobacco retailer violations.
The bill reduces the frequency of the Department's reports on minority business participation in the Energy Efficient Homes Construction Loan Program from semi-annual to annual.
The Department is designated as the lead agency for submitting the outcomes evaluation report for children's services, coordinating with other departments.
The bill repeals a reporting requirement for the Department related to the 'End the Violence' P.R.O.T.E.C.T. program.
The Department will receive precinct boundary information electronically rather than via written documents and continues to co-author reports on the impact of wastewater treatment upgrades on growth.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill modifies inmate data reporting (removing place of birth) and repeals the Department's reporting role in the P.R.O.T.E.C.T. program.
As the parent agency of the Motor Vehicle Administration, the Department is impacted by the repeal of several MVA reporting mandates concerning service of process and organ donation.
The Commission takes on new reporting duties for FAFSA completion data and foster care/homeless youth data while several of its other reporting requirements are repealed or modified.
The bill repeals the Department of State Police's reporting requirement regarding the progress of the 'End the Violence' P.R.O.T.E.C.T. program.
The bill modifies how the State Administrator sends precinct boundary descriptions and maps to other departments, requiring electronic transmission.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill repeals reporting requirements for the Criminal Justice Information Advisory Board regarding interoperability and modifies reporting for other advisory bodies.
The Children's Cabinet, of which this office is a key part, has its reporting requirements for home visiting programs modified and shifted to the Maryland Department of Health.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The office (formerly GOCPYVS) is removed as the lead reporter for home visiting programs and has its reporting role for the P.R.O.T.E.C.T. program repealed.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The office will no longer receive certain video lottery employee data reports from the State Lottery and Gaming Control Commission due to the repeal of that requirement.
The bill repeals a requirement for the Board to submit an annual report on the procurement system, as this information is already reported by the Procurement Advisor.
The bill repeals the requirement for the Commission (referenced as the Public School Construction Program) to report annually on the use of alternative financing mechanisms for school construction.
The reporting date for the Center's annual report and its report on the use of the Fund and investment outcomes is changed from October to December.
The bill transfers certain FAFSA reporting duties to the Higher Education Commission and modifies the Center's requirements for publishing foster care and homeless youth data dashboards.
The bill removes the requirement for the Commission's independent audit reports to be submitted to the Department of Legislative Services.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland State Lottery & Gaming Control Agency
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill repeals requirements for the Commission to submit data regarding Baltimore City video lottery operation employees.
The bill repeals several reporting requirements for the Board of Trustees related to rehired retirees in local school systems.
The bill reduces the corporation's reporting frequency from quarterly to every six months and repeals a duplicative report on the Maryland Technology Incubator Program.
As a public institution of higher education, the university is impacted by changes in how data on foster care recipients and homeless youth is reported to the state.
Several of the MVA's reporting requirements are repealed, including those regarding service of process for nonresidents and annual reports on organ donor designations on licenses.
The bill repeals a reporting requirement where the Public Service Commission was required to use input from the Attorney General regarding VoIP consumer complaints.
The bill repeals a reporting requirement where the Public Service Commission was required to use input from the People's Counsel regarding VoIP consumer complaints.
The bill repeals the Commission's discretionary reporting requirement concerning consumer protections and complaints for Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) services.
As a public institution of higher education, the college is impacted by changes in how data on foster care recipients and homeless youth is reported to the state.
The bill repeals reporting requirements for the Board of Regents regarding business entities established by the university and specifically repeals reporting for UMCEED.
The bill maintains the Service's role in coordinating with the Office of Recycling to study and report on recycling centers and solid waste management.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits the District Court from ordering an individual to appear for an examination or answer written interrogatories to enforce or execute a money judgment resulting from a small claims action (claims for $5,000 or less).
qa_results.stakeholders: Small claims judgment creditors (including small businesses), judgment debtors, and the Maryland District Court.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not expected to materially affect State or local finances. Implementation costs for the District Court, such as updating forms and webpages, can be handled with existing budgeted resources. Small businesses may experience a meaningful impact due to the limitation of methods available to collect on small claims judgments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts District Court operations by prohibiting specific enforcement tools (examinations and interrogatories) for small claims judgments. The Administrative Office of the Courts is the primary agency responsible for updating the court's forms, digital systems, and public brochures to comply with these procedural changes.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill removes the legal basis for body attachments (arrests for civil contempt) in small claims cases by prohibiting the prerequisite orders for examination. This impact is noted in the fiscal note as it reduces the potential for individuals to be taken into custody and processed within the state's detention systems for these specific civil matters.

As the chief legal officer for Maryland, the OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies regarding changes in judicial procedures and the enforcement of money judgments, and may be involved in interpreting the impact of these changes on state-held small claims judgments.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill converts enhanced underinsured motorist (EUIM) coverage from an opt-in to an opt-out option for new private passenger motor vehicle liability insurance policies issued on or after July 1, 2024. Insurers are required to provide EUIM coverage unless the insured affirmatively waives it in writing. The bill also mandates the Maryland Insurance Administration to study the impact of this change on premiums and consumer selection, with reporting requirements through 2028.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland drivers purchasing private passenger motor vehicle insurance and motor vehicle liability insurance companies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund revenues for the Maryland Insurance Administration (MIA) increase minimally from a $125 rate and form filing fee. General fund revenues from the 2% premium tax may increase minimally because EUIM coverage is generally more expensive than standard uninsured motorist coverage, resulting in higher total premiums. The MIA can implement the bill's requirements and conduct the mandatory study using existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary regulator of Maryland's insurance market, the MIA is responsible for implementing the transition to an opt-out system for enhanced underinsured motorist coverage, which includes reviewing and approving the required waiver forms and conducting a mandatory multi-year study on the impact of the coverage conversion on premiums and consumer choice.

As the state-created insurer of last resort for drivers unable to obtain private insurance, MAIF is directly impacted by changes to coverage standards; additionally, the bill explicitly establishes that enhanced underinsured motorist coverage is primary to any right to recovery from MAIF.

The bill requires the Maryland Insurance Administration to conduct a formal study and submit interim and final reports regarding its findings and recommendations on the insurance coverage conversion directly to the Governor.

The bill prohibits the use of binding arbitration for disputes related to enhanced underinsured motorist coverage, effectively directing these legal challenges into the Maryland court system and potentially impacting judicial caseloads.

As the state's principal tax collector, the Comptroller oversees the general fund, which is anticipated to receive a minimal increase in revenue from insurance premium taxes due to the potentially higher premiums associated with the shift toward enhanced underinsured motorist coverage.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the criminal offense of unnatural or perverted sexual practice from Maryland law. It updates references in various statutes—including those related to child abuse, vulnerable adult abuse, and sex offender registration—to instead refer to any other sexual conduct that is a crime, and it establishes specific rules for the expungement or vacating of past convictions related to this repealed offense.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary, Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services, and State's Attorneys' offices
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents, the judiciary, law enforcement, and individuals with past convictions for the repealed offense.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not expected to materially affect State or local government operations or finances. According to the Fiscal Note, the statute being repealed was already largely unenforceable for consensual private activity due to existing case law, and the number of annual convictions is minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts judicial operations by altering the criminal code, establishing new criteria for expungement petitions, and providing for motions to vacate judgments in specific circumstances. The AOC provides the operational and technological support for the courts to process these filings.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill enables individuals convicted under the repealed statute to file motions to vacate judgments if they were victims of human trafficking and to seek expungements. The OPD will represent indigent defendants in these proceedings.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifies classifications for Tier III sex offenders for past convictions and impacts the maintenance of criminal records and the sex offender registry, both of which are managed by DPSCS.

The bill amends the Family Law Article and Criminal Law Article regarding the definition of 'sexual abuse' and 'sexual molestation or exploitation' of children, which are critical to the department's child protective services and social service missions.
As the chief legal representative of the state, the OAG provides counsel to state agencies on the repeal of criminal statutes and handles appeals related to criminal convictions and registry status.
The repeal of a criminal statute and changes to definitions of sexual activity affect law enforcement investigations, charging procedures, and the maintenance of police records.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The GOCPP coordinates public safety strategies and advises the Governor on criminal justice policy; changes to sexual offense laws and sex offender tiering fall under its policy purview.
The bill's provisions regarding the expungement of police and court records impact the Archives' role as the central depository for permanent government records.
Changes to definitions of 'sexual abuse' and 'sexual molestation' of minors are relevant to the department's assessment and supervision of youth in the juvenile justice system.
The bill amends definitions related to the abuse and exploitation of 'vulnerable adults,' a population for whom MDH sets policy and provides health and safety services.
The bill's modification of child abuse and exploitation definitions relates to the office's mission to coordinate networks that protect and promote the well-being of Maryland's children.

The repeal of the 'unnatural or perverted sexual practice' statute is historically and legally relevant to civil rights, as these laws were frequently used in a discriminatory manner against the LGBTQ+ community.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill increases the qualifications for a journeyman license in heating, ventilation, air-conditioning, and refrigeration (HVACR) services by requiring applicants to have held an apprentice license for at least four years (up from three) and to have completed at least 6,000 hours of training (up from 1,875).
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Heating, Ventilation, Air-Conditioning, and Refrigeration Contractors
qa_results.stakeholders: HVACR apprentices, applicants for HVACR journeyman licenses, and HVACR contractors and small businesses.
