qa_results.bill_summary: The Maryland Consolidated Capital Bond Loan of 2023 authorizes the creation of State Debt through the issuance of general obligation bonds to fund capital improvements. These funds are used for the planning, construction, renovation, and equipping of State-owned facilities, as well as providing grants to local governments and nonprofit organizations for various community, educational, and historical projects. The bill also amends previous capital bond acts from 2011 through 2022 and provides preauthorizations for capital debt in 2024, 2025, and 2026.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland state agencies and departments, local governments, nonprofit organizations, public and nonpublic schools, community colleges, and higher education institutions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill authorizes $1,218,639,000 in State Debt for fiscal year 2024, though net new debt is limited to $1,205,000,000 after accounting for $13,639,000 in reductions to prior authorizations. Additionally, it provides $29,100,000 in bond premium proceeds. The act also sets preauthorizations for future debt: $629,689,000 for 2024; $239,789,000 for 2025; and $32,307,000 for 2026. Debt service is funded by an annual tax on all assessable property in the State.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the chief executive, the Governor proposes the annual state budget, including the capital budget (MCCBL), and serves as the chair of the Board of Public Works, which is the primary authority for issuing the bonds and approving the expenditures authorized by this bill.
The Comptroller is a member of the Board of Public Works and is explicitly required by the bill to make transfers, adjustments, and reconciliations of bond proceeds and premiums.

The State Treasurer is a member of the Board of Public Works and is responsible for managing the issuance and sale of state general obligation bonds, receiving the proceeds, and determining bond premiums.
The Board of Public Works is the central authority named in the bill to borrow money, issue bonds, approve all project expenditures, and certify matching funds.

DBM is the principal fiscal agency responsible for supervising budget execution and must approve the use of design or construction funds for other project phases or the credit of excess funds to the Construction Contingency Fund.

DGS is the primary operational support agency for state construction and facilities management; it is responsible for executing many of the projects funded and must submit reports on the Construction Contingency Fund.
The bill provides significant funding for the Maryland Stadium Authority to construct the new Department of Legislative Services building and conduct various feasibility studies.
The bill authorizes multi-million dollar debts for the design and construction of several new judicial facilities, including a new Courts of Appeal building and Harford County District Court.
The Department of Health receives substantial funding for community health facilities, addiction treatment centers, and renovations at state hospitals such as Clifton T. Perkins.
The IAC is the primary body responsible for the planning and funding of public school construction projects authorized under this bill, including the Public School Construction Program.
The bill authorizes extensive funding for capital projects across multiple USM campuses, including UM College Park, UM Baltimore, Towson University, and others.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill funds several major correctional facility projects, including a new women's prerelease center and electrical infrastructure upgrades.
DNR receives funding for the Natural Resources Development Fund, oyster restoration, and various community park and playground grants.
MDE is responsible for administering grants for flood management, water supply assistance, and the transition to zero-emission school buses authorized by the bill.
The bill funds major transportation-related projects, including capital maintenance for WMATA and grants for bus rapid transit and zero-emission buses.
As an independent state agency, Morgan State University receives specific funding for several major campus projects, including its New Health and Human Services Building.
The bill funds several MDP programs, specifically the Maryland Historical Trust's preservation grants and the Jefferson Patterson Park and Museum.
The bill authorizes funding for the design and construction of several new Maryland State Police barracks and specialized operations buildings.
MSDE, through the IAC and Aging Schools Program, oversees the distribution of school construction and renovation grants funded by this debt.
MHEC is responsible for administering the Community College Construction Grant Program, which receives significant allocations in this bill.
DHCD is tasked with administering the Business Facade Improvement Program and grants for shelter and transitional housing facilities.
MDEM receives direct funding for the renovation and expansion of its headquarters at Camp Fretterd.
DoIT receives funding to continue construction of the statewide unified public safety radio communications system.
The bill funds the Maryland Agricultural Cost-Share Program, which is administered by the Department of Agriculture to reduce nutrient runoff.
The agency receives funding for the expansion and improvement of the Garrison Forest Veterans Cemetery.
The Military Department receives funding for readiness center renovations and improvements at the Freestate Challenge Academy.
The bill authorizes debt for the design of new residences and a student center at the Maryland School for the Deaf.
The college receives funding for campus infrastructure improvements and building renovations.
The bill provides funding for the Public Library Capital Grant Program, which the agency coordinates for the state's library systems.
The Department of Aging administers the Senior Citizens Activities Centers Capital Improvement Grants Program funded by this bill.
MES receives funding for the Infrastructure Improvement Fund to upgrade water and wastewater facilities at various state institutions.
The bill provides funding for accessibility modifications at state-owned facilities, which the Department of Disabilities oversees.
The agency is responsible for administering the Maryland Arts Capital Grant Program authorized in this bill.
The bill allocates funds specifically for the creation of cannabis incubator spaces, contingent on related legislation.
MEDCO is tasked with managing funds for secure information facilities and cannabis incubator spaces.
The commission receives funding for site improvements, exhibit design, and the construction of a storage facility for the Maryland Dove.
The bill provides a grant to the Chesapeake Bay Trust for infrastructure improvements to its green campus.
The OAG provides legal counsel for the bond issuances and represents the state agencies involved in the large-scale procurements authorized by the bill.
OLA is responsible for conducting fiscal compliance audits of the agencies that will expend the billion-dollar bond proceeds authorized by this bill.
The Secretary of State is the official custodian of the executive orders and laws of the state, including this capital budget act.
The Archives manages the state's historical records and the Commission on Artistic Property, which may be involved in the preservation of artwork during renovations funded by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Budget Reconciliation and Financing Act of 2023 executes actions to provide mandate relief, contain costs, change the use of special funds, and swap funds in the fiscal 2024 Maryland operating budget. Key provisions include altering the specialization requirements for faculty at the University of Maryland Capital Region Medical Center, reducing the Medicaid Deficit Assessment for fiscal year 2024, repealing certain repayment requirements for the Local Reserve Account, and transferring $500,000,000 from the Revenue Stabilization Account to the Blueprint for Maryland’s Future Fund.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Budget and Management
qa_results.stakeholders: State agencies, Maryland hospitals, homeowners receiving property tax credits, recipients of Department of Human Services assistance payments, and natural gas customers in the Washington Gas service territory.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, general fund expenditures increase by $49,500,000 in FY 2024 and decrease by $500,500,000 in FY 2027. Special fund revenues decrease by $50,000,000 in FY 2024, and special fund expenditures decrease by $40,800,000 in FY 2024. The bill also reduces the FY 2023 general fund appropriation for the Assistance Payments Program within the Department of Human Services by $7,500,000.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As an Administration bill, the Governor's Office is the primary sponsor and overseeing body for this budget reconciliation act, which alters appropriations and revenue distributions to align with the Governor's fiscal priorities.
As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM is directly responsible for the development and execution of the budget changes mandated by this bill, including cost containment and fund swaps.

The Comptroller is responsible for paying eligible homeowners' property tax refunds from the Local Reserve Account and serves on the Board of Public Works, which oversees the capital expenditure carryovers mentioned in the bill.
The Treasurer manages state funds and serves on the Board of Public Works, which oversees and approves the significant state fiscal changes and property transfers described in the bill.
The bill directly impacts the Department of Health by altering the Medicaid Deficit Assessment for fiscal 2024 and affecting hospital assessment and remittance policies.
The bill specifically reduces the fiscal 2023 general fund appropriation for the Department's Assistance Payments Program by $7.5 million.
The bill extends the deadline for the transfer of a portion of the Bainbridge Naval Training Center site to the Department for the creation of the Port Deposit State Historical Park.
The bill authorizes the transfer of unexpended capital appropriations, which DGS manages, to provide funding for specific library projects in Baltimore County.
The bill authorizes the Maryland Energy Administration to use $9.25 million in merger-derived funds for the repair of natural gas infrastructure in the Washington Gas service territory.
The bill alters the clinical care faculty assignment requirements for the University of Maryland School of Medicine at the Capital Region Medical Center.
The bill involves the management of unspent capital appropriations and property transfers that require approval or oversight by the Board of Public Works.
The bill specifies appropriations to the accumulation funds of the State Retirement and Pension System based on general fund surpluses.
The bill redirects $500 million from the Rainy Day Fund to the Blueprint for Maryland’s Future Fund, which supports the state's public education system overseen by MSDE.
The bill references funds derived from a merger approved by the Public Service Commission and authorizes their specific use for infrastructure repair.
The bill references policies to be adopted by the Commission and the Health Department regarding hospital assessment and remittance revenue.
The bill includes specific appropriation instructions for the Maryland Equity Investment Fund, which is located within TEDCO.
The bill facilitates the transfer of $10 million in grant funding specifically to support the Woodlawn and Essex library systems.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the eligibility criteria for Montessori schools and programs to participate in the Prekindergarten Expansion Grant Program and other publicly funded prekindergarten initiatives. It establishes specific requirements for staff credentials, requiring teachers to hold a bachelor's degree and a recognized Montessori credential, and sets unique student-to-staff ratios and classroom size limits tailored to the Montessori educational model.
qa_results.stakeholders: Montessori schools and programs, prekindergarten students and their families, and the Maryland State Department of Education.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note indicates there is no anticipated material impact on State or local funding. While the bill alters eligibility requirements for Montessori schools and programs, it is not expected to change the overall levels of State or local expenditures or revenues. Montessori programs that are small businesses may benefit from the specific eligibility requirements established by the bill.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees the state budget and the execution of funding for the Prekindergarten Expansion Grant Program, which this bill modifies.

MSDE is the primary agency responsible for the leadership and oversight of Maryland's public education system, including the administration of prekindergarten programs and grants modified by this bill.

The Governor's Office oversees policy initiatives related to early childhood education and the implementation of the Blueprint for Maryland's Future, which this bill adjusts regarding Montessori programs.
The GOC focuses on child well-being and reducing poverty; expanding access to quality early childhood education is a core component of their policy mission.
The MLDSC links student performance and workforce data from MSDE; changes to prekindergarten program eligibility and staffing will impact the data they collect and analyze for state policy.
This team pilots initiatives to address child poverty and enhance economic mobility, areas where early childhood education strategy is a critical factor.
The OAG provides legal counsel to MSDE and would be involved in interpreting and defending the adjusted regulatory requirements for Montessori schools.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill makes several technical and administrative revisions to Maryland's Corporations and Associations laws. Key provisions include prohibiting the issuance of stock, scrip, or limited liability company membership certificates in bearer form; clarifying that stockholder meetings can be held partially or entirely by remote communication; and requiring Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) to file a formal notice of termination with the State Department of Assessments and Taxation when undergoing voluntary dissolution. It also allows nonstock corporations to include ex officio directors in their charter or bylaws and applies the Maryland Control Share Acquisition Act to certain statutory trusts.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT)
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland corporations, stockholders, limited liability company (LLC) members, nonstock corporation directors, and Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT) trustees.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note indicates that the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government operations or finances. While the bill introduces new filing requirements for REIT dissolutions and modifies various corporate governance procedures, these changes are largely technical and are not expected to result in significant changes to revenue or expenditures.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly grants the Attorney General the authority to institute proceedings to dissolve a real estate investment trust (REIT) that has abused or misused its powers. Additionally, the Office of the Attorney General's Securities Division is listed as a primary information source for the fiscal note, reflecting its interest in provisions related to the issuance of stock and convertible securities.

The bill makes significant revisions to the Corporations and Associations Article, which governs the legal structure and operations of businesses in Maryland. These provisions, including those related to remote meetings, stock issuance, and REIT dissolution, are subject to judicial interpretation and enforcement in state courts. The bill also specifies the venue for judicial proceedings regarding REIT dissolutions.

As the chief executive, the Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies (such as the State Department of Assessments and Taxation, though not on this list) that implement corporate filing requirements. The Governor also signed this bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill staggers the election and appointment terms for the Baltimore County Board of Education. It mandates that the seven elected members be elected in gubernatorial election years and the four members appointed by the Governor be appointed in presidential election years. To align these schedules, the bill extends the terms of the four currently appointed members, originally set to expire in 2026, to terminate in 2028.
qa_results.responsible_party: Governor of Maryland, the Baltimore County School Board Nominating Commission, and Baltimore County Public Schools
qa_results.stakeholders: Appointed and elected members of the Baltimore County Board of Education, candidates for the board, and the residents and voters of Baltimore County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is procedural in nature and has no state or local fiscal impact. According to the fiscal note, Baltimore County finances are not affected and there is no state expenditure or revenue change.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly involves the Governor's statutory authority to appoint members to the Baltimore County Board of Education, altering the timeline and requirements for these appointments.

This office is responsible for assisting the Governor in recruiting and vetting candidates for state and local boards, including the appointments to the Baltimore County Board of Education modified by this bill.

The bill codifies that the seven elected members of the Baltimore County Board of Education must be elected in gubernatorial election years, a process supervised and administered by the State Board of Elections.
The bill amends the Education Article regarding the leadership and governance of a local school system, which falls under the leadership and oversight of the Maryland State Department of Education.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill involves the Baltimore County School Board Nominating Commission, an advisory body tasked with providing the list of nominees from which the Governor must make appointments.

As the custodian of official records and the agency that processes commissions for the Governor's appointees, the Secretary of State's office is involved in the formalization of these board appointments.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill establishes the Commission on Public Health to make recommendations for improving the delivery of foundational public health services in Maryland. The Commission is tasked with assessing the capabilities of the Maryland Department of Health and local health departments in areas such as communicable disease control, chronic disease prevention, and emergency preparedness. It requires the Commission to submit an interim report by December 2023 and a final report by December 2024.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents, the Maryland Department of Health, local health departments, State academic institutions, and public health professionals.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no fiscal impact in FY 2023. Any impact on the Maryland Department of Health and other State agencies can be absorbed within existing budgeted resources. Higher education expenditures for State academic institutions providing staff support to the Commission may increase in FY 2024 and 2025, but specific dollar estimates are unavailable. Local health departments can absorb any impact within existing budgeted resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health is the primary subject of the commission's assessment and foundational public health capability review. Furthermore, three senior MDH officials—the Deputy Secretary for Public Health, the Deputy Secretary for Behavioral Health, and the Director of the Office of Minority Health and Health Disparities—are designated members of the Commission.

The Executive Director of the Maryland Health Care Commission is a statutory member of the new Commission on Public Health, and the commission is explicitly required to consult with the MHCC in performing its duties.

The Executive Director of the Health Services Cost Review Commission is a member of the Commission on Public Health, and the bill requires the Commission to consult with the HSCRC to inform its recommendations for public health reform.

The bill requires a faculty member from a public health program at an HBCU (such as Morgan State) to co-chair the commission. Additionally, the academic institutions represented by the co-chairs are mandated to provide the primary staff support for the commission's work.

The commission must include representatives from State academic institutions with public health expertise to serve as co-chairs. These institutions (typically USM members like UMB or UMCP) are required to provide the staff support necessary for the commission to function and conduct its assessment.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill creates a new Commission on Public Health, which falls under this category of temporary or permanent bodies established to study and make recommendations on specific government policy issues.

The bill explicitly requires the Commission on Public Health to consult with the Department of Budget and Management while performing its duties and making recommendations for reform in public health funding and organization.
The commission is mandated by the bill to consult with the Department of General Services to assist in its assessment of foundational public health capabilities and organizational reforms.

The commission is required to consult with the Maryland Department of Disabilities to ensure that recommendations for the delivery of foundational public health services account for the needs of individuals with disabilities.

The Governor is responsible for appointing several specific members to the commission, including local health officers and public health experts, and receives the commission's interim and final reports.

The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in the recruitment and vetting of the diverse set of stakeholders required to be appointed to the commission, including local health officers and members of the public with specialized expertise.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the state agencies involved in the commission and would likely provide legal guidance to the newly created commission regarding its regulatory and statutory duties.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits landlords from including lease provisions that limit or penalize tenants for summoning law enforcement or emergency services and forbids retaliatory actions, such as evictions or rent increases, for such calls. Additionally, it prohibits local jurisdictions from enacting ordinances that use emergency call thresholds to designate properties as nuisances or penalize residents and owners for seeking emergency assistance.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary and local governing bodies of counties and municipalities
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential tenants, landlords, property owners, and local government authorities
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not materially affect State or local operations or finances. Specific dollar estimates are unavailable as the impact is anticipated to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates new affirmative defenses and claims for damages that must be adjudicated in court. It also provides for specific judicial remedies, including the awarding of damages, attorney's fees, and the reinstatement of rental licenses, directly impacting court proceedings and the Judicial Branch.

The OAG's Consumer Protection Division often handles disputes related to landlord-tenant law. Additionally, as the chief legal officer for the state, the OAG provides counsel to agencies regarding the enforcement of real property laws and the impact of the bill on local jurisdictions.

As the primary agency for ensuring safe and secure housing, DHCD is impacted by policies that regulate residential leases and prevent retaliatory actions against tenants, which are intended to preserve housing stability and community vitality.

The bill specifically defines 'emergency services' to include mobile crisis teams as defined in the Health - General Article. The Department of Health oversees these behavioral health units and public health services that tenants may be summoning.
As the primary state law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police are one of the core entities whose assistance tenants are explicitly protected in summoning under this legislation.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The office advises on public safety strategies and manages funds for law enforcement and victim services. This bill impacts public safety coordination by ensuring residents can contact law enforcement without fear of housing-related penalties.

Individuals with disabilities may rely more frequently on emergency medical services or mobile crisis teams; this bill protects their right to access these services without risking their housing or facing nuisance designations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the requirement that applicants for an alcoholic beverages license in Howard County include a petition of support signed by at least three local property owners and registered voters. It also preserves the ability of the Howard County Board of License Commissioners to waive county residency requirements for certain license applicants, such as purchasers of existing businesses or those who have owned the premises for at least two years.
qa_results.responsible_party: Howard County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Applicants for alcoholic beverages licenses in Howard County and the Howard County Board of License Commissioners.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no fiscal impact on State or local government. Howard County can implement the changes to the license application process using existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the state's principal regulator for the alcohol industry. As this bill amends the 'Alcoholic Beverages' Article of the Annotated Code of Maryland, it falls under the broad regulatory subject matter overseen by this agency, even though the primary impact is on local licensing procedures in Howard County.
The Governor's Office is responsible for overseen the executive branch and has the constitutional authority to sign bills into law, as occurred with House Bill 218.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the primary legal counsel for the State of Maryland, including the interpretation and enforcement of laws regarding alcoholic beverages and local government authority.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the St. Mary’s County Board of County Commissioners to study and produce a report recommending specific areas in the county suitable for the operation of unregistered golf carts on public highways. The report must include detailed maps and consider factors such as geographic isolation, proximity to docking facilities, tourism viability, and road speed limits. The final report must be submitted to the General Assembly by December 1, 2023.
qa_results.responsible_party: St. Mary’s County Board of County Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents and visitors of St. Mary's County, and the St. Mary's County Board of County Commissioners.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no state fiscal impact. St. Mary’s County can produce and submit the required report using existing resources, and no changes to state or local revenues are expected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is relevant as the administrative support for the Governor, who signed this bill into law and oversees the executive branch's adherence to state legislative reporting requirements.

The bill requires a report concerning the operation of vehicles on public highways and potential exceptions to registration requirements, which falls under the broad policy and operational oversight of MDOT.

The MVA is responsible for vehicle registration and driver licensing. Although this bill only mandates a study, the subject matter—unregistered golf carts and driver's license requirements—directly impacts the regulations and laws administered by the MVA.

The bill's criteria for suitable golf cart areas include the presence or absence of State highways and specific speed limits (under 30 mph), both of which are under the jurisdiction and management of the SHA.

The criteria for the required report emphasize the viability of areas as destinations for the boating public and their inclusion in a network of tourist attractions, which aligns with the mission of the Office of Tourism Development.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the legal definition of a 'person in a position of authority' to include individuals aged 21 or 22 and older who work or volunteer for schools or various youth-oriented programs, such as sports, arts, tutoring, and religious activities. It prohibits these individuals from engaging in sexual acts, sexual contact, or vaginal intercourse with minors participating in those programs. Additionally, the bill specifies that these offenses are not considered lesser included crimes of other offenses unless specifically charged and allows for sentences to be served consecutively or concurrently with other crimes based on the same act.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary, Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services, and local State's Attorneys' offices
qa_results.stakeholders: Minors enrolled in schools or youth programs, and adults who work or volunteer in supervisory or instructional roles within those schools and programs
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is expected to have a minimal fiscal impact. According to the Fiscal Note, there will be a minimal increase in State general fund revenues from fines and a minimal increase in expenditures due to incarceration costs. Local revenues and expenditures are also projected to increase minimally. Misdemeanor convictions for fourth-degree sexual offenses carry a fine of up to $1,000 and/or imprisonment for up to one year, or up to three years for subsequent offenders.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the executive authority over state agencies, the Governor's Office oversees the units of government whose employees, contractors, and volunteers are newly defined as 'persons in a position of authority' subject to the bill's criminal prohibitions.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies regarding the interpretation and application of the revised criminal statutes and represents the state in appeals related to these offenses.

The department oversees programs like 4-H and other agricultural activities involving minors; its employees and volunteers in these programs will be classified as 'persons in a position of authority' under the bill.

As the central personnel agency, DBM is responsible for state employee policies; the bill expands criminal liability for state employees who interact with minors as 'persons in a position of authority.'

MSDE oversees public schools, preschools, and secondary schools, which are primary environments addressed by the bill's expanded definitions of 'persons in a position of authority' and prohibited conduct.
MDH regulates child care facilities and operates various health programs and facilities that serve or supervise minors, making its staff subject to the bill's requirements.

DHS oversees child care facilities, after-school care, and other social service programs for minors, all of which fall under the bill's expanded definition of 'programs' where authority figures are restricted.
DJS staff work directly with minors in residential and community-based settings; they are specifically included as 'persons in a position of authority' within a state government unit.
DNR manages state parks and recreational programs that serve minors; its employees and volunteers in these settings are impacted by the new 'position of authority' definitions.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DPSCS is responsible for the incarceration of individuals convicted under the bill's expanded criminal provisions, leading to a potential increase in inmate populations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Service and Civic Innovation
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DSCI manages volunteer and service programs (e.g., Service Year Option) that may place participants in positions of authority over minors in schools or community programs.
The Guard operates youth-focused programs such as the Freestate ChalleNGe Academy, where staff and volunteers will be considered 'persons in a position of authority' under the bill.
As a state agency providing education to minors, its faculty, staff, and volunteers are directly affected by the bill's expanded protections and definitions for school environments.

The Judicial Branch is responsible for adjudicating the new criminal cases, managing the sentencing changes, and implementing the procedural requirements for subsequent offenders established by the bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: GOCPP coordinates state public safety strategies and advises the Governor on criminal justice policy, including the sexual offense statutes modified by this bill.
MCSS provides a coordinated approach to school safety and training; the bill's focus on school-based authority figures and minor safety is central to its mission.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The OPD is responsible for defending indigent individuals charged with fourth-degree sexual offenses under the bill's expanded definitions, leading to a direct increase in caseload.
As the primary state law enforcement agency, MSP is responsible for investigating and enforcing the criminal laws regarding sexual offenses against minors.
The university hosts various instructional, sports, and enrichment programs for minors; staff and volunteers in these programs fall under the bill's expanded definition of authority figures.
USM institutions frequently operate summer camps, clinics, and outreach programs involving minors, making their employees and volunteers subject to the bill's criminal provisions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Commissioner of Labor and Industry to share the contact information (name, home address, and telephone number) of minors at least 16 years old who have work permits with registered employers, apprenticeship sponsors, educational institutions, and local workforce development boards, provided the minor or their parent or guardian explicitly opts in to allow the information sharing.
qa_results.stakeholders: Minors at least 16 years old with work permits, their parents or guardians, employers, apprenticeship sponsors, educational programs and institutions, and local workforce development boards.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill has no state or local fiscal impact. The Maryland Department of Labor can release the required information and adopt necessary regulations using existing budgeted resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates the Commissioner of Labor and Industry (within the Maryland Department of Labor) to manage the release of minor work permit data, handle registrations of organizations seeking the data, and adopt necessary regulations to implement the opt-in process.

The bill explicitly includes local workforce development boards as authorized recipients of minor work permit data to facilitate information sharing with employers and apprenticeship sponsors. The GWDB is the primary body coordinating these boards and state workforce strategy.

The bill identifies educational programs and institutions as recipients of work permit data and specifically references county school systems in the context of work-study and student-learner programs, which fall under MSDE's oversight.

Postsecondary educational programs and institutions, which are overseen and regulated by the Maryland Higher Education Commission, are designated in the bill as entities eligible to register for and receive the work permit information of minors.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Service and Civic Innovation
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This department focuses on workforce pathways and service opportunities for recent high school graduates; access to work permit data for 16- and 17-year-olds aligns with their mission of developing the state's talent pipeline.

As the legal counsel for the Department of Labor and MSDE, the OAG would be involved in drafting and reviewing regulations regarding the privacy and legal implications of releasing the personal information of minors to third parties.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Clean Trucks Act of 2023 requires the Maryland Department of the Environment to adopt regulations by December 1, 2023, establishing sales requirements for new zero-emission medium- and heavy-duty vehicles, incorporating California's Advanced Clean Trucks standards beginning with model year 2027. It also requires a comprehensive needs assessment and deployment plan, and increases mandated funding for the Medium-Duty and Heavy-Duty Zero-Emission Vehicle Grant Program to $10,000,000 annually for fiscal years 2024 through 2027.
qa_results.stakeholders: Commercial and industrial vehicle owners and operators, truck manufacturers, electric utilities, and residents of overburdened or underserved communities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill mandates an annual appropriation of at least $10,000,000 from the Strategic Energy Investment Fund for fiscal years 2025 through 2027 for zero-emission vehicle grants. In FY 2024, general fund expenditures increase by $474,200 for administrative and contractual costs at the Department of the Environment, while special fund expenditures increase by $5,250,000 for grant funding. Local governments and State agencies may face increased vehicle procurement costs but decreased long-term operating expenses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE) is the primary agency responsible for implementing the Clean Trucks Act of 2023. It is tasked with adopting regulations for zero-emission medium- and heavy-duty vehicles, incorporating California's standards, and lead-authoring the required needs assessment and deployment plan.

The Maryland Energy Administration (MEA) is mandated to administer the Medium-Duty and Heavy-Duty Zero-Emission Vehicle Grant Program, oversee the Strategic Energy Investment Fund (SEIF) appropriations adjusted by the bill, and serve as a primary consultant for the state's needs assessment and deployment plan.

The Department of Transportation (MDOT) is a key consultant for the needs assessment and deployment plan. It is directly impacted by the bill's requirements to assess the transition of the state's vehicle fleet and the infrastructure needed for zero-emission transit and trucking.

The Department of General Services (DGS) manages the state's vehicle fleet and real estate. It is named as a required consultant for the needs assessment, specifically regarding the timeline and feasibility of transitioning the state fleet and installing charging infrastructure at state facilities.

The Public Service Commission (PSC) is a named consultant required to assess the additional electrical capacity, transmission, and distribution demands resulting from the widespread adoption of zero-emission medium- and heavy-duty vehicles on the state's electric grid.

The Department of Budget and Management (DBM) is responsible for state budget development and personnel. It is relevant due to the bill's mandated $10 million annual appropriation from the Strategic Energy Investment Fund (SEIF) and the fiscal impact on state personnel and fleet budgets.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and is responsible for proposing the annual state budget, which must include the specific mandatory appropriations for zero-emission vehicle grants required by the bill.

The Office of the Attorney General (OAG) provides legal counsel to MDE and other agencies in developing the required regulations and ensuring they align with both state law and federal Clean Air Act requirements.

The Board of Public Works (BPW) approves significant state procurements and contracts. It will be relevant as state agencies transition their medium- and heavy-duty vehicle fleets to zero-emission models through new state-wide contracts.

The Comptroller is a member of the Board of Public Works and regulates the motor fuel industry. The transition away from traditional fuels to electricity and hydrogen for heavy vehicles will impact state revenues and regulatory frameworks under the Comptroller's purview.

The State Treasurer's Office is a member of the Board of Public Works and manages state funds. The agency is relevant to the oversight of expenditures from the Strategic Energy Investment Fund and potential bond-funded infrastructure projects related to zero-emission vehicles.

The Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) oversees the public education system, which utilizes a significant fleet of heavy-duty school buses. The transition to zero-emission vehicles and the availability of grant incentives directly impact local school system logistics and funding.

The Maryland Environmental Service (MES) operates heavy-duty equipment and vehicles for state and local government clients. As a major operator of such machinery, it is directly impacted by the new regulatory standards and potential grant opportunities for equipment upgrades.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office is relevant because the bill mandates that the Maryland Energy Administration give preference for grants to entities engaged in business activities that impact public health or infrastructure in overburdened or underserved communities.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the legal definition of neglect in Child in Need of Assistance (CINA) cases to clarify that a parent's or caregiver's use of cannabis does not, by itself, constitute neglect. Cannabis use only qualifies as neglect if it results in actual harm or creates a substantial risk of harm or mental injury to the child.
qa_results.responsible_party: The Judiciary (Administrative Office of the Courts)
qa_results.stakeholders: Parents and legal guardians, children involved in CINA proceedings, and Maryland State courts.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government finances or operations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Human Services is the primary state agency responsible for child welfare and the protection of children from abuse and neglect. Since the bill narrows the definition of 'neglect' regarding cannabis use in Child in Need of Assistance (CINA) cases, it directly impacts the investigations, case management, and court petitions handled by local departments of social services.

The bill amends the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article of the Maryland Code, specifically affecting the standards used by judges and magistrates in CINA proceedings. The Administrative Office of the Courts provides operational and legal support to the judicial branch which must implement these changes.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Public Defender provides legal representation to indigent parents and children in CINA proceedings. This bill changes the legal definition of neglect, which is a core element in the defense and advocacy work performed by this agency in family and juvenile courts.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Department of Human Services, and represents the state in civil and criminal appeals. They would be responsible for interpreting and defending the new legal standard for neglect in court.

The Governor's Office for Children is focused on the well-being of Maryland's children and the coordination of support networks. This bill aligns with policy goals regarding child welfare and the prevention of unnecessary family separation or state intervention based solely on legal cannabis use.

As the independent agency overseeing the regulated cannabis industry, the Maryland Cannabis Administration has an interest in ensuring that the legal use of cannabis by adults is consistent with state laws regarding parental rights and family welfare.

The Department of Health oversees behavioral health and general health policy. The bill touches on the intersection of public health (cannabis use) and child safety, which may require coordination with the department regarding health-based standards for harm or risk.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the State Board of Chiropractic Examiners to take disciplinary actions against applicants or licensees who fail to comply with any board order. It also establishes that a board order may not be stayed pending judicial review, allows the board to appeal court decisions that reverse or modify its orders, and specifies the conditions under which a suspended or revoked license may be reinstated.
qa_results.stakeholders: Chiropractic license applicants, licensed chiropractors, and chiropractic assistants.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill's requirements can be handled with existing budgeted resources. Revenues are not affected. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Chiropractic Examiners is a regulatory board housed within the Maryland Department of Health. This bill directly expands the Board's disciplinary authority and modifies its procedures for stays of enforcement and license reinstatement.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the primary legal representative for all state agencies, including health boards. The bill's provisions regarding judicial review, the authority of the Board to appeal court decisions, and legal stays of enforcement would be executed by the OAG.

The Office of Administrative Hearings adjudicates contested cases for state agencies under the Administrative Procedure Act. Because this bill deals with disciplinary actions and stays of enforcement under that Act, OAH is relevant to the implementation of the board's disciplinary process.

The bill explicitly prohibits the staying of Board orders pending judicial review and authorizes the Board to appeal judicial decisions that reverse or modify its orders, directly affecting the workflow and procedural rules of the state's courts regarding these specific administrative appeals.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill extends the timeframe for tax sale proceedings on owner-occupied residential properties statewide. It increases the waiting period before a holder of a certificate of sale can file a foreclosure complaint from six to nine months and delays the first required notice and expense reimbursement eligibility from four to seven months after the date of sale, applying standards previously unique to Baltimore City to all Maryland counties.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Assessments and Taxation and local tax collectors
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners of owner-occupied residential properties and holders of tax sale certificates
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government finances, as indicated in the Fiscal Note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly modifies the legal timeline for filing complaints to foreclose the right of redemption in Maryland's court system, which the Administrative Office of the Courts supports operationally and technologically.

The bill addresses housing security and foreclosure prevention for owner-occupied residential properties, aligning with this agency's mission to ensure residents have access to secure and affordable housing.

As the primary social service provider, this agency serves residents in economic need; extending the timeline to redeem property in a tax sale helps prevent homelessness among the vulnerable populations they assist.
The Comptroller is the state's principal tax collector and enforces tax laws; the bill amends sections of the Tax-Property Article regarding the procedures for tax sales.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies and the General Assembly on the interpretation of the Tax-Property Article and state laws regarding residential property rights.

The bill provides additional time for homeowners to redeem properties, which significantly impacts the quality of life and housing stability for older adults, a core advocacy focus for the Department of Aging.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives related to tax relief and housing stability, as reflected in the Governor's approval of the act.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the State Tax Sale Ombudsman to conduct outreach to homeowners whose properties have been sold at tax sale to encourage enrollment in the Homeowner Protection Program. The outreach must prioritize live telephone calls or other non-mail methods and is funded by the Homeowner Protection Fund, with a legislative intent that at least 2% of the fund's balance be used for this purpose annually.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Tax Sale Ombudsman (State Department of Assessments and Taxation)
qa_results.stakeholders: Homeowners in tax sale, the State Tax Sale Ombudsman, local tax collectors, and the Comptroller of Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund expenditures from the Homeowner Protection Fund are estimated to increase by $204,200 in FY 2024, followed by annual expenditures between approximately $187,100 and $202,100 through FY 2028. These costs cover the hiring of a coordinator, a call center vendor contract, and data programming. Special fund revenues may decrease starting in FY 2025 as outreach costs are not recoverable, unlike funds used to purchase liens. Local government fiscal impact is expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates that the Comptroller provide the telephone numbers of homeowners (from tax returns) to the State Tax Sale Ombudsman or its vendor to facilitate the outreach campaign. The Comptroller is also responsible for accounting for the Homeowner Protection Fund used to finance the program.

The Governor oversees the executive branch, which includes the State Tax Sale Ombudsman (housed within SDAT). Additionally, the bill mandates that the Governor include specific appropriations for the Homeowner Protection Fund in the annual budget bill.

The State Treasurer is responsible for holding the Homeowner Protection Fund separately and investing the money of the fund. This fund is the exclusive source of funding for the outreach campaign and program activities described in the bill.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM is responsible for the oversight of budget execution for the Homeowner Protection Fund and would manage the personnel requirements for the additional staff position identified in the fiscal note to coordinate the outreach campaign.

The Board of Public Works oversees significant state contracts and expenditures. The Ombudsman's authority to contract with a private vendor for a statewide outreach campaign would likely require approval or oversight from the Board.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Ombudsman and SDAT. The agency would likely be involved in reviewing the contract with the private vendor and ensuring compliance with privacy laws (such as Tax-General Article § 13-202) when sharing data.

The bill requires the Ombudsman to collaborate with social service providers to reach vulnerable homeowners. Older adults are a key demographic impacted by tax sales, and the Department of Aging provides housing assistance and long-term care advocacy that aligns with the program's goals.

The Department of Housing and Community Development is the state's lead agency for homeownership support and community revitalization. The bill specifically mentions referring homeowners to housing counseling, a core service provided or funded by this department.

DHS is the primary social service provider for Marylanders in economic need. The bill requires the Ombudsman to collaborate with social service agencies and refer homeowners to public benefits, which falls directly under DHS's operational scope.

The outreach campaign is timed to occur before a holder of a certificate of sale can file a complaint in court to foreclose the right of redemption. The AOC provides support for these judicial proceedings and is noted as a potential source of information for the Ombudsman.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill extends the expiration date of a sales and use tax exemption from June 30, 2026, to June 30, 2036, for construction materials and warehousing equipment purchased for use in a specific target redevelopment area in Baltimore County, which includes property previously owned by the Bethlehem Steel Corporation.
qa_results.stakeholders: Businesses and developers purchasing construction materials or warehousing equipment for use in the designated target redevelopment area in Baltimore County (the former Bethlehem Steel site).
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund revenues will continue to decrease through fiscal 2036 due to the extension of the tax exemption. The fiscal note indicates that the exact revenue decrease is indeterminate as it depends on the value of eligible purchases made within the redevelopment area. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller is the primary agency responsible for tax collection and administration in Maryland. The bill specifically requires that buyers provide vendors with evidence of eligibility for the sales and use tax exemption issued by the Comptroller.

As the state's primary economic development agency, Commerce is relevant because the bill extends a tax incentive designed to stimulate the redevelopment of a major industrial site (the former Bethlehem Steel plant) into a warehousing and distribution hub.

DBM is responsible for estimating revenues and overseeing the state budget. The fiscal note indicates that this bill will result in a continued decrease in general fund revenues through FY 2036, impacting DBM's fiscal planning.

The 'target redevelopment area' is explicitly defined by its participation in the Voluntary Cleanup Program under Title 7, Subtitle 5 of the Environment Article, which is overseen by the Maryland Department of the Environment.

MEDCO provides financing and real estate development services for industrial and economic prosperity projects. This bill impacts large-scale industrial redevelopment, which is central to MEDCO's mission.

The target redevelopment area (formerly Bethlehem Steel/Sparrows Point) is a critical maritime and logistics hub adjacent to the Port of Baltimore. The expansion of warehousing equipment and construction there directly impacts waterborne commerce and port-related activities.
The Treasurer manages state funds and serves on the Board of Public Works. The bill's impact on general fund revenue totals is relevant to the office's oversight of the state's fiscal health.

The Governor's Office oversees executive policy and state revenue priorities. The extension of this tax exemption is a legislative policy choice affecting the state's annual budget proposed by the Governor.

As the highest level of administrative review for tax disputes, the Maryland Tax Court would be the body responsible for hearing appeals if there were disputes regarding the eligibility or application of the sales and use tax exemption.

MDP promotes sustainable growth and land use planning. The redevelopment of a brownfield site like Sparrows Point into a productive warehousing area aligns with state planning visions for infrastructure development and community revitalization.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the Maryland Massage Therapy Act by modifying disciplinary grounds and procedures for massage therapists and practitioners. It adds failure to comply with a Board order as a ground for discipline, authorizes the State Board of Massage Therapy Examiners to grant stays of enforcement for its orders, and removes the requirement to publish disciplinary notices in the Maryland Register within 24 hours. It also establishes a six-month grace period for individuals with expired licenses before they are subject to criminal penalties for practicing without authorization.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Massage Therapy Examiners
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed massage therapists, registered massage practitioners, and the State Board of Massage Therapy Examiners
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the State Board of Massage Therapy Examiners can implement the bill's changes using existing budgeted resources. The bill does not affect State revenues and has no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Massage Therapy Examiners is a regulatory board housed within the Department of Health. This bill directly alters the board's disciplinary procedures, licensing definitions, and enforcement mechanisms.

The bill governs disciplinary actions and contested cases for massage therapists. Such cases are typically heard by Administrative Law Judges from the Office of Administrative Hearings under the Administrative Procedure Act.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Board of Massage Therapy Examiners and represents the board in judicial reviews and legal appeals of its decisions.

The bill specifies the process for judicial review of board orders and modifies criminal penalties (misdemeanors) for practicing massage therapy without a license, which are handled by the state's judicial system.

The bill explicitly authorizes law enforcement officers to demand proof of licensure from individuals practicing massage therapy and involves the enforcement of criminal penalties for unauthorized practice.

The bill repeals a requirement for the Board to file notices of license revocation or suspension for publication in the Maryland Register, which is an official record maintained under the Secretary of State's jurisdiction.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Accessory Dwelling Unit Policy Task Force to study state and local efforts regarding the creation of accessory dwelling units (ADUs) on land zoned for single-family residential use. The task force is charged with surveying existing laws, identifying best practices for streamlining permit processes, and making policy recommendations to the Governor and General Assembly regarding the practical issues of ADU development and their impact on housing markets and neighborhood livability.
qa_results.stakeholders: Homeowners, local governments (Maryland Association of Counties and Maryland Municipal League), the Maryland Building Industry Association, the Maryland Association of Realtors, architects, interior designers, and the Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $50,000 in fiscal 2024 for the Maryland Department of Planning to hire a consultant to assist the task force with its research and recommendations. There is no impact on state revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Planning is the primary agency responsible for the bill's implementation. The Secretary of Planning is a mandatory member of the Task Force, the department is required to provide staff for the Task Force, and the fiscal note assigns a $50,000 cost to the department for hiring a consultant to support the Task Force's work.

The Secretary of Housing and Community Development is a mandatory member of the Task Force. The bill's focus on Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs) as a solution for affordable housing and community development aligns directly with the department's core mission.

The Governor is responsible for appointing eleven members of the Task Force and designating the chair. Additionally, the Task Force is required to submit two reports detailing its activities, findings, and recommendations directly to the Governor.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates the Accessory Dwelling Unit Policy Task Force, a temporary body composed of appointed public servants and private citizens tasked with conducting a study and providing policy recommendations to the executive and legislative branches.

The Office of Appointments will be responsible for recruiting, vetting, and recommending the eleven candidates for the Governor-appointed positions on the Task Force, ensuring they meet the specific professional and geographic criteria outlined in the bill.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM will oversee the $50,000 general fund expenditure identified in the fiscal note for the consultant services required by the Department of Planning to support the Task Force.

ADUs are a significant housing strategy for 'aging in place' for older adults. While the department is not a mandatory member, the Task Force includes a representative from AARP, and the policy recommendations will impact the housing options for the population the Department of Aging serves.

Accessory Dwelling Units are often utilized to provide accessible, integrated housing for individuals with disabilities. The Task Force's study of permit standardizing and building codes is relevant to the department's mission of advancing accessible housing.

The Task Force includes a mandatory member with professional experience in the planning and development of roads and highways. This expertise is relevant to assessing the impact of increased ADU density on local traffic, parking, and transportation infrastructure.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows the surviving spouse of a Correctional Officers’ Retirement System member who died in the line of duty by homicide on or after June 1, 2001, to apply for a special death benefit. The benefit includes a retroactive lump sum payment with 4% annual interest, adjusted for any previously received general death benefits. The legislation is specifically intended to provide benefits to the family of Officer Gregory Collins, who was killed in 2001 before special death benefits were established for the system in 2006.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Trustees of the State Retirement and Pension System
qa_results.stakeholders: Surviving spouses and beneficiaries of members of the Correctional Officers’ Retirement System who died in the line of duty by homicide on or after June 1, 2001.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note indicates that the bill is expected to apply to only one individual; consequently, it has no discernible effect on State pension liabilities or employer contributions. There is no impact on State revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System, which is managed by the Maryland State Retirement Agency, to administer and award special death benefits for specific individuals in the Correctional Officers' Retirement System.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill pertains to the Correctional Officers' Retirement System, whose members are employed by this department. The fiscal note specifically identifies the agency's involvement in providing information regarding the performance-of-duty deaths that prompted the legislation.
As the central personnel and fiscal agency for the state, DBM is responsible for managing state employee benefits and policies, including those related to the state retirement systems.

The OAG provides legal counsel to all state agencies, including the Maryland State Retirement Agency, and would be responsible for ensuring the legal eligibility of claimants under the retroactive provisions of this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the definition of massage therapy and extends the deadline by one year, until September 30, 2024, for individuals to qualify as registered massage practitioners. It also removes a provision in existing law that explicitly stated health insurance plans and carriers were not required to reimburse massage therapists or practitioners for their services.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Massage Therapy Examiners
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed massage therapists, registered massage practitioners, health insurance carriers (insurers, HMOs, and third-party administrators), and massage therapy clients.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the State Board of Massage Therapy Examiners can implement the bill's changes using existing budgeted resources. State revenues are not affected, and there is no fiscal impact on local governments. The impact on small businesses is estimated to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Massage Therapy Examiners is a regulatory board housed within the Department of Health. The bill directly modifies the Board's statutes, including the definition of massage therapy and the timeline for phasing out the registered massage practitioner credential.

The bill repeals a specific provision of the Health Occupations Article (Section 6-403) that explicitly stated health insurance plans and insurers were not required to reimburse massage therapists. This change directly impacts the insurance regulations and policy forms overseen by this agency.

The Office of the Attorney General provides primary legal counsel to the Department of Health and its professional licensing boards, ensuring that the changes to definitions and regulatory requirements are legally implemented and enforced.

The bill relates to the qualifications for massage therapy licensure and registration, which require applicants to graduate from programs approved or accredited by the commission and other educational bodies.

The commission is responsible for health system planning and monitoring health care financing; the repeal of reimbursement exemptions for health services provided by massage therapists relates to their interest in health care cost and accessibility.

The exchange manages the state's health insurance marketplace. Removing the statutory exclusion for massage therapy reimbursement may affect the coverage requirements and benefit structures of the health plans offered through the exchange.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill transfers the administration of the Baltimore City Young Readers Program and the Young Readers Matching Grant Program from the Governor’s Office of Crime Prevention, Youth, and Victim Services to the Maryland State Library Agency. It expands the Young Readers Program to be a statewide initiative, allows public libraries to apply for matching grants, and increases the mandated annual appropriation for the Young Readers Matching Grant Program from $250,000 to $350,000. Additionally, it requires the Maryland State Library Board to report biennially on the effectiveness of the matching grant program.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland children (especially those in low-income zip codes), public library systems, nonprofit early reader organizations, and local governments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, State general fund expenditures will increase by $100,000 annually beginning in fiscal 2025 to account for the increased mandated appropriation for the Young Readers Matching Grant Program. In fiscal 2024, existing funds for the programs (totaling approximately $746,193) are transferred to the Maryland State Library Agency. Administrative costs for a half-time staff member at the agency are estimated at $42,300 annually, which are expected to be covered by the mandated appropriation. Local library systems and counties may see increased revenues and expenditures if they apply for and receive matching grants under the program.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly transfers the Baltimore City Young Readers Program and the Young Readers Matching Grant Program to the Maryland State Library Agency. The State Librarian is tasked with administering the programs, and the Maryland State Library Board must report on their effectiveness.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill transfers administrative authority and existing funding for the Young Readers programs away from this office (formerly known as GOCPYVS) to the Maryland State Library Agency.

The bill increases a mandated annual appropriation for the Young Readers Matching Grant Program from $250,000 to $350,000 and requires the creation of a new half-time administrative position to manage the grants.

The Governor's Office for Children originally implemented the Young Readers Program. The program's mission to distribute books to children, particularly in low-income areas, aligns with the agency's focus on child well-being and reducing child poverty.
The bill requires the Governor to include specific mandated appropriations for these literacy programs in the annual state budget bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires Maryland registers of wills to accept documents filed electronically through an established system, with the exception of original wills. It prohibits registers from refusing documents based on the manner of signature and specifies that paper filings remain an acceptable option. The bill also aligns these processes with the Maryland Rules.
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals and legal professionals involved in estate administration, probate court users, and the Registers of Wills in all 24 Maryland jurisdictions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund revenues are expected to increase by a relatively minimal amount beginning in fiscal 2024 due to reduced administrative costs associated with paper filings. For illustrative purposes, the Howard County register estimates statewide savings of at least $40,000 annually. Local government operations and finances are not affected, and the electronic filing system is already included in the budget.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates the Registers of Wills to accept electronic filings (excluding original wills) and prohibits them from refusing documents based on the type of signature used, fundamentally altering their filing and administrative procedures.

The Administrative Office of the Courts supports the Maryland Judicial Branch; since Registers of Wills serve as clerks for the Orphans' Courts and the bill requires electronic filings to comply with Maryland Rules, there is a strong operational and procedural link.

The Comptroller oversees state revenue collection. The bill is expected to increase general fund revenues through administrative savings at the Register of Wills' offices, which collect inheritance taxes and probate fees.

As the principal fiscal agency, the Department of Budget and Management monitors revenue changes to the General Fund, such as the administrative cost reductions projected in the fiscal note for this bill.

The Maryland State Archives is responsible for preserving permanent judicial and state records. The transition to electronic filing for estate documents impacts the long-term preservation and accessibility protocols for these records.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Registers of Wills, regarding statutory compliance and the legal standards for electronic signatures and digital records.

The Department of Information Technology oversees state IT strategy and data governance. While the registers are building an independent system, the department's role in setting statewide standards for electronic signatures and secure digital platforms remains relevant.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Governor’s Office of Small, Minority, and Women Business Affairs (GOSBA) to include an attestation in its annual Minority Business Enterprise (MBE) report that all required State units are represented. GOSBA must conduct an audit of the Executive Branch every three years to identify which entities are required to report, and the Office of State Procurement must maintain a list of these units. The bill also updates reporting requirements for State agencies and provides for legislative oversight through the Joint Committee on Fair Practices and State Personnel Oversight.
qa_results.responsible_party: Governor’s Office of Small, Minority, and Women Business Affairs and the Office of State Procurement in the Department of General Services
qa_results.stakeholders: State Executive Branch units, certified minority business enterprises (MBEs), and prime contractors involved in State procurement.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no financial impact on State or local governments. The Governor’s Office of Small, Minority, and Women Business Affairs can fulfill the bill's requirements, including periodic audits and reporting, using existing budgeted resources.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency overseeing the Minority Business Enterprise (MBE) program, GOSBA is responsible for the annual report, including the new attestation and the triennial audit of the Executive Branch mandated by the bill.

The Office of State Procurement (OSP) within DGS is required by the bill to maintain the official list of reporting units and must be consulted by GOSBA regarding regulations for debarring non-compliant contractors.
The BPW receives the annual MBE reports, must approve the reporting forms, and is a consulting body for regulations concerning the persistent failure of prime contractors to meet MBE goals.
The OAG is consulted on the development of regulations for identifying failing contractors and handles the subsequent debarment process for those referred by GOSBA.
MDOT serves as the state's MBE certification agency mentioned in the bill's reporting requirements and is one of the primary units whose procurement data is audited and reported.
GOSBA is a coordinating office within the Executive Department. The Governor also serves as a member of the Board of Public Works which has oversight duties under the bill.
As a member of the Board of Public Works, the Comptroller reviews the annual reports and is consulted on regulations regarding the MBE program and contractor debarment.
