The Maryland Department of Health is the primary agency responsible for administering these programs and is identified in the fiscal note as the entity likely to receive and manage these funds.

The revenue for the new account is derived from litigation, settlements, and enforcement actions. The Office of the Attorney General handles these legal actions on behalf of the state, such as the JUUL Labs settlement mentioned in the fiscal note.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM oversees budget execution and will be responsible for the creation and management of the new account within the Cigarette Restitution Fund and its inclusion in the state budget.

The Comptroller acts as the state's chief financial officer and principal tax collector. The agency will be involved in the accounting, collection, and distribution of revenues related to tobacco and electronic smoking device settlements and enforcement.

The bill requires the Governor to develop vision and mission statements, key performance indicators, and provide annual reports on the outcomes of the funds expended from the Cigarette Restitution Fund.

The Treasurer's Office is responsible for the management, protection, and investment of state funds, including the Cigarette Restitution Fund and its new separate account for electronic smoking device settlement proceeds.

The funds in the new account are designated for programs aimed at reducing tobacco use among youth, which specifically includes school education campaigns mentioned in the bill's statutory references.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of Health to expand access to and provide reimbursement for wraparound, intensive in-home, and case management services for children and youth with intensive behavioral health needs. It specifically focuses on the 1915(i) Intensive Behavioral Health Services model and requires the Behavioral Health Administration to fund 100 slots in a mental health case management program for youth who are ineligible for Medicaid and at risk of out-of-home placement. Additionally, it mandates annual appropriations for customized goods and services for participants.
qa_results.stakeholders: Children and youth with intensive mental health needs, their families, and behavioral health service providers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by at least $3,496,500 in FY 2024, which includes $3,000,000 for a consultant and funding for three new personnel positions. The bill also establishes mandated appropriations for customized goods and services starting at $150,000 in FY 2025 and rising to $350,000 by FY 2027. There is an additional indeterminate impact on expenditures and federal fund revenues related to the reimbursement of new services and the expansion of program eligibility.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health is the primary agency responsible for implementing the bill. It must expand access to the 1915(i) Intensive Behavioral Health Services program, manage mental health case management slots, provide training for care coordinators, and submit annual reports to the General Assembly.

DBM is responsible for the state's budget and personnel management. The bill includes mandated appropriations for fiscal years 2025 through 2027 and beyond, and the fiscal note indicates a need for three new full-time positions within the Department of Health to fulfill the bill's requirements.
The bill requires the Governor to include specific mandated appropriations in the annual budget bill starting in fiscal year 2025 to fund customized goods and services for youth.

DHS manages services for children at risk of out-of-home placement. The bill specifically funds mental health slots for youth who are not Medicaid-eligible but are at risk of out-of-home placement, which aligns with DHS’s mission to support vulnerable children and prevent foster care entries.

The bill focuses on the well-being of Maryland's children and families by providing intensive mental health services and community-based support, which is the primary mission of the Governor's Office for Children.

The behavioral health services expanded by this bill (wraparound services) are intended to reduce disruptions for youth in home and school settings. These services often intersect with the needs of students in the public education system.

DJS is responsible for youth with complex emotional and behavioral needs who are involved in or at risk of entering the juvenile justice system. The evidence-based practices and wraparound services mentioned in the bill are critical tools for rehabilitating and stabilizing this population in the community.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Health. The bill requires MDH to update regulations and potentially develop new value-based purchasing contracts, which would require legal review and support.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Office of Home Energy Programs to automatically enroll households in fuel and utility assistance programs if they meet financial eligibility requirements for other social service programs like SNAP, TANF, SSI, or means-tested Veterans Affairs benefits. It also expands the income eligibility limit for the Electric Universal Service Program to 200% of the federal poverty level for all customers.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Human Services, Office of Home Energy Programs
qa_results.stakeholders: Low-income households, recipients of SNAP, TANF, SSI, or means-tested Veterans Affairs benefits, and electric customers with annual incomes at or below 200% of the federal poverty level.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund expenditures increase by at least $15,000,000 in fiscal 2024 and $30,000,000 annually thereafter due to the auto-enrollment of SNAP recipients and expanded income eligibility for the Electric Universal Service Program (EUSP). These costs reflect an estimated 32,000 additional households qualifying for benefits. No local fiscal impact is expected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DHS is the primary agency responsible for administering the social service programs mentioned in the bill, including the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP), Temporary Assistance for Needy Families (TANF), and the Office of Home Energy Programs (OHEP). The bill directly mandates changes to DHS's enrollment procedures and eligibility criteria.

The bill amends the Public Utilities Article regarding the Electric Universal Service Program (EUSP), which is a program the Commission is responsible for establishing and regulating. The bill changes the income eligibility requirements for this program.

The fiscal note indicates that the implementation of auto-enrollment will require the OHEP Data Management System to be integrated with DHS's new Eligibility and Enrollment System. DoIT oversees state IT strategy and major system infrastructure projects.

The bill specifically adds individuals receiving 'means-tested Veterans Affairs benefits' as a category for automatic enrollment in fuel and utility assistance programs, directly impacting the veterans served by this department.

The bill has a significant fiscal impact, with special fund expenditures estimated to increase by $15 million in FY 2024 and $30 million annually thereafter. DBM is responsible for state budget development and fiscal planning.

The bill relates to energy emergency crisis intervention programs for low-income households, specifically mentioning the elderly. The Department of Aging advocates for and coordinates services for older adults who are primary beneficiaries of these expanded programs.

As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the implementation of policy initiatives by DHS and the Public Service Commission, and the Governor signed this specific legislation into law.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies like DHS and the Public Service Commission regarding the interpretation and implementation of new statutory requirements and eligibility regulations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the State Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners to license animal control facilities to administer rabies vaccines and requires an expedited licensing process for out-of-state veterinary practitioners to become licensed in Maryland. It also allows registered veterinary technicians and other clinical staff at animal shelters or control facilities to administer rabies vaccines under specified training, supervision, and documentation conditions.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners and the Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Animal control facilities, animal shelters, out-of-state veterinary practitioners, and clinical staff or veterinary technicians employed by shelters.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State expenditures increase by $25,000 in fiscal 2024 for one-time programming costs to update the licensing portal. Special fund revenues may increase by an indeterminate amount from fees paid by out-of-state practitioners seeking licensing. The bill has no direct impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners, which is housed within the Department of Agriculture, is the primary entity responsible for implementing the bill's requirements, including issuing licenses to animal control facilities and establishing an expedited licensing process for out-of-state veterinary practitioners.

The Secretary of Health is specifically directed to allow registered veterinary technicians and clinical staff in animal shelters to administer rabies vaccines under certain conditions and must be consulted by the Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners during the regulatory process.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners is the specific regulatory body tasked with the direct implementation of the bill's licensing and reporting mandates.

The bill requires the Board to report on its disciplinary activities and the status of the new expedited licensing process to the Governor, who also oversees the executive branch and board appointments.
The fiscal note identifies a $25,000 expenditure for IT programming to update the licensing portal, which falls under DBM's oversight of the state budget and fiscal operations.

As the chief legal officer for state boards and commissions, the OAG will provide legal counsel to the Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners regarding the new licensing protocols and regulations established by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes prompt payment requirements for State grants issued to nonprofit organizations. It requires State agencies to pay grant invoices within 37 days of receipt or the due date, establishing a 9% annual interest rate for late payments and specific procedures for invoice processing and dispute notification.
qa_results.responsible_party: State grant-making entities and the Comptroller of Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: Nonprofit organizations (501(c)) receiving State grants and Maryland State grant-making agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general and special fund expenditures are estimated to increase by up to $8,100 in fiscal 2023 and up to $97,100 annually thereafter to cover interest on late payments to grantees. Interest must be paid from agency operating budgets rather than grant funds. There is no local fiscal impact as the bill applies only to State grants.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: Identified in the fiscal note as a grant-making entity (Maryland Military Department) that may be impacted by the prompt payment requirements and potential interest liabilities for late payments to nonprofit grantees.

The bill explicitly excludes grants funded from general obligation bond proceeds or general fund capital appropriations to the Board of Public Works, requiring the agency to distinguish between covered and non-covered grant instruments.
The Comptroller is a primary implementing agency, responsible for processing and authorizing payments within 5 business days after receiving a proper invoice from a grant-making entity.

As a state agency that administers federal and state programs through local entities, it is subject to the prompt payment policies and interest penalties for grants awarded to nonprofit organizations.
As a grant-making entity, the agency must comply with the new 37-day payment cycle for nonprofit grants and may face interest penalties paid from its operating budget for non-compliance.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM oversees the operating budgets from which interest penalties for late grant payments must be paid; interest cannot be paid from the grant funds themselves.
As a primary economic development agency that provides financial assistance and grants to nonprofits, it is directly subject to the invoicing and prompt payment requirements of the bill.
Identified as a state agency impacted by the requirements to process grant invoices for nonprofit service providers within the established 37-day timeframe.
The agency manages significant state and federal funding and grants to nonprofit organizations; it is subject to the administrative procedures and interest penalties established by the bill.
Identified as a grant-making entity that must ensure on-time payments to nonprofit organizations to avoid interest penalties and manage new invoicing procedures.

A primary implementing agency with a high volume of grants; the fiscal note indicates a significant administrative impact, potentially requiring additional staff to ensure compliance and avoid substantial interest penalties.
As a major grant-making entity for community development and revitalization, it is heavily impacted by the prompt payment mandates for its nonprofit partners.
The agency provides a wide range of grants to nonprofits for social services; it must comply with the 37-day policy to avoid paying interest from its operating budget.
Identified in the fiscal note as an agency that awards grants to nonprofits and is subject to the new prompt payment requirements.
The agency awards grants for workforce development and adult learning to nonprofits; it is subject to the bill's prompt payment and interest penalty provisions.

A major grant-making agency specifically cited in the fiscal note as having a significant potential workload increase, possibly requiring up to nine additional staff to meet the 37-day payment deadline.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a state agency that may issue grants to nonprofits for rehabilitation and community supervision, it must adhere to the bill's prompt payment timelines.
MDOT is a major grant-maker; the fiscal note highlights its concern regarding grants that require annual reports before payment, which the bill's strict 37-day invoicing policy may impact.
Identified as a state agency providing grants to support veterans and families; it is subject to the administrative and fiscal requirements for prompt payments.
As a coordinating board that administers over $100 million in financial aid and potential grants, it is subject to the prompt payment policies for nonprofit educational entities.
Identified as a state agency subject to the bill's requirements if it awards grants to nonprofit organizations.
As an agency that administers state and federal grants to library systems and related nonprofits, it must comply with the prompt payment and interest accrual mandates.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland State Lottery & Gaming Control Agency
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: Identified as an information source in the fiscal note; the agency is subject to the bill's prompt payment mandates for any grants awarded to nonprofits.
Identified as an agency impacted by the prompt payment requirements for grant awards given to nonprofit entities.
The Treasurer serves on the Board of Public Works (BPW) and oversees state funds; the bill excludes specific BPW grants, and the Treasurer's Office generally oversees state financial management.
As a state agency and educational institution that both issues and receives grants, it is subject to the invoicing and payment standards set for executive branch units.
OLA conducted the study on grant awards cited in the fiscal note and will be responsible for auditing state agencies' compliance with these new fiscal requirements.
As a state agency that manages significant grant funding, it is subject to the prompt payment and interest penalty provisions when dealing with nonprofit grant recipients.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a process for hospitals to identify and reimburse patients who paid more than $25 for medical services between 2017 and 2021 but would have qualified for free care at the time of service. It requires the Health Services Cost Review Commission to coordinate with various state agencies to share tax and benefit data to verify patient eligibility, while mandating that hospitals provide refunds to qualifying individuals and reimburse the state for administrative costs associated with implementing the process.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland hospital patients who paid out-of-pocket for services while eligible for free care between 2017 and 2021, Maryland hospitals, the Office of the Comptroller, and the Maryland Departments of Health, Human Services, and Education.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase in FY 2024 and 2025 for affected agencies to implement the data-matching refund process, including approximately $28,000 for the Office of the Comptroller. However, because hospitals are required to reimburse the state for all implementation costs, there is no anticipated net impact on state finances. Local education agencies may experience operational or fiscal impacts from supplying data to MSDE. Hospitals that fail to comply may be subject to fines of up to $50,000 per violation and penalties under the Maryland Consumer Protection Act.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The primary agency responsible for developing, implementing, and reporting on the hospital medical bill reimbursement process, including overseeing hospital compliance and imposing fines.

The Health Education and Advocacy Unit (HEAU) within this office is mandated to assist patients in obtaining reimbursements, and the agency enforces violations under the Maryland Consumer Protection Act.
Required to coordinate on the reimbursement process, match hospital records with tax data to verify patient income eligibility, and disclose tax information to relevant units.

Designated as a primary coordinating agency (referred to as 'the Department' in the Health - General Article) responsible for providing program enrollment data (e.g., WIC) to verify patient eligibility.
Mandated to coordinate on the reimbursement process and provide data on benefits such as SNAP and energy assistance to determine patient eligibility for hospital refunds.
Required to coordinate on the process development and provide data regarding households with children enrolled in the free and reduced-cost meal program.
Provides overall executive branch leadership; the Governor signed the bill and oversees the reporting process for executive commissions and departments.
The fiscal note indicates personnel impacts and general fund expenditure increases for several agencies under its fiscal oversight to implement the new mandate.
Responsible for the management and custody of state funds, including the reimbursements paid by hospitals to the state for implementation costs.
Likely to conduct future fiscal and performance audits regarding the multi-agency coordination and the collection of implementation costs from hospitals.
Oversees state data governance and security, which is critical for the inter-agency exchange of sensitive tax and health records required by the bill.
Would preside over any contested cases if hospitals appeal fines or administrative decisions made by the Health Services Cost Review Commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill extends the duration of the Maryland Lynching Truth and Reconciliation Commission and pushes back the deadline for its final report. The commission, which researches and holds hearings on racially motivated lynchings in Maryland, is now required to submit its final report by December 1, 2025, and its termination date is extended to June 30, 2026.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Lynching Truth and Reconciliation Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Families and communities affected by racially motivated lynchings, the general public, Bowie State University, and the Office of the Attorney General.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the commission can continue its operations using existing resources. The bill has no effect on State revenues and no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Attorney General is explicitly mentioned in the fiscal note as providing a staff member to assist the commission and is granted the power to issue subpoenas for its investigations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill directly affects the reporting deadlines and termination date for the Maryland Lynching Truth and Reconciliation Commission, which is a state body under this category.
Bowie State University, which is part of the University System of Maryland, provides the primary staffing and administrative support for the commission.
The commission is required by statute to submit its interim and final reports of findings and recommendations to the Governor.
The Maryland Commission on Civil Rights was identified as an information source and stakeholder regarding the historical and restorative justice objectives of the commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill changes the membership requirements for the Montgomery County Alcohol Beverage Services Advisory Board to allow the Director of Alcohol Beverage Services and the Director of the Montgomery County Department of Police to appoint designees to serve on the board in their place.
qa_results.responsible_party: Montgomery County Alcohol Beverage Services and Montgomery County Department of Police
qa_results.stakeholders: Montgomery County Alcohol Beverage Services Advisory Board members and Montgomery County government officials.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on State finances and is not expected to materially affect the operations or finances of Montgomery County.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the primary state official responsible for regulating the alcohol industry. As this bill amends the Alcoholic Beverages Article of the Maryland Code regarding the governance of the Montgomery County Alcohol Beverage Services—which operates as a wholesaler and retailer—it falls within the Comptroller's broad regulatory interest in the industry.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and is responsible for signing into law acts passed by the General Assembly, including those that modify the statutory membership of local advisory boards established under state law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill makes permanent an annual $250,000 distribution from Maryland's Unclaimed Property Fund to the Tax Clinics for Low-Income Marylanders Fund by repealing a previously scheduled sunset provision. The fund provides grants to the University of Maryland School of Law, the University of Baltimore School of Law, and the Maryland Volunteer Lawyers Service to support tax clinics for low-income residents.
qa_results.stakeholders: Low-income Maryland residents and legal clinics at the University of Maryland School of Law, the University of Baltimore School of Law, and the Maryland Volunteer Lawyers Service.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, general fund revenues will decrease by $250,000 annually starting in FY 2024 because that amount is permanently diverted from unclaimed property proceeds to the Tax Clinics for Low-Income Marylanders Fund. While FY 2024 sees an increase in special fund and higher education expenditures due to existing discretionary budgeting, subsequent years are expected to see a corresponding $250,000 decrease in general fund expenditures, resulting in a net neutral impact on spending.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller is the primary administrator of the Tax Clinics for Low-Income Marylanders Fund and is responsible for distributing $250,000 from unclaimed property funds to the TCLIM Fund annually.

Two of the three entities receiving mandatory grant funding from the Fund are the University of Maryland School of Law and the University of Baltimore School of Law, which are institutions within the University System of Maryland.
The bill requires the State Treasurer to hold the Tax Clinics for Low-Income Marylanders Fund separately and to invest the money of the Fund.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for budget development and supervision. The bill creates a permanent mandated distribution from unclaimed property, affecting general fund revenues and state budget execution.
The Governor oversees the executive branch agencies implementing the bill and retains the discretionary authority to include additional appropriations for the Fund in the annual budget bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the procedures for the Baltimore County School Board Nominating Commission regarding vacancies. It reduces the number of public hearings required for a single vacancy from three to one, requires all public hearings to be live-streamed and archived on the commission's website, and permits the commission to reactivate candidate applications for up to two years with the applicant's consent.
qa_results.responsible_party: Baltimore County School Board Nominating Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of Baltimore County, candidates for the Baltimore County Board of Education, and Baltimore County Public Schools staff.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note indicates there is no State impact. Baltimore County is expected to meet the bill's requirements, including video streaming and application management, using existing resources. Specific dollar estimates for expenditures were not provided.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor is the final appointing authority for members of the Baltimore County Board of Education and receives the recommendations from the Commission whose procedures are modified by this bill.

This office assists the Governor in fulfilling the responsibility to appoint individuals to state-authorized boards and manages the administrative process for vetting and recommending candidates, which is directly affected by the Commission's updated nominating procedures.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Baltimore County School Board Nominating Commission is a statutory body that functions as an advisory commission to the Governor, and this bill directly alters its operating and hearing procedures.

The bill amends the Education Article of the Maryland Code regarding the composition and selection of a local board of education, which falls under the broad oversight and leadership scope of the MSDE.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Governor and state commissions regarding statutory compliance, public meeting requirements, and the legality of administrative procedures such as application reactivation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the termination date for the Maryland School for the Blind's eligibility to receive state funding under the Public School Construction Program, making the school permanently eligible for funding beyond fiscal 2028.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland School for the Blind and Maryland local school systems
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no effect on the total funding for the Public School Construction Program, as those levels are determined annually through the capital budget process. However, the bill may result in a redistribution of available funds, as any grants awarded to the Maryland School for the Blind after fiscal 2028 would reduce the amount of funding available for projects in local school systems.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Board of Public Works is responsible for overseeing and approving significant state expenditures, including the capital projects and bond issuances used to fund the Public School Construction Program mentioned in the bill.
As a member of the Board of Public Works, the Comptroller reviews and approves significant state expenditures and contracts related to school construction projects funded by the program.
The Department of Budget and Management is responsible for developing the state's budget, including the capital budget allocations that fund the Public School Construction Program.

The Department of Disabilities coordinates services for residents with disabilities; the Maryland School for the Blind is a key educational facility for youth with visual impairments and their families.

The Maryland State Department of Education oversees the state's public education system and manages programs related to special education, which directly pertains to the mission and students of the Maryland School for the Blind.
The Governor's Office is responsible for proposing the annual state budget and overseeing executive policy regarding the state's education system and capital investments.

The Interagency Commission on School Construction is the primary agency responsible for administering the Public School Construction Program and adopting regulations for funding eligibility at the Maryland School for the Blind.
As a member of the Board of Public Works, the Treasurer assists in approving state expenditures and manages the issuance of state bonds used to finance school construction projects.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Governor of Maryland to annually proclaim March 9 as 6888th Central Postal Directory Battalion Day to recognize the service of the women in that battalion during World War II.
qa_results.stakeholders: Women who served in the 6888th Central Postal Directory Battalion and their descendants, as well as the general public of Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no fiscal impact on State or local finances, as commemorating the day does not require specific expenditures or revenue changes.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires the Governor to annually issue a proclamation declaring March 9 as 6888th Central Postal Directory Battalion Day, making the Governor's Office the primary entity responsible for the bill's implementation.
The Secretary of State is the principal custodian of Maryland's official records and the Great Seal, and is responsible for processing, sealing, and recording the Governor's official proclamations.

As the principal state agency for assisting veterans and managing memorials, this department has a strong mission-based interest in the formal recognition of a historic World War II military battalion.

The 6888th Central Postal Directory Battalion was a historic all-Black, all-female military unit; the establishment of a day in their honor directly aligns with the corporation's mission to preserve and interpret the historical experiences of African Americans in Maryland.
The Archives is responsible for preserving the state's permanent executive records, including proclamations, and maintains the Maryland Manual On-Line which tracks official commemorative days.
The Adjutant General serves as the senior military advisor to the Governor and heads the Maryland Military Department, which maintains the state's military traditions and legacy of service.

This office oversees ethnic and cultural commissions that connect residents with government resources and advice the Governor on community services, which includes public engagement for commemorative days honoring specific groups.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the threshold for recordation tax exemptions on indemnity mortgages from $3 million to $15 million. It also establishes annual reporting requirements for local tax collectors or clerks of the circuit court to document the number of exemptions granted, the amount of debt secured by those mortgages, and the resulting recordation tax revenue forgone by the county.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland counties, Baltimore City, and the Clerks of the Circuit Court
qa_results.stakeholders: Local governments, Clerks of the Circuit Court, and parties involved in indemnity mortgage transactions including borrowers, guarantors, and lenders.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund revenues will decrease starting in FY 2025 because clerks of the circuit court will collect fewer commissions on recordation taxes. Local governments will face a potentially significant reduction in recordation tax revenue; however, specific dollar estimates are unavailable because the impact depends on the number and value of future transactions exceeding the new $15 million threshold.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly identifies the Clerk of the Circuit Court (which the AOC supports) as a primary entity responsible for collecting recordation taxes in multiple counties and mandates that they submit annual reports detailing the number of exemptions granted and the revenue forgone.

As the state's chief financial officer, the Comptroller oversees the collection of state revenues; the bill's reduction in recordation tax commissions remitted to the General Fund directly impacts the state revenue streams managed by this agency.

DBM is responsible for revenue estimation and fiscal planning. The bill's impact on General Fund revenues (via decreased clerk commissions) requires DBM to account for these changes in state budget forecasting and execution.

The Maryland Tax Court serves as the highest level of administrative review for tax-related disputes; it may be required to adjudicate appeals regarding the eligibility of specific indemnity mortgage transactions for the increased exemption threshold.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Judiciary and the General Assembly, and may be called upon to interpret the application of the new exemption thresholds or advise on the legality of local implementing ordinances.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and proposes the annual budget, which is impacted by the decrease in General Fund revenues resulting from the bill's changes to recordation tax commissions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill renames the Veterans of the Afghanistan and Iraq Conflicts Scholarship to the Douglas J. J. Peters Veterans of the Afghanistan and Iraq Conflicts Scholarship.
qa_results.stakeholders: Veterans and active-duty members of the U.S. Armed Forces who served in the Afghanistan and Iraq conflicts, members of the reserve or Maryland National Guard activated for those conflicts, and their spouses, children, and stepchildren.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Higher Education Commission can implement the scholarship name change using existing budgeted resources. Although the commission estimated a $1,500 contractual cost to update its processing systems, the Department of Legislative Services indicates this can be absorbed within the current budget. The bill has no impact on state revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency responsible for administering state financial aid, the Maryland Higher Education Commission is tasked with implementing the scholarship name change and managing the renamed scholarship fund.
The bill explicitly mandates that the State Treasurer hold the Douglas J. J. Peters Veterans of the Afghanistan and Iraq Conflicts Scholarship Fund separately.
The bill explicitly mandates that the Comptroller account for the Douglas J. J. Peters Veterans of the Afghanistan and Iraq Conflicts Scholarship Fund.
The scholarship serves veterans and military families, the primary constituency of this department, which advocates for and assists veterans in securing educational benefits.
Members of the Maryland National Guard who were activated for the conflicts in Afghanistan and Iraq are eligible for this scholarship, making the program directly relevant to its members.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes additional requirements and restrictions regarding the pharmacy audit process for pharmacy benefits managers (PBMs) that provide services on behalf of carriers. It requires the Secretary of Health to adopt regulations for PBMs contracting with managed care organizations (MCOs) to ensure audit requirements are substantively similar to those for other carriers. Key provisions include limits on the number of prescriptions audited, prohibitions on recouping funds for specific package-size related errors, requirements for secure communication portals, and the authorization for PBMs to use registered auditing entities.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Secretary of Health and the Maryland Insurance Commissioner
qa_results.stakeholders: Pharmacy benefits managers, pharmacies, pharmacists, managed care organizations, and participants in the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State Medicaid expenditures and federal matching fund revenues may increase by an indeterminate amount if PBMs pass on increased administrative or contract costs to managed care organizations. Expenditures for the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program (State Plan) may also increase if PBMs incur additional costs that are passed to the State. Local governments may see an increase in health care expenditures for fully insured plans. The Maryland Insurance Administration can handle the bill's implementation with existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill impacts the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program (State Plan), which is managed by DBM. The fiscal note indicates that the new audit requirements may lead to increased administrative expenses for PBMs, which could be passed on to the State Plan, resulting in an indeterminate increase in expenditures.

The bill explicitly requires the Secretary of Health to adopt regulations for pharmacy benefits managers (PBMs) that contract with Medicaid managed care organizations (MCOs) to establish requirements for conducting audits of pharmacies or pharmacists. It also directly impacts Medicaid expenditures and revenues.

MIA is the primary regulator for PBMs and the new 'auditing entities' established by the bill. The Insurance Commissioner is authorized to register these entities, adopt regulations to carry out the provisions, and oversee the amended audit procedures in the Insurance Article.

As the chief legal officer for state agencies, the OAG will likely provide counsel to both the Maryland Insurance Administration and the Department of Health regarding the development and enforcement of the new regulations and statutory requirements for PBM audits.

OLA is responsible for fiscal compliance audits of executive branch agencies. If the bill leads to significant shifts in Medicaid or State Plan expenditures related to PBM contracts, OLA would oversee the financial accountability of these increased costs in future audits.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits health insurance carriers from requiring copayments, coinsurance, or deductibles for diagnostic and supplemental breast examinations, though an exception is provided for high-deductible health plans to maintain health savings account eligibility. Additionally, the Maryland Health Care Commission is required to study and report on the financial implications of eliminating cost-sharing for diagnostic image-guided biopsies for breast cancer.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Insurance Administration and Maryland Health Care Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents requiring breast cancer screenings and examinations, health insurance carriers, the Maryland Health Care Commission, and state and local government health plan administrators.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, state special fund revenues will see a minimal increase in FY 2024 due to $125 rate and form filing fees paid to the Maryland Insurance Administration. The Maryland Health Care Commission can perform the required study within existing budgeted resources. The impact on the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program is indeterminate, while local governments and small businesses may see a minimal increase in health insurance premiums, estimated at no more than 0.5%.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: Section 2 of the bill specifically mandates the Commission to study and report on the financial impact of eliminating health insurance cost-sharing for diagnostic image-guided biopsies for breast cancer by October 1, 2023.
As the state's insurance regulator, MIA is responsible for reviewing and approving the rate and form filings from carriers to ensure compliance with the bill's cost-sharing prohibitions.
The bill addresses public health services and health policy; furthermore, the Maryland Health Care Commission is an independent regulatory agency situated within the Department of Health.
DBM manages the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program, and the fiscal note indicates that the bill may have an indeterminate impact on the costs of the State Plan.

The bill's requirements apply to health benefit plans offered through the state's health insurance marketplace, affecting the coverage standards for individual and small group plans overseen by the Exchange.
The Maryland Health Care Commission is required to report its findings regarding the financial impact of eliminating cost-sharing for biopsies directly to the Governor.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the state agencies involved in implementing and enforcing the new insurance regulations and mandates.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the contract value threshold from $5,000 to $20,000 above which the Friendship Heights Village Council must solicit competitive bids. It also allows the council to bypass the competitive bidding process for contracts over $20,000 during defined emergencies, provided there is a majority vote.
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents and businesses within the Village of Friendship Heights special taxing district and potential contractors.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no impact on State finances. The bill is expected to have limited or no fiscal impact on the Village of Friendship Heights, but it will improve operational and administrative efficiency by allowing greater flexibility in procurement.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is responsible for overseeing the executive branch and signing state legislation into law. This bill modifies the procurement powers of a local special taxing district, the Friendship Heights Village Council, which falls under the Governor's administrative and policy coordination duties.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the General Assembly and the Governor, reviewing legislation for legal sufficiency. This bill amends local procurement laws, requiring legal oversight to ensure compliance with broader state legal standards.

The Secretary of State is the principal custodian of the state's official records, including enacted laws. This bill, as an act of the General Assembly, is part of the permanent records managed by the agency.

The bill establishes specific procedures for procurement in response to an 'emergency' to mitigate damage to public health, safety, or welfare. This relates to the department's mission to support local jurisdictions in their response to unforeseen conditions and emergencies.

As the state's central procurement authority, the Department of General Services manages the standards for competitive bidding and procurement thresholds. While the bill specifically targets a local entity, it modifies procurement practices and bidding thresholds, which are core areas of the department's expertise.

The Maryland State Archives preserves permanent legislative records and maintains the Maryland Manual On-Line, which includes the official descriptions and administrative details of local governmental bodies such as the Village of Friendship Heights.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill renames the Office of the Commissioner of Financial Regulation as the Office of Financial Regulation within the Maryland Department of Labor. It also reorganizes the office's leadership structure by creating two distinct Deputy Commissioner positions: one for Depository Activities and one for Non-Depository Activities, each with specific professional experience requirements.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor, Commissioner of Financial Regulation
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland Department of Labor (OFR) staff and State-chartered or State-licensed financial institutions, including banks, trust companies, credit unions, and non-depository entities like mortgage lenders and check cashers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, one-time costs for signage and other administrative updates, as well as the salary and benefits for the new Deputy Commissioner position, will be absorbed within the Maryland Department of Labor's existing budgeted resources. The bill is expected to have no effect on State revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Department of Labor by establishing the Office of Financial Regulation within it and reorganizing its leadership structure, specifically regarding the Commissioner and Deputy Commissioners of Financial Regulation.

The bill creates a new mandatory Deputy Commissioner position and renames an office, requiring the Department of Budget and Management to manage personnel classification and budgetary appropriations for the renamed entity and its new staff.
The bill amends the Real Property Article regarding the Foreclosed Property Registry Fund, which is used in judicial processes that the Administrative Office of the Courts supports and manages.

The Governor oversees the executive branch, including the Department of Labor, and the bill requires the Governor to include specific appropriations in the annual budget bill for the newly renamed Office of Financial Regulation.

As the chief legal officer for state agencies, the OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Labor and would be responsible for ensuring the legal transition of the office's name change and internal reorganization.

The OLA is responsible for fiscal compliance audits of executive agencies, including the Department of Labor, and will need to account for the structural and naming changes in future audits of the Office of Financial Regulation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of Health and the Prescription Drug Affordability Board to conduct a joint study of prescription drug claim payments made by managed care organizations in 2021 and 2022. The study must compare these payments to Medicaid fee-for-service reimbursement rates and recommend methodologies for determining accurate drug ingredient costs and dispensing fees to address any inconsistencies.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and the Prescription Drug Affordability Board
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland Department of Health, Prescription Drug Affordability Board, Medicaid managed care organizations, and pharmacies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the Maryland Department of Health and the Prescription Drug Affordability Board can conduct the required study and report their findings using existing budgeted resources. The bill has no impact on State revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is the primary agency responsible for the Maryland Medical Assistance Program (Medicaid) and is explicitly tasked by the bill to lead and conduct the joint study on pharmacy claims and managed care organization (MCO) reimbursement rates.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This category includes the Prescription Drug Affordability Board (PDAB), which is specifically named as the joint lead alongside the Department of Health to study pharmacy claim data, reimbursement methodologies, and total costs to the State.

As the agency responsible for long-term fiscal planning and budget development, DBM is directly interested in the study's focus on the total cost to the State and the fiscal impact of inconsistencies in Medicaid pharmacy reimbursements.

MIA regulates the insurance market, including managed care organizations (MCOs) and pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs). The bill's focus on MCO pharmacy claims and the definitions of purchasers within the Insurance Article directly pertains to MIA's regulatory oversight.

The OAG provides legal counsel to MDH and the Prescription Drug Affordability Board, likely advising on the legal and privacy implications of accessing and analyzing proprietary pharmacy claim data from private MCOs.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments (MDH and DBM) and independent boards (PDAB) involved in the study, ensuring that policy recommendations align with the administration's health and fiscal goals.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits Maryland institutions of higher education from withholding a current or former student's transcript or taking other punitive measures because the student owes a debt to the institution, ensuring students can access their academic records regardless of outstanding financial obligations.
qa_results.stakeholders: Current and former students of Maryland institutions of higher education and the higher education institutions themselves
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State and local revenues from debt collection are expected to decrease, potentially significantly, because institutions and the Central Collection Unit will lose the ability to use transcript withholding as a debt collection tool. While a specific total estimate is unavailable, the Fiscal Note indicates that Baltimore City Community College collected $3,600,000 in past-due receivables using this method between 2015 and 2019, and the University of Maryland, College Park has over $15,740,000 in student balances currently subject to transcript holds. These forgone revenues may be partially offset by increases in tuition and fees for future students.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the state's central regulatory and coordinating board for postsecondary education, this agency oversees the institutions and policies impacted by the bill's requirements regarding transcript issuance.

The University System of Maryland governs 12 public institutions that are directly prohibited from using transcript withholding as a debt collection tool, which the fiscal note indicates could lead to significant revenue decreases.
As an independent public research university, Morgan State is directly impacted by the bill's provisions and is expected to see a decrease in the collection of delinquent student debt.
As an independent public honors college, the institution is directly subject to the bill's prohibitions and is identified as an entity whose revenues may be affected.
The fiscal note identifies the Central Collection Unit (CCU), which is part of this department, as an entity that will likely experience a decrease in student debt collection revenues.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies and institutions of higher education and has interests in consumer protection regarding student debt collection.
The judicial branch was consulted for the fiscal analysis and may be responsible for adjudicating legal disputes arising from transcript access and student debt collection.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies and institutions implementing the bill and the Governor signed the legislation into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a commercial blue and flathead catfish finfish trotline license, which allows individuals to catch and sell blue and flathead catfish in Maryland's tidal waters using a finfish trotline without requiring a general tidal fish license.
qa_results.stakeholders: Commercial fishers, seafood dealers, and individuals harvesting blue or flathead catfish in Maryland tidal waters.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, special fund revenues are projected to increase by approximately $1,500 annually starting in fiscal 2023 due to the collection of the $15 license fee from an estimated 100 licensees. The Department of Natural Resources can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources, so there is no impact on State expenditures. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for managing fisheries and natural resources, DNR is responsible for establishing the licensing and regulatory scheme for catfish trotlines, collecting fees, and enforcing compliance through the Natural Resources Police.
The OAG provides legal counsel to DNR, which includes reviewing the legal sufficiency of the new licensing regulations and the statutory framework established by the bill.

DBM is the principal fiscal agency responsible for estimating state revenues and overseeing the budget; it monitors the projected increase in special fund revenues for the Fisheries Research and Development Fund.
The Department of Commerce is the state's primary economic development agency; the fiscal note indicates a meaningful impact on small businesses in the commercial fishing industry.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Agricultural & Resource-Based Industry Development Corp.
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MARBIDCO supports the profitability of Maryland's seafood industry, and the establishment of this license provides a commercial opportunity for the harvest of catfish.
As the state's chief financial officer, the Comptroller's office oversees the collection of state revenues, including the fees generated from this new commercial fishing license.
The Treasurer's Office serves as the custodian of state funds and manages the deposit of license fees into the Fisheries Research and Development Fund.
OLA performs periodic fiscal compliance audits of executive branch agencies, including DNR's collection and management of licensing fees.
The Trust supports Bay restoration efforts; the management and removal of invasive blue and flathead catfish is a significant ecological goal for the Chesapeake Bay ecosystem.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Council on the Chesapeake Bay
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Council advises on Bay restoration strategies and promotes interagency collaboration regarding the management of resources within the watershed, including invasive species.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Board of Public Works and the State Treasurer to provide public notice for the sale of State bonds through the Municipal Market Monitor (TM3) or other methods the Treasurer deems appropriate, rather than relying on previous specific service requirements.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Public Works and the State Treasurer
qa_results.stakeholders: State Treasurer, Board of Public Works, and potential bond bidders and investors
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is a potential minimal decrease in State general fund expenditures because the bill allows for the use of less costly forms of public notification for bond sales. There is no impact on State revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly names the State Treasurer and grants them the authority to determine the appropriate service or method for providing public notice of State bond sales, including the use of the Municipal Market Monitor (TM3).
The bill directly modifies the statutory requirements for the Board of Public Works regarding how it offers State bonds at public sale and provides the required 10-day notice to the public.
The Governor is a member of the Board of Public Works, which is the entity responsible for offering and noticing the public sale of State bonds under the procedures amended by this bill.

The Comptroller serves as a member of the Board of Public Works and is responsible for the state's financial oversight, including the approval and management of bond sales affected by these notification changes.

As the principal fiscal agency for the state, DBM is responsible for supervising budget execution and long-term fiscal planning, which includes the potential expenditure savings related to bond notification costs mentioned in the fiscal note.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Board of Public Works and the State Treasurer's Office, ensuring that the methods of public notification adopted under this bill comply with State Finance and Procurement laws.
The OLA conducts fiscal compliance audits of the State Treasurer's Office and the Board of Public Works, and would monitor adherence to the revised public notice requirements for bond sales.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the position of State Specialist for Value-Added Agriculture within the Maryland Department of Agriculture. The specialist serves as the primary contact for individuals and businesses interested in or currently engaging in value-added agriculture, which involves enhancing raw agricultural products to increase their worth. The specialist's duties include providing information on state and federal regulations, acting as a liaison between government partners, and, during the first year, prioritizing recommendations related to state meat processing inspections.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland farmers, agricultural producers, processors, and businesses involved in value-added agriculture, including equine activities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, state general fund expenditures increase by $81,500 in fiscal 2024, which accounts for the hiring of one administrator to serve as the State Specialist for Value-Added Agriculture. These costs are projected to increase annually to $111,800 by fiscal 2028 due to inflation and salary annualization. There is no estimated impact on state revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the State Specialist for Value-Added Agriculture as a position directly within the Department of Agriculture, and the Secretary of Agriculture is responsible for appointing the individual to this role.

The bill requires the creation of a new Position Identification Number (PIN) and includes a general fund expenditure for salary and benefits. As the principal personnel and fiscal agency, DBM oversees the implementation of new positions and the state budget.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Agricultural & Resource-Based Industry Development Corp.
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note identifies MARBIDCO as an instrumentality that assists agricultural businesses with profitability and technical resources. The new specialist's mission to support value-added agriculture and act as a liaison between partners directly aligns with and supplements MARBIDCO's mission.

The specialist is a position within a principal executive department (MDA) whose Secretary is appointed by the Governor. The office oversees the implementation of policy initiatives like the value-added agriculture support established by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates that the specialist's priority during the first year is to implement the recommendations of the 'Task Force to Study the Feasibility of Return to State Meat Process Inspection,' which falls under this category of advisory bodies.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires parking citations issued by Maryland State agencies, political subdivisions, or third-party contractors to include a checkbox for the recipient to indicate that the registration plates cited were not issued for the vehicle described. If the box is checked, the issuing entity must verify the vehicle information through the Motor Vehicle Administration and dismiss the citation if the person is not liable.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State agencies, political subdivisions, third-party parking enforcement contractors, and the Motor Vehicle Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Registered vehicle owners, local governments, Maryland State agencies that operate parking facilities, and third-party parking enforcement contractors.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State and local expenditures increase in fiscal 2024 to reprint or modify parking citation materials. Additional operational costs may be incurred by State and local agencies to handle the verification process, though many entities likely have existing appeals processes that can be modified. Revenues may be delayed due to the verification process but are not expected to be materially affected. The Motor Vehicle Administration expects no material impact as it already has an interface for vehicle verification.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note indicates that the District Court, supported by the AOC, handles parking citations in contested cases. The new verification process may lead to fewer cases being contested in court if citations are dismissed early.
As the principal fiscal agency, DBM will oversee the budget execution and potential increase in expenditures for various state agencies required to reprint or modify parking citation materials.
DGS manages and maintains many state-owned facilities and parking lots. The bill's requirements apply to any state agency authorized to regulate parking and issue citations.
MDH operates numerous healthcare facilities and hospitals throughout the state that include parking areas where citations may be issued to manage access and safety.
DNR manages over 500,000 acres of public lands and state parks. The agency through the Natural Resources Police often regulates parking and issues citations within these areas.
MDOT is the parent agency for several modal administrations (MVA, MTA, MAA, SHA) that manage parking facilities and are responsible for the verification process through the MVA.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill impacts third-party contractors authorized to conduct parking enforcement, many of whom are assumed to be small businesses. This office monitors socioeconomic procurement programs affecting such contractors.
MAA operates BWI and Martin State Airport, which include extensive parking facilities where citations are issued. The agency must update its citations and verification procedures.
MFCA manages regional food distribution facilities, including wholesale markets with parking areas. As a state agency managing real estate and traffic flow, it may issue parking citations.
MPA manages state-owned port facilities, including terminals with regulated parking areas. The agency is responsible for complying with the new citation format and verification requirements.
MSA manages sports and entertainment complexes like Camden Yards, which involve significant parking operations and enforcement actions that must comply with the bill.

MSP is a primary law enforcement agency responsible for enforcing motor vehicle laws and issuing citations. They were also identified as an information source for the fiscal note regarding citation logistics.
MTA manages transit-related parking lots for buses, light rail, and MARC trains. They will need to modify their citations and verification processes for parking violations in these lots.
MDTA maintains its own police force and manages toll facilities and related property where parking enforcement occurs. They must adapt their citations to include the required checkbox.
As a state agency with its own campus and parking enforcement authority, Morgan State must comply with the new requirements for parking citations issued on its property.

The bill explicitly requires that issuing entities verify citation discrepancies through 'the Administration' (MVA). The MVA provides the interface and data needed to verify if registration plates match the vehicle described.
The OAG provides legal counsel to all state agencies and boards. They would likely advise agencies on the proper implementation of the new verification and dismissal procedures required by the bill.
As an independent state unit with campus parking, the college must update its parking citation forms and establish the required verification process with the MVA.
SHA manages state-numbered highways and adjacent public property where parking enforcement and citation issuance may occur.
USM institutions manage extensive campus parking systems and issue citations. They must adopt the new citation format and comply with the MVA verification requirement.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the Maryland Tort Claims Act to allow the State Treasurer to provide notice of a claim denial via e-mail or regular mail, rather than requiring certified mail.
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals filing tort claims against the State of Maryland and the Maryland State Treasurer's Office.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, general fund expenditures for postage and handling will decrease by approximately $17,800 annually starting in FY 2024. This is due to the shift from certified mail to e-mail or regular mail for claim denial notifications. There is no impact on state revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly modifies the administrative procedures of the Maryland State Treasurer's Office by authorizing the Treasurer or their designee to notify claimants of a tort claim denial via email or regular mail, rather than certified mail. The Treasurer's Office is the primary agency responsible for managing claims under the Maryland Tort Claims Act (MTCA) and will realize the fiscal savings identified in the bill's fiscal note.

The Office of the Attorney General (OAG) serves as the chief legal representative for the State and is specifically required to be consulted by the Treasurer regarding the compromise and settlement of MTCA claims. As the OAG represents state agencies in civil litigation following the denial of such claims, changes to the statutory notice requirements for denials directly affect the legal procedures and timelines managed by the OAG.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires principal departments of the Executive Branch to follow specific procedures before closing or materially changing the use, purpose, or function of a State facility. These procedures include providing public notice at least 90 to 180 days in advance, holding a public hearing, and for closures, submitting a detailed report to the Governor and the General Assembly justifying the action. It also expands the definition of 'State facility' to include any location where 25 or more State employees are regularly assigned.
qa_results.responsible_party: Principal departments of the Executive Branch of State government
qa_results.stakeholders: State employees and their exclusive bargaining representatives, members of the Maryland General Assembly, and patients, inmates, or residents of State health, juvenile, or correctional facilities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill's requirements can be handled within existing budgeted resources. While State operations and finances could be affected if the new procedural requirements delay the closure or change of a facility, such impacts are expected to be minimal and absorbable. Local governments are not impacted.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires principal departments to submit a report justifying or explaining any facility closure directly to the Governor. As the head of the executive branch, the Governor oversees the departments implementing these new procedures.

The bill's definition of a 'State facility' specifically includes those providing health services. MDH operates numerous facilities that are directly subject to the new notice, hearing, and reporting requirements for closures or changes in use.

The bill's definition of a 'State facility' specifically includes those providing juvenile services. DJS operates residential and detention facilities that are directly impacted by the mandated procedures for facility changes and closures.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill's definition of a 'State facility' specifically includes those providing correctional services. DPSCS operates a large network of prisons and detention centers subject to these stringent closure and modification procedures.

DGS is the primary agency for planning, design, and management of state-owned and leased facilities. It handles real estate leasing and acquisitions, which are subject to the bill's notice and hearing requirements.

DBM is responsible for the state's personnel management and fiscal planning. The bill requires closure reports to address budgetary impacts and plans for assisting affected state employees with finding other employment.

The Board of Public Works oversees and approves significant state expenditures, including the acquisition, lease, and transfer of state property. Facility closures or changes in use typically result in property transactions requiring BPW approval.

The bill requires notice to be sent to the 'exclusive representative' (unions) of state employees. PERB is the agency responsible for administering collective bargaining and labor relations for these employees.

OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies. It will be responsible for advising executive departments on compliance with the new statutory notice, hearing, and reporting timelines established by the bill.

As a principal department with a vast network of facilities (MVA, SHA, Transit) often housing more than 25 employees, MDOT must comply with the bill's procedures when closing or changing the use of those sites.
As a principal department with numerous barracks and facilities housing 25 or more employees, the State Police are subject to the bill's requirements for facility closures and use changes.

The department oversees the Charlotte Hall Veterans Home, which provides health services. This facility falls under the specific definition of 'State facility' and the associated closure/change procedures.
As a principal department, the Department of Aging must comply with the bill's procedures if it seeks to close or change the use of a facility regularly assigned 25 or more state employees.
As a principal department, MDA must comply with the new notice, reporting, and hearing requirements for any facility changes or closures meeting the bill's criteria.
As a principal department, the Department of Commerce is subject to the bill's mandates for facility closures or changes in use for sites with 25 or more employees.
As a principal department, it is subject to the requirements for facility closures and changes, particularly if it operates centers for people with disabilities that meet the bill's criteria.
As a principal department, MSDE must follow the bill's procedures for any of its facilities that meet the definition (e.g., those with 25+ employees).
As a principal department, MDEM is subject to the new statutory requirements for closing or changing the use of its facilities.
As a principal department, MDE must comply with the bill's mandates for facility changes or closures for sites with 25 or more employees.
As a principal department, DHCD is subject to the bill's mandates for facility closures or changes in use for its regional offices or centers with 25 or more employees.
As a principal department that operates numerous local offices and service centers, DHS is significantly impacted by the bill's requirements regarding facilities with 25 or more employees.
As a principal department, DoIT is subject to the bill's mandates for facility closures or changes in use for its operational centers with 25 or more employees.
As a principal department, DNR must follow the bill's procedures for its various regional and administrative facilities that meet the criteria of having 25 or more employees.
As a principal department, MDP is subject to the requirements for facility closures and modifications. Additionally, MDP's planning visions relate to state land use and resource management.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Service and Civic Innovation
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a newly established principal department, DSCI is subject to the bill's mandates for any facility closure or change in use meeting the statutory definition.
As a principal department with significant employee headcounts at various centers, the Department of Labor must comply with the bill's hearing and notice requirements.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill removes the 10% cap on State investments in commercial paper and requires the State Treasurer to establish new investment thresholds for all authorized asset classes through regulation. It also requires the Treasurer to notify local government units of these changes to allow them to review and revise their own investment policies accordingly.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland State Treasurer, local government units, and the State of Maryland
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund revenues may increase by an indeterminate amount beginning in fiscal 2024 to the extent that higher investment returns are realized through increased investment in commercial paper. No impact on State or local expenditures is anticipated. Local government revenues may also be minimally affected by the increased flexibility in their investment policies.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Treasurer's Office is the primary agency responsible for managing the State Investment Portfolio. The bill directly alters the Treasurer's authority regarding investment thresholds, requires the Treasurer to adopt new regulations, and mandates the Treasurer notify local governments of these changes.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires the Treasurer to work with this office to develop guidelines for identifying and evaluating qualified minority business enterprises for brokerage and investment management services. The Treasurer must also submit an annual report to this office regarding MBE participation.

As the state's chief financial officer and a member of the Board of Public Works, the Comptroller has a significant interest in the management of state revenues and the fiscal health of the investment portfolio, which impacts the overall state treasury.

DBM is responsible for estimating state revenues and long-term fiscal planning. The bill's potential to increase general fund revenues through optimized investment returns directly impacts the state's budget and revenue forecasting.

The Governor oversees the executive branch, coordinates policy initiatives, and is the recipient (via the sub-office for small and minority business) of required annual reports. The Governor also signed the bill into law.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Treasurer and will be responsible for reviewing the mandated regulations for legal sufficiency.

OLA is responsible for fiscal compliance audits of executive branch agencies. The new investment policy and regulatory thresholds established under this bill will become part of the future audit scope for the Treasurer's Office.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the definitions of 'hearing aid dispensing' and 'practice audiology' to include the ordering of prescription hearing aids and the management of osseointegrated devices such as cochlear implants. It also specifies that state licensing and regulatory requirements do not apply to over-the-counter (OTC) hearing aids, aligning Maryland law with federal Food and Drug Administration regulations.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Examiners for Audiologists, Hearing Aid Dispensers, Speech-Language Pathologists, and Music Therapists
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed audiologists, hearing aid dispensers, speech-language pathologists, music therapists, and consumers of hearing aid instruments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The State Board of Examiners for Audiologists, Hearing Aid Dispensers, Speech-Language Pathologists, and Music Therapists can update regulations with existing budgeted resources. State revenues are not affected, and there is no fiscal impact on local governments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill amends the Maryland Audiology, Hearing Aid Dispensing, Speech-Language Pathology, and Music Therapy Act, which is administered by the State Board of Examiners for Audiologists, Hearing Aid Dispensers, Speech-Language Pathologists, and Music Therapists, an entity under the Maryland Department of Health. The department is responsible for updating regulations and overseeing the licensure and practice of these health professionals.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state boards and commissions, including the Board of Examiners for Audiologists, and would be responsible for advising the board on the statutory changes and ensuring that new regulations align with federal law regarding over-the-counter hearing aids.

As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the Department of Health and appoints members to the various professional licensing boards affected by this legislation. The Governor also signed this bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill alters the composition of the Kent County Board of Education to include a nonvoting student member. The bill specifies that the student member must be a resident of Kent County, a junior or senior at Kent County High School, and a member of the student government.
