It outlines the application and election process by the high school student body and defines the student member's advisory role and term of office.
qa_results.stakeholders: Students of Kent County Public Schools and the Kent County Board of Education
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, there is no impact on State or local finances because the bill codifies the Kent County Board of Education's existing practice of having a nonvoting student member.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) provides oversight for the state's public education system. This bill directly amends the Education Article regarding the composition of a local board of education and specifies the authority of the State Board (within MSDE) to remove members.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and signs bills into law. This bill was enacted as Chapter 675 and affects local board governance, which falls under the executive branch's broad oversight of education policy.

The bill provides for a de novo review by the Circuit Court for Kent County in the event that a board member is removed under the specified administrative procedures, involving the judicial branch in the resolution of board membership disputes.

The State Ethics Commission is responsible for overseeing the ethics laws of local school boards. Adding a student member to the board involves potential ethics compliance and regulatory standards governing board members.

The Secretary of State is the custodian of the state's official records, including the laws passed by the General Assembly and signed by the Governor. This bill alters the codified laws of Maryland which the SOS manages.

While the student member is elected by the student body, the bill modifies the statutory structure of an elected board where the voting members are chosen in general elections supervised by the State Board of Elections.

The Governor's Office for Children promotes the well-being and engagement of Maryland youth. Codifying student representation in educational governance aligns with the agency's mission to support child and youth interests at the state and local levels.

As Kent County is one of Maryland's 18 rural counties, the Rural Maryland Council is relevant to policy changes affecting the governance and institutional health of its local entities, including its public education system.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes Maryland counties and municipal corporations to increase the income eligibility limit for local supplements to the homeowners' property tax credit, which is currently set at a combined gross income of $60,000. This allows local governments to grant property tax credits to homeowners whose incomes exceed the previous state-defined threshold.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT) and local governing bodies of counties and municipal corporations.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland homeowners with combined gross incomes exceeding $60,000 and local government jurisdictions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no impact on State finances as the State Department of Assessments and Taxation can administer the local supplement with existing resources. Local property tax revenues are expected to decrease beginning in fiscal 2024 to the extent that local governments choose to expand eligibility for their property tax credit supplements. The specific dollar amount of the revenue decrease is indeterminate and depends on the adoption of new income limits by local jurisdictions and the number of newly eligible applicants.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The PTAAB is the independent agency responsible for hearing second-level administrative appeals regarding property tax assessments and credits. This bill allows local jurisdictions to alter income eligibility for homeowners' tax credit supplements, which directly impacts the criteria the board must use when adjudicating disputes over tax credit eligibility.

As the highest level of administrative review for tax-related disputes, the Maryland Tax Court hears appeals from the PTAAB. It is responsible for making factual determinations on real property assessments and tax credits, including the local supplements modified by this bill.

The Homeowners' Property Tax Credit Program is a critical resource for older adults living on fixed incomes. By allowing local governments to expand income limits for these credits, the bill supports the Department of Aging's mission to improve housing stability and the quality of life for Maryland's senior population.

The DHCD is tasked with ensuring residents have access to safe, affordable, and secure housing. Tax credits that reduce the financial burden on homeowners directly align with the agency's goals of community revitalization and maintaining homeownership stability.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Department of Assessments and Taxation (which administers these credits) and the Judiciary. It would be responsible for representing the state in legal challenges or interpreting the expanded local authority granted by this bill.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, including the administration of tax programs and the appointment of members to the Property Tax Assessment Appeals Boards. The Governor also signed this bill into law as part of the state's executive policy implementation.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM monitors all legislation with fiscal impacts on state and local governments. Although the bill primarily affects local revenues, DBM tracks these changes as part of the broader state fiscal and personnel landscape.

The Comptroller is the state's chief financial officer and has a general interest in tax policy and revenue collection, although the specific administration of local property tax supplements is handled by the Department of Assessments and Taxation and local jurisdictions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires individuals who are exempt from hunting license requirements to obtain a free identification number from the Department of Natural Resources (DNR) before using snares, traps, or similar devices to capture wildlife. The identification number must be stamped on the device or a metal tag affixed to it, although DNR may waive this requirement for devices used on private property owned or rented by the user. The bill also allows individuals to report unidentified traps to the DNR or the Natural Resources Police Force.
qa_results.stakeholders: Hunters and trappers exempt from licensing (such as landowners protecting property), wildlife control operators, and the Natural Resources Police Force.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local finances. The Department of Natural Resources can issue the required identification numbers using its existing online COMPASS licensing system and current budgeted resources. The application of existing misdemeanor penalties to violations of the bill is not expected to significantly impact state revenue or expenditures.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Natural Resources (DNR) is the primary agency responsible for implementing and enforcing the bill. It must issue identification numbers through its COMPASS electronic system, manage the tagging requirements, and oversee enforcement through its Natural Resources Police Force.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the chief legal officer for the State and provides counsel to the Department of Natural Resources regarding the implementation and enforcement of hunting and trapping regulations.

Violations of the bill's trapping identification requirements are categorized as misdemeanors under Title 10 of the Natural Resources Article. The Judicial Branch, supported by the Administrative Office of the Courts, would be responsible for adjudicating these cases.

The bill requires the use of the state's electronic licensing system (COMPASS) to issue identification numbers. As the agency responsible for the state's IT strategy and infrastructure, DoIT oversees the systems utilized by executive branch departments like DNR.

The bill impacts the agricultural community, as farmers and landowners who use traps to protect property or livestock from wildlife may now be required to obtain and display a DNR identification number on their devices.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, including the Department of Natural Resources, and coordinates the policy initiatives that led to the enactment of this legislation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes new procedures for local boards of elections regarding polling place changes, requiring public meetings and testimony opportunities before a change is approved. It mandates that local boards submit a comprehensive polling place plan to the State Board of Elections for approval at least six months before statewide primary elections, requiring detailed demographic and impact analysis if the number of polling places is reduced from 2018 levels. The bill also expands the prohibition against influencing a voter's decision to include voting by mail or other lawful means and shifts the 2024 primary election date to the second Tuesday in May.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Board of Elections and local boards of elections
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland voters, historically disenfranchised communities, local boards of elections, and state and local elected officials
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $156,400 in FY 2024 for the State Board of Elections to hire a program administrator and procure mapping services. Future years see varying expenditure increases between $109,400 and $164,500. Local government expenditures increase collectively by at least $37,500 in FY 2024 and in subsequent primary election years due to cost-sharing requirements for mapping services and potential additional personnel or consulting costs for polling place plans.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Elections is the primary agency responsible for implementing the bill's requirements, including the review and approval of polling place plans from local boards and managing the administrative shift of the 2024 primary election date.

The bill expands the definitions and prohibitions regarding the illegal influence of voters through force, fraud, or intimidation; the Office of the State Prosecutor is specifically tasked with investigating and prosecuting offenses related to the integrity of the electoral process.

As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversaw this policy initiative and the Governor signed the bill into law. The office also provides administrative support and policy coordination for the State Board of Elections.

The OAG serves as the primary legal representative and counsel for the State Board of Elections and will be responsible for providing legal interpretations and defending the new regulations regarding polling place plans and voter influence.
The bill creates and modifies misdemeanor penalties and civil penalties related to election law violations, which will be adjudicated within the state's judicial system managed by the AOC.

The bill explicitly requires local boards to utilize demographic reports published by the Maryland Vital Statistics Administration, which is a unit of the Department of Health, to conduct impact analyses for polling place plans.

The fiscal note identifies significant general fund expenditure increases for new personnel and mapping services at the State Board of Elections, which must be managed and approved by DBM as the state's principal fiscal agency.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The OPD would be responsible for providing legal representation to indigent individuals charged with the election-related misdemeanors or civil penalties established or expanded by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill defines misdemeanors subject to imprisonment for up to five years, potentially impacting state correctional facilities and the Division of Parole and Probation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits incarcerated individuals from violating pretrial or posttrial release conditions that forbid contacting, harassing, or abusing an alleged victim, or visiting the victim's home or place of employment, when the individual is charged with specific crimes including violence, stalking, sexual offenses against minors, or domestic abuse.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary and Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
qa_results.stakeholders: Incarcerated individuals charged with specific violent or sexual crimes and their alleged victims
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal and Policy Note, the bill is not expected to materially affect State or local finances or operations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The judicial branch is responsible for setting and enforcing the pretrial and posttrial conditions that this bill addresses. The Administrative Office of the Courts provides support for these judicial functions.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency manages incarcerated individuals and oversees pretrial and posttrial supervision (Parole and Probation). The bill specifically applies to incarcerated persons violating conditions regarding victim contact.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Public Defender represents indigent defendants who may be charged with the misdemeanor crime established or clarified by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the coordinating body for public safety and victim services, this office is relevant to policies aimed at protecting victims from harassment by incarcerated or supervised individuals.
The OAG represents the state in criminal appeals and provides legal guidance on the implementation of criminal procedure laws.
As a primary law enforcement agency, the State Police may be involved in investigating or charging individuals who violate the conditions specified in the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Behavioral Health Workforce Investment Fund to provide reimbursement for costs associated with educating, training, certifying, recruiting, placing, and retaining behavioral health professionals and paraprofessionals in Maryland. It also requires the Maryland Health Care Commission to conduct a comprehensive behavioral health workforce needs assessment by October 1, 2024.
qa_results.stakeholders: Behavioral health professionals, paraprofessionals, healthcare providers, and Maryland residents requiring mental health services.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, special fund expenditures for the Maryland Health Care Commission increase by $75,000 in FY 2024 to conduct a workforce assessment. General fund expenditures and special fund revenues are estimated to increase by at least $1,000,000 annually beginning in FY 2025 to capitalize the fund, with corresponding special fund expenditures for reimbursements. Local government revenues may increase if local health departments are eligible for reimbursement from the fund.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health is responsible for administering the newly established Behavioral Health Workforce Investment Fund and is required to submit annual reports on the fund's activities and expenditures.
The Maryland Health Care Commission is specifically mandated by the bill to lead and conduct a comprehensive behavioral health workforce needs assessment in coordination with other state agencies.
The Department of Labor is named as a coordinating partner for the behavioral health workforce needs assessment and oversees workforce development programs that intersect with the bill's objectives.

The Higher Education Commission is required to coordinate on the workforce needs assessment and may see fiscal impacts as a potential recipient of fund distributions for student training and education programs.
The bill explicitly subjects the Behavioral Health Workforce Investment Fund to audit by the Office of Legislative Audits.

DBM is responsible for state budget development and personnel oversight; the bill establishes a new special fund requiring general fund appropriations and focuses on state workforce recruitment and retention.
The Comptroller is statutorily required by the bill to account for the Behavioral Health Workforce Investment Fund.
The State Treasurer is required to hold the Behavioral Health Workforce Investment Fund separately and invest its money in accordance with state law.
As a provider of behavioral health education and training, the University System of Maryland is a potential recipient of reimbursements and special fund revenues from the new investment fund.
Morgan State University provides professional health training and is identified as a potential recipient of funds to support behavioral health workforce development.
As a state educational institution, Saint Mary's College is a potential beneficiary of the fund's resources for training and educating the behavioral health workforce.
MSDE oversees career and technical education (CTE) and coordinates with the CTE Committee, which is a named partner in the mandated workforce needs assessment.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Career and Technical Education Committee, which falls under this category, is named as a coordinating stakeholder for the behavioral health workforce needs assessment.
As the state's chief policy body for workforce strategy, the GWDB has a strategic interest in the bill's focus on expanding the pipeline for behavioral health professionals and paraprofessionals.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Health Services Cost Review Commission to ensure that its methods for determining whether hospital rates are reasonable—specifically when using standards for efficiency and effectiveness—align with the requirements of Maryland's all-payer model contract with the federal government.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland hospitals and purchasers of health care facility services
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no fiscal impact on state or local governments because it codifies existing practices currently followed by the Health Services Cost Review Commission under the All-Payer Model Contract and its successor, the Total Cost of Care Model.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The HSCRC is the primary agency responsible for the hospital rate-setting system and the implementation of the All-Payer Model Contract (and its successor, the Total Cost of Care Model) mentioned in the bill. The bill directly regulates how the Commission determines the reasonableness of hospital rates.

The HSCRC is an independent agency located within the Maryland Department of Health. Furthermore, the bill specifies that the HSCRC may assist the Secretary of Health in the implementation of federally approved model programs.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and its various departments, including the Department of Health. The Governor also signed this bill into law and is responsible for overall state policy regarding healthcare cost containment and federal contract compliance.

The OAG serves as the chief legal counsel to all state agencies, including the HSCRC and the Department of Health. They would be responsible for ensuring that the HSCRC's rate-setting investigations and standards remain legally consistent with the All-Payer Model Contract as required by the bill.

The MHCC is a sister regulatory agency within the Department of Health that works alongside the HSCRC to plan for health system needs and improve access and quality. While the bill is specific to HSCRC, changes to hospital rate-setting frameworks are relevant to the MHCC's broader health planning and accountability mission.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM monitors the execution of state programs and fiscal practices. While the fiscal note indicates no direct impact on state funds, DBM oversees the budget and personnel of the implementing agencies involved in the All-Payer Model.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the State Retirement and Pension System to pay regular interest on member contributions held in nonvested accounts for former members who rejoin the system as active members in any of the state's retirement or pension plans. It applies retroactively to accounts of individuals who are active members on the bill's effective date and have not withdrawn their previous contributions.
qa_results.stakeholders: Nonvested former members of the State Retirement and Pension System who return to service as active members and have not withdrawn their prior contributions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is estimated to increase State and local pension liabilities by approximately 514000 due to retroactive interest payments on roughly 1,600 identified accounts. This results in a minimal or negligible increase in annual pension contributions for the State and participating governmental units. There is no impact on state or local revenue.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency responsible for the operation and management of the State Retirement and Pension System (SRPS), the Maryland State Retirement Agency is the lead implementing body for this bill. It will be responsible for identifying eligible nonvested accounts, calculating the required interest, and managing the retroactive application of these payments.

As the principal personnel and fiscal agency for the state, DBM manages state employee benefits and policies. It is responsible for overseeing the fiscal impact of pension liabilities and ensuring that state personnel records align with pension system requirements.
The bill specifically amends the State Police Retirement System (Section 24-206), affecting how interest is calculated for former members of that specific system who rejoin the SRPS.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically amends the Correctional Officers’ Retirement System (Section 25-204), affecting how interest is calculated for former members of that specific system who rejoin the SRPS.
The bill specifically amends the Judges’ Retirement System (Section 27-203), affecting how interest is calculated for former members of the judiciary who rejoin the SRPS.
The bill specifically amends the Teachers’ Retirement System and the Teachers’ Pension System (Sections 22-215 and 23-213), which are overseen in coordination with educational system personnel.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the SRPS and state agencies. It would be involved in interpreting the retroactive application of the law and ensuring the statutory changes are implemented in accordance with state law.
The Comptroller serves as a key member of the Board of Public Works and as a trustee of the SRPS, overseeing the state's financial integrity and revenue flow.
The Treasurer serves as a trustee of the SRPS and is responsible for the management and protection of state funds, which include the assets within the retirement system.
The Governor oversees the executive branch and proposes the state budget, which includes state contributions to the retirement systems impacted by changes in liability.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands and clarifies immunity from criminal arrest, charge, prosecution, and sanctions for violations of pretrial release, probation, or parole for individuals experiencing a medical emergency due to alcohol or drug use, as well as those who assist them, provided the evidence was obtained solely through seeking or receiving medical assistance.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary, Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services, and State and local law enforcement agencies
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals experiencing medical emergencies from alcohol or drug use, persons assisting those individuals, and individuals on pretrial release, probation, or parole
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note indicates that the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local finances or operations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly prohibits the sanctioning of individuals for violations of conditions of pretrial release, probation, or parole if the evidence was obtained solely due to seeking or receiving medical assistance for a drug or alcohol emergency. This department oversees the Division of Parole and Probation and pretrial detention.

The judicial branch is responsible for the court proceedings where these immunities from prosecution and mitigating factors in criminal cases are applied, as well as the administration of pretrial release conditions.
As Maryland's primary law enforcement agency, the State Police are directly affected by the statutory changes to immunity from criminal arrest for specific drug and alcohol violations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Public Defender represents indigent clients in criminal and juvenile cases who will utilize these expanded immunity provisions as a critical legal defense.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office serves as the state's coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies, including policy development related to reducing drug and alcohol-related crimes and recidivism.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies on criminal law interpretation and represents the state in appeals regarding statutory immunity and criminal procedure.
The department manages juvenile probation and aftercare services; the bill's provisions regarding immunity from sanctions for supervision violations apply to the youth under their jurisdiction.
The department oversees behavioral health and drug-related public health policies which provide the clinical context for the medical emergencies and drug use addressed in the bill.
The Governor oversees the executive branch agencies implementing these changes, signs the legislation, and coordinates the state's overall public safety and health policy initiatives.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Board of Regents of the University System of Maryland to provide a grant to a student member during the first year of their two-year term. The grant is funded by restricted funds from private donors through the University System of Maryland Foundation or a constituent institution and is capped at the cost of tuition and fees incurred by the student, ensuring total financial aid does not exceed the cost of attendance.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Regents of the University System of Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: Student members of the University System of Maryland Board of Regents
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State higher education revenues and expenditures increase by an offsetting amount as private donor funds are received and distributed as grants to student board members. While specific total funding depends on donations and the students enrolled, the fiscal note indicates that per-student costs could range from approximately 10100 for in-state undergraduates up to 92500 for certain graduate programs. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly applies to the University System of Maryland (USM) Board of Regents, authorizing them to provide grants to student members and establishing the criteria for these grants and tuition waivers.

The bill explicitly states that the grants or tuition waivers provided to student regents are not to be considered gifts or compensation under the Maryland Public Ethics Law (Title 5 of the General Provisions Article), which the Commission enforces.

The Governor oversees the executive branch and is responsible for appointing members to the USM Board of Regents; the bill changes the financial benefits associated with these gubernatorial appointments.

The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in recruiting and vetting candidates for the USM Board of Regents; the availability of grants for student members is relevant to the recruitment and selection process.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Board of Regents and state agencies regarding compliance with state law, including the interpretations of 'compensation' and 'gifts' as modified by this bill.
As the central personnel agency, DBM is interested in the bill's provision that ensures student regents receiving these grants are not classified as employees of the state.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the membership of the Procurement Improvement Council to include the Comptroller and the Attorney General, allowing them to send designees to meetings. It also specifies that the Council must provide a forum for discussing strategies to increase the participation of small, minority-owned, and women-owned businesses in State procurement contracts.
qa_results.responsible_party: Chief Procurement Officer and the Procurement Improvement Council
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland State agencies, the Comptroller, the Attorney General, and small, minority-, and women-owned businesses.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill does not affect State or local finances. It has no projected impact on small businesses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly adds the Comptroller as a member of the Procurement Improvement Council and authorizes the Comptroller to send a designee to meetings.
The bill explicitly adds the Attorney General as a member of the Procurement Improvement Council and authorizes the Attorney General to send a designee to meetings.

The Chief Procurement Officer, who serves as the Chairman and principal staff for the Procurement Improvement Council, is located within the Department of General Services. The agency is also responsible for managing procurement resources like eMaryland Marketplace mentioned in the bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Special Secretary is a member of the Council, and the bill adds a specific duty for the Council to provide a forum for discussing strategies to increase participation of small, minority-, and women-owned businesses in state procurement.
The Council is tasked with advising the Board of Public Works on procurement problems and recommendations. Additionally, the Board's Procurement Advisor is a statutory member of the Council.
The State Treasurer is a statutory member of the Council, and the bill clarifies the Treasurer's authority to designate the Deputy Treasurer to attend meetings.
The Chancellor of the University System of Maryland is a statutory member of the Council.
The Secretary of Health is a statutory member of the Council.
The Secretary of Human Services is a statutory member of the Council.
The Secretary of Information Technology is a statutory member of the Council, reflecting the agency's role in IT procurement.
The Secretary of Juvenile Services is a statutory member of the Council.
The Secretary of Transportation is a statutory member of the Council, representing the state's significant transportation procurement interests.
The State Superintendent of Schools (head of MSDE) is a statutory member of the Council.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Procurement Improvement Council is a statutory advisory body within the executive branch that conducts studies and provides recommendations, fitting the description of this agency category.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and procurement policy; the Council advises the Board of Public Works (chaired by the Governor) and includes several of the Governor's cabinet secretaries.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the number of full-time assistant sheriffs for the Prince George’s County Sheriff's Office from four to five. It also transfers the responsibility for negotiating benefits (other than salary increases) for deputy sheriffs from the Deputy Sheriffs’ Association to the Fraternal Order of Police and clarifies that the Sheriff's Office is authorized to investigate domestic violence calls and is not required to transfer those investigations to another law enforcement agency.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George’s County Office of the Sheriff
qa_results.stakeholders: Prince George's County Sheriff's Office, deputy sheriffs, the Fraternal Order of Police, and Prince George's County residents.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no impact on State finances. Prince George’s County expenditures increase by approximately $331,600 annually beginning in fiscal 2024 to account for the salary and fringe benefits of the additional assistant sheriff position. County revenues are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill amends the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article of the Maryland Code, which is the primary statutory framework supported by the Administrative Office of the Courts. The legislation also affects the staffing and duties of the Prince George's County Sheriff's Office, which provides critical security and prisoner transportation services for the state's judicial system.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly references training requirements for deputy sheriffs to be set by the Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission, an entity administratively housed within this department. It also updates language to align with the Public Safety Article regarding law enforcement standards and officer rights.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As Maryland's primary coordinating body for public safety policy, this office is impacted by legislation that clarifies or expands the investigative authority of law enforcement officers, particularly concerning domestic violence calls.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the chief legal representative for the State of Maryland and provides legal counsel to the Judiciary and state agencies. It would be responsible for providing guidance on the legal implications of the changes to law enforcement duties and the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives, including those related to public safety. The Governor is also responsible for signing the legislation into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the number of mobile or stationary speed monitoring systems permitted on Maryland Route 210 in Prince George's County from three to six and extends the program's expiration date from 2023 to September 30, 2028. It also mandates that the State Highway Administration, in coordination with Prince George’s County, provide annual reports on highway safety findings and the allocation of fine revenues collected from these systems.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George's County and the Maryland State Highway Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Motorists traveling on Maryland Route 210 and residents of Prince George's County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill maintains and increases State Transportation Trust Fund revenues and expenditures through fiscal 2029 due to the program extension and addition of cameras; specifically, $140,200 was remitted to the State Highway Administration in fiscal 2022. There is no net fiscal impact on Prince George’s County as fine revenues are used to cover implementation costs, with any remaining funds remitted to the state. General fund revenues may increase from fines paid in contested District Court cases.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires the State Highway Administration to perform safety examinations of Maryland Route 210, use remitted fine revenues for safety solutions, and submit annual reports to the Governor and General Assembly.
As the parent agency of the State Highway Administration, MDOT is responsible for the state's highway network, including MD 210, and is noted in the fiscal note as an affected agency.
The bill requires that fines collected from the speed monitoring systems on MD 210 be remitted to the Comptroller for distribution to the State Highway Administration.
The fiscal note indicates that the bill will likely increase the number of contested speed camera citations in the District Court, impacting court operations and revenues.
The bill requires the State Highway Administration to report its findings, recommendations, and fund usage regarding MD 210 safety to the Governor annually.
As the primary law enforcement agency for state highways, the Maryland State Police is a stakeholder in traffic safety and speed enforcement on Maryland Route 210.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill permits the Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners to transfer Class A beer, wine, and liquor licenses to specific districts if no other similar license exists within a one-mile radius. It also authorizes the issuance of Class B-DD (Development District) licenses with off-sale privileges for certain large restaurants located outside the Capital Beltway that meet specific seating, size, and investment requirements. Additionally, the bill modifies the geographical areas and caps for B-DD licenses, specifically for the South Lake mixed-use development and areas along Central Avenue.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Restaurant owners, alcoholic beverage license applicants, and consumers in Prince George’s County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Prince George’s County revenues increase beginning in fiscal 2024 to the extent that new Class B-DD alcoholic beverages licenses are issued. The annual fee for a standard Class B-DD license is $3,025, while the fee for a Class B-DD license with off-sale privileges is $5,000. The exact revenue increase is indeterminate as it depends on the number of licenses issued. There is no impact on State finances, and local enforcement costs are expected to be covered by existing resources.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly empowers the Board of License Commissioners for Prince George's County, which is a state-authorized regulatory body, to issue and transfer specific alcoholic beverages licenses as part of their administrative duties.
The Comptroller functions as the state's principal regulator of the alcohol industry and oversees the legal and regulatory framework within which local boards of license commissioners operate.

The bill focuses on the issuance of licenses within 'Development Districts' to stimulate local economic growth and support restaurant development, aligning with the department's mandate to attract and retain businesses.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state-authorized boards and commissions, including providing interpretations of the Alcoholic Beverages Article which this bill amends.

The Governor's Office is responsible for the appointment of members to the Prince George's County Board of License Commissioners, the primary body tasked with implementing the changes described in the bill.
This office assists the Governor in recruiting and vetting candidates for the local Board of License Commissioners to ensure leadership positions are filled by qualified individuals.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill changes the title of the Chief Executive Officer of the Prince George's County public school system back to the County Superintendent and establishes the Office of Integrity and Compliance (OIC). The OIC is responsible for investigating fraud, waste, and abuse, as well as evaluating the effectiveness, productivity, and internal controls of the county's public school system. The bill outlines the appointment process for an Integrity and Compliance Officer by the County Council and establishes protections for whistleblowers.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George's County Council and the Prince George's County Board of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Prince George's County Public Schools employees, officials, vendors, and the general student and parent population of Prince George's County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State finances are not affected. Local school board expenditures in Prince George's County are estimated to increase by at least 741,041 dollars annually beginning in fiscal 2024. This figure is based on the assumption that the school system will repurpose existing internal audit staff while adding three new positions: an Integrity and Compliance Officer, a financial administrator, and a financial analyst. If the office is established without repurposing existing staff, costs could be significantly higher as the current Office of Internal Audit has a budget of approximately 2.4 million dollars.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary oversight body for Maryland public schools, MSDE is directly impacted by changes to the Education Article. The bill requires the State Superintendent to chair the search committee for the PGCPS Superintendent and mandates written approval from the State Superintendent for the final appointment.

The Governor has a direct statutory role in the selection of the PGCPS Superintendent by appointing two residents to the search committee. The Governor also oversees the executive branch and approved the enactment of this administrative policy change.

The bill explicitly requires the newly established PGCPS Integrity and Compliance Officer to report serious violations of law to the State Ethics Commission, integrating the commission into the school system's new accountability framework.

OLA performs fiscal compliance and performance audits of local school systems. The creation of a local Office of Integrity and Compliance with overlapping investigative and auditing powers creates a significant new coordination requirement for OLA's oversight work.

The OAG provides legal counsel to MSDE and other state boards on the interpretation and enforcement of the Education Article. The creation of local investigative powers, including subpoena authority, may require legal guidance and coordination from the OAG.

The OSP investigates criminal offenses related to the integrity of public institutions. The bill establishes an office to investigate fraud, waste, and abuse and requires it to report violations to law enforcement agencies, which include the OSP's jurisdiction.

The bill mandates that the PGCPS Superintendent enter into a memorandum of understanding for policy analysis and advice with several USM institutions, including the University of Maryland, College Park, UMGC, and Bowie State University.

The bill authorizes courts of competent jurisdiction to compel compliance with subpoenas and orders issued by the local Integrity and Compliance Officer, directly involving the judicial branch in the school system's new investigative process.

PERB oversees labor relations for local public school system personnel. The bill establishes whistleblower protections for school employees, and PERB would be responsible for adjudicating related claims of retaliation or unfair labor practices.

OAH adjudicates contested administrative cases, including those involving local school systems. Findings by the new Office of Integrity and Compliance regarding personnel or regulatory matters may lead to cases heard by OAH.

The IAC oversees state funding for public school capital projects. The new Office of Integrity and Compliance will audit vendor contracts and procurement, which directly supports the IAC's mission of ensuring fiscal responsibility in school facility projects.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows the holder of a Class B-Stadium license for a baseball stadium in Prince George's County to sell liquor for on-premises consumption, in addition to beer and wine. It also updates the names of stadium licenses to distinguish between baseball and football stadiums and specifies that profits from alcohol sales may accrue to the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission or its contracted concessionaires.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission, private concessionaires, and stadium patrons in Prince George's County
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no State fiscal impact. According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not materially affect Prince George's County finances or operations. While it expands the types of alcohol that can be sold under a baseball stadium license, the annual license fee remains $2,420.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the state's principal tax collector and regulates the alcohol, tobacco, and motor fuel industries. This bill alters the scope of Class B-Stadium licenses (expanding from beer and wine to include liquor) in Prince George's County, which directly impacts the agency's regulatory oversight and enforcement responsibilities regarding alcoholic beverages and state revenue collection.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the chief legal officer for the State and provides legal counsel to the Governor, the General Assembly, and state agencies. It is relevant here for its role in interpreting and providing legal guidance on changes to the Alcoholic Beverages Article of the Annotated Code of Maryland.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives across the state. The Governor is responsible for signing the bill into law and oversees the general enforcement of state laws, including those related to local commissions such as the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission mentioned in the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires Maryland courts to schedule expedited hearings for petitions regarding the proposed relocation of a child who is the subject of a custody or visitation order, specifically in cases where the relocation is 40 or more miles away or would significantly interfere with the established parenting time schedule.
qa_results.stakeholders: Parents and guardians involved in child custody or visitation proceedings and the children subject to those orders.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not expected to materially affect State or local finances or operations, although there may be minimal expenditures associated with scheduling and conducting expedited hearings in circuit courts.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates that the courts schedule hearings on an expedited basis for child custody relocation petitions, which falls under the operational and administrative support duties of the AOC for the Judicial Branch.

As the state's primary social service provider for children and families, DHS is a key stakeholder in child custody and welfare matters and was specifically consulted for the fiscal note analysis of this bill.

The bill's focus on child relocation, parenting time, and the 'Assurance of Child's Safety Act' aligns with the office's mission to coordinate statewide efforts and support networks for the well-being of Maryland's children.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the state agencies and the Judiciary; they would be responsible for advising on the implementation and legal interpretation of these changes to family law and court procedures.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The OPD provides legal representation in parental defense and juvenile proceedings; changes to custody relocation procedures and the scheduling of expedited hearings may impact their caseload and representation strategies in relevant cases.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, coordinates child-related policy initiatives, and the Governor signed this legislation into law.
As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM monitors legislation for fiscal impacts on state operations, although the fiscal note indicates this bill's impact is minimal.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes Prince George’s County to enter into payment in lieu of taxes (PILOT) agreements with owners of low-income housing developments. The authorization applies to properties financed through the county’s Right of First Refusal program or those acquired, constructed, or rehabilitated specifically to provide rental housing for lower-income individuals.
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners and developers of low-income housing in Prince George's County, lower-income residents seeking rental housing, and the Prince George's County government.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no impact on state finances. At the local level, Prince George’s County property tax revenues may decrease starting in FY 2024 due to new property tax exemptions. The exact amount of the revenue decrease is indeterminate and depends on the number of eligible properties and their assessed values; however, these losses will be partially mitigated by the negotiated payments required under the PILOT agreements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, signs legislation into law, and coordinates state-level policy initiatives regarding affordable housing and local government relations.

The agency is responsible for financing and supporting affordable rental housing and community revitalization; the bill expands the use of PILOT agreements for low-income housing projects that may be supported by state or federal programs.

The office is dedicated to reducing child poverty and youth homelessness, making the authorization of new financial tools for low-income housing in Prince George's County highly relevant to its core mission.
The team focuses on addressing child poverty and enhancing economic mobility, both of which are central goals of expanding and preserving affordable rental housing for lower-income residents.

The Maryland Tax Court serves as the highest administrative body for hearing appeals related to property tax disputes, which may include challenges to the local property tax exemptions authorized under this bill.

This board is responsible for hearing appeals regarding property tax exemptions and credits; it would adjudicate disputes concerning the eligibility of specific housing projects for the PILOT agreements authorized by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Teacher Pay Parity Act requires nonpublic schools that provide special education placements for children with disabilities to pay their teachers salaries equivalent to those of public school teachers with similar training and experience in the same county. This pay parity is phased in over three years beginning in fiscal year 2024. The additional funding required for these salary increases is shared by the State and local county governments based on a specific cost-sharing formula.
qa_results.stakeholders: Teachers at nonpublic special education schools, children with disabilities in nonpublic placements, nonpublic educational facilities, and local school systems.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures increase by 3500000 in FY 2024, rising to 11400000 by FY 2028. Local county government expenditures increase by 5000000 in FY 2024, reaching 16300000 by FY 2028. These increases represent the State and local shares of the additional funding needed to achieve teacher salary parity at nonpublic special education schools.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for K-12 oversight, MSDE is responsible for administering the nonpublic special education placement program, adopting regulations to carry out the teacher pay parity requirements, and managing the funding formulas that calculate the State and local shares of these costs.

The bill creates a mandated general fund expenditure that reaches $11.4 million annually by FY 2028. DBM is responsible for budgeting these mandated funds and oversees the fiscal personnel policies that the nonpublic salaries must now mirror.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and proposes the annual state budget. This bill establishes a significant new mandated appropriation that must be included in future executive budgets to fund teacher salary parity.

The bill specifically addresses the quality of education and support for children with disabilities. The Department of Disabilities coordinates services and policies for this population and has a direct interest in the financial stability of special education placements.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the State Department of Education and the State Board of Education regarding the implementation of the new salary parity mandate and ensuring compliance with the federal Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA).
The Comptroller's office manages the collection and distribution of state revenues and will be responsible for the actual disbursement of mandated funds to support nonpublic teacher salaries.

The Treasurer's Office is responsible for the management and distribution of state funds, including those allocated for educational programs and special education placements under the revised funding formula.
The MLDS links student and workforce data; it may be used to analyze the impact of the Teacher Pay Parity Act on teacher retention and workforce outcomes in the special education sector.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Maryland Board of Physicians to establish an emeritus status for retired physicians who have practiced in the state for at least 10 years and meet specific conduct requirements. It updates the inactive status process and exempts both statuses from continuing medical education requirements and the standard three-year license term. Licensees on inactive or emeritus status are prohibited from practicing medicine or delegating medical acts, though emeritus physicians may continue to use titles such as 'Dr.' or 'M.D.' as long as they do not represent themselves as authorized to practice.
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed physicians in Maryland, particularly those retiring or seeking inactive license status
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Maryland Board of Physicians can handle the additional workload with existing budgeted resources. Any net impact on the board's special fund revenues from emeritus or inactive status fees is expected to be minimal. There is no fiscal impact on local governments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Physicians is a regulatory body within the Maryland Department of Health. This bill directly alters the Board's licensing procedures by establishing a new emeritus status, modifying inactive status requirements, and setting associated fees and continuing education exemptions.

The OAG serves as the chief legal counsel to state agencies, including the Department of Health and its professional boards. The OAG would be involved in drafting regulations for the new license status and providing legal guidance on the enforcement of practice prohibitions for inactive or emeritus physicians.

The OAH adjudicates contested administrative cases for state agencies. If a physician is denied emeritus status or is charged with practicing medicine while on inactive or emeritus status, the resulting legal disputes would be heard by Administrative Law Judges at the OAH.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits Maryland health occupations boards from requiring applicants to provide proof of lawful presence in the United States or a Social Security number as a condition for professional licensure, certification, or registration. Instead, it allows applicants to provide an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN) or other alternative documentation. The bill also updates data collection and reporting requirements for these boards to include ITINs for child support enforcement and tax assessment purposes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and State health occupations boards
qa_results.stakeholders: Immigrants residing in Maryland seeking healthcare professional licenses, the State Board of Nursing, the Child Support Administration, and the Department of Assessments and Taxation.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund revenues for health occupations boards will increase by an indeterminate amount starting in fiscal 2024 due to potential additional license applications. Special fund expenditures for the State Board of Nursing are estimated to increase by $100,000 in fiscal 2024 for one-time programming costs. The Board of Nursing also estimates additional staffing costs of approximately $293,587 in fiscal 2024 and over $337,100 annually thereafter, though the Department of Legislative Services notes that the precise staffing need depends on actual application volume.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Maryland Department of Health (MDH) as it prohibits health occupations boards, which operate within MDH, from requiring proof of lawful presence or a Social Security number for licensure. The fiscal note highlights significant impacts on the State Board of Nursing within MDH.

The bill amends the Family Law Article regarding child support enforcement by the Child Support Administration (CSA), which is a unit of the Department of Human Services. It modifies the identification data (adding ITINs) that licensing authorities must collect and share with CSA.

The bill specifically amends Section 10-119.3(b)(2) of the Family Law Article, which details the Social Security number disclosure requirements for recreational hunting and fishing licenses issued by the Department of Natural Resources.

The bill lists 'clerks of the court' and the 'Court of Appeals' as licensing authorities subject to the identification disclosure requirements in the Family Law Article, which are overseen and supported by the Administrative Office of the Courts.

The Comptroller is designated as a 'licensing authority' under the Family Law provisions being amended and is subject to the updated requirements for recording and disclosing applicant identification numbers to the Child Support Administration.

The Department of Agriculture is listed as a 'licensing authority' whose procedures for recording applicant identification information (SSNs/ITINs) for child support enforcement are updated by this bill.
The State Department of Education is listed as a 'licensing authority' subject to the bill's updated identification disclosure requirements in the Family Law Article.

The Department of Labor is designated as a 'licensing authority' in the bill, meaning its licensing units must comply with the modified requirements for collecting and sharing Social Security numbers or ITINs.
The Department of Transportation is listed as a 'licensing authority' subject to the bill's changes regarding the disclosure of identification numbers for child support enforcement purposes.
The Department of the Environment is identified as a 'licensing authority' and is required to follow the bill's updated protocols for recording applicant identification information.

The Maryland Insurance Administration is listed as a 'licensing authority' affected by the bill's changes to the Family Law Article regarding the collection of Social Security numbers and ITINs for licensing.
The Office of the Attorney General is listed as a 'licensing authority' in the bill and provides legal counsel to the health occupations boards that must implement the new licensure criteria.
The Public Service Commission is designated as a 'licensing authority' subject to the updated applicant identification disclosure requirements for child support enforcement.
The Secretary of State is identified as a 'licensing authority' whose identification number collection procedures are modified by the bill's amendments to the Family Law Article.

The bill facilitates the entry of immigrants into the health workforce by removing lawful presence and Social Security number barriers, which aligns with the board's mission to develop innovative workforce solutions and talent pipelines.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies and makes permanent the financial assistance provided to Ocean Downs Race Course for operating losses from the Purse Dedication Account. It replaces a fixed annual limit of $1,200,000 with a formula of $30,000 per live racing day, provided the racecourse maintains a minimum of 40 live racing days per year.
qa_results.stakeholders: Ocean Downs Race Course, standardbred racing industry participants, and the Maryland State Racing Commission.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on overall State revenue or total special fund expenditures because it only redirects funds within the Purse Dedication Account (PDA). According to the fiscal note, the annual grant to Ocean Downs is expected to increase to approximately $1,410,000 (based on 47 racing days). This increase reduces the PDA funds available for standardbred purses by approximately $210,000 in 2023 and 2024, and by the full $1,410,000 annually thereafter as the original subsidy was set to expire.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Racing Commission, which is responsible for the authority and administration of the Purse Dedication Account described in the bill, is an entity within the Department of Labor.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland State Lottery & Gaming Control Agency
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency regulates the video lottery terminals (VLTs) that generate the revenue distributed into the Purse Dedication Account, which this bill alters.
As the chief financial officer, the Comptroller is responsible for the collection and distribution of state revenues, including the proceeds from gaming that fund the Purse Dedication Account.
The Governor oversees the executive branch agencies responsible for implementing the bill's provisions and signed the legislation into law.
The department supervises budget execution and fiscal planning, including the oversight of special fund accounts like the Purse Dedication Account.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the State Board of Pharmacy to adopt regulations ensuring that blind, visually impaired, or print disabled individuals have access to accessible prescription labels, bag tags, and medical guides at no extra cost. It also mandates that the Maryland Department of Health submit a report on the potential costs and processes for implementing enhanced dispensing fees for these accessible materials.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Pharmacy and Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Blind, visually impaired, and print disabled individuals; Maryland pharmacists and pharmacies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the State Board of Pharmacy and Maryland Department of Health can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. State revenues are not affected. Small business pharmacies may incur additional expenses to provide accessible materials, though the exact impact depends on the regulations adopted by the Board.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates the State Board of Pharmacy, which is overseen by the Department of Health, to adopt new regulations. Furthermore, Section 3 of the bill explicitly requires the Maryland Department of Health to submit a report to the General Assembly regarding the costs and process for implementing enhanced professional dispensing fees for accessible packaging.

The bill focuses on improving accessibility and service delivery for Marylanders with visual impairments and print disabilities. This aligns with the department's mission to coordinate services and ensure programs are inclusive and provided in integrated settings.

The Maryland State Library Agency directly oversees the Maryland State Library for the Blind and Print Disabled (LBPD), which serves the specific population addressed by the bill and manages resources related to accessible formats.

A significant portion of the population that is blind or visually impaired consists of older adults. The bill's requirements for accessible prescription labels directly impact the quality of life and healthcare autonomy of Maryland's aging population.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as legal counsel to the Department of Health and the State Board of Pharmacy and will be responsible for reviewing the legality of the new regulations mandated by the bill.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies (MDH and the Board of Pharmacy) responsible for implementing the bill and coordinates the policy initiatives that led to this legislation.

As the independent agency that hears contested cases for the Department of Health, the OAH would be responsible for adjudicating any regulatory enforcement disputes or appeals involving pharmacies failing to comply with the new label requirements.

The Commission is responsible for improving access to health care and promoting informed decision-making. The bill's requirement for accessible prescription information is a direct effort to improve health literacy and access for a specific patient population.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Interagency Commission on School Construction as an independent unit of State government and increases the state's annual funding commitment to the Prince George’s County public-private partnership for school construction from $25 million to $27 million starting in fiscal 2025. The bill also raises the cost thresholds for school remodeling projects and change orders that require approval from the State Superintendent of Schools and repeals the School Safety Grant and Aging Schools programs effective June 30, 2026, redirecting those funds to high-priority construction projects. Additionally, it mandates that local school boards perform life-cycle cost evaluations for major school construction or renovation projects.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland local school systems, the Prince George's County Board of Education, the Maryland State Department of Education, and public school students.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, State general fund expenditures increase by $123,300 in FY 2024 for a new human resources position, and by approximately $2.1 million annually starting in FY 2025 to cover staffing and a $2 million backfill of Education Trust Fund revenues redirected to Prince George's County. Local revenues for the Prince George's County Board of Education will increase by $2 million annually beginning in FY 2025 for school construction. The repeal of the School Safety Grant and Aging Schools programs in FY 2027 is expected to be revenue-neutral, as approximately $16.1 million will be redirected to the Nancy K. Kopp Public School Facilities Priority Fund.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill primarily concerns the Interagency Commission on School Construction (IAC), establishing it as an independent unit of State government, altering its membership removal process, and modifying its responsibilities regarding program administration, reporting, and life-cycle cost evaluations.

The bill alters the school construction approval authorities of the State Superintendent of Schools, moves the IAC out from being a unit within the Department, and requires coordination on life-cycle cost analysis standards.

The bill requires the Maryland Stadium Authority (MSA) to manage and deposit significant funds into the Prince George’s County Public–Private Partnership Fund and requires MSA to review P3 agreements and report on construction progress.

The Secretary of General Services is a member of the IAC. Additionally, the bill mandates that DGS develop life-cycle cost analysis standards in coordination with the IAC and the Department of Education.

The bill repeals the requirement for the Board of Public Works to approve certain grants awarded under school construction programs, reducing its direct oversight role in those specific funding allocations.

The Governor appoints and may remove certain members of the IAC. The bill also authorizes the Governor to process budget amendments related to the Supplemental Public School Construction Financing Fund.
The Secretary of Planning (or designee) is a statutory member of the Interagency Commission on School Construction, which is restructured and given new mandates by this bill.

The State Treasurer is a member of the Board of Public Works and is responsible for holding, investing, and managing the special funds mentioned in the bill, such as the Prince George's County Public-Private Partnership Fund.

The Comptroller is a member of the Board of Public Works and is responsible for accounting for the Prince George's County Public-Private Partnership Fund and depositing funds from the Education Trust Fund into the Financing Fund.
As the state's fiscal and personnel agency, DBM is impacted by the IAC's transition to an independent unit and the associated staffing requirements and budget amendments authorized by the bill.
The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in recruiting and vetting the public members of the IAC, for whom the bill establishes new removal protocols.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the state agencies involved in the restructuring and the development of new life-cycle cost standards and P3 agreement reviews.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill extends the termination date for the State Board of Architects within the Maryland Department of Labor, allowing the board to continue its operations until July 1, 2028, in accordance with the Maryland Program Evaluation Act.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Architects, Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals seeking architectural licensure, businesses practicing architecture, and the Maryland Department of Labor.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Special fund revenues and expenditures for the State Board of Architects are maintained through fiscal 2028. There is no impact on local government or small businesses. Specific dollar estimates for the maintained revenues and expenditures were not provided in the fiscal note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Architects is a regulatory body located within the Maryland Department of Labor. The bill extends the board's termination date, directly enabling the department to continue its mission of licensing and regulating the architectural profession.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Architects is a permanent regulatory body that falls under this collective entity of executive-created boards and commissions. The bill specifically extends the statutory and regulatory authority of this board.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and signed HB 463 into law. The Governor is responsible for the overall administration of the agencies and boards that regulate professional services in Maryland.

The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in filling vacancies on state boards. Extending the termination date of the State Board of Architects ensures that the office will continue to recruit and vet members for this board until 2028.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees budget execution. The bill maintains the board's special fund revenues and expenditures in the state budget for an additional five years, impacting long-term fiscal planning.

The bill is part of the Maryland Program Evaluation Act process, which requires evaluations of state boards. OLA is the agency responsible for conducting fiscal and performance audits that ensure these boards are operating efficiently and within the law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows individuals under the age of 16 to catch crabs for recreational purposes in the Chesapeake Bay and its tidal tributaries using specific gear, such as collapsible traps, net rings, handlines, or dip nets, without the requirement of obtaining a recreational crabbing license.
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals under the age of 16 engaged in recreational crabbing and the Maryland Department of Natural Resources.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is expected to have a negligible impact on State finances. While there may be a slight decrease in license fee revenue from individuals under 16 who previously purchased licenses, this loss is considered immaterial. There is no fiscal impact on local governments, and the effect on small businesses is minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Natural Resources is the primary implementing agency, as it manages Maryland's fisheries and is responsible for adopting the regulations that will define the specific gear allowed for youth recreational crabbing.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Natural Resources and will be responsible for reviewing the new crabbing regulations for legal sufficiency.
The Comptroller is the state's chief tax collector and revenue manager, responsible for overseeing the accounts into which recreational crabbing license fees are deposited.

The Department of Budget and Management oversees state revenue estimation and budget execution, including monitoring any fiscal impacts on the state's special funds from changes in license fee requirements.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, including the Department of Natural Resources, and coordinates the implementation of policy initiatives related to natural resource management.

The Judicial Branch, supported by the Administrative Office of the Courts, adjudicates citations and legal disputes related to natural resources violations, including potential enforcement issues involving recreational crabbing gear.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows the Department of Natural Resources to authorize Sunday hunting for game birds and mammals in Worcester County on both private property and designated public land, typically restricted to the hours between 30 minutes before sunrise and 10:30 a.m. except during certain deer hunting seasons. Additionally, the bill reduces the required safety zone for archery hunting in Worcester County from 150 yards to 50 yards from occupied buildings.
qa_results.stakeholders: Hunters in Worcester County, private landowners, agricultural businesses, and local retail and hospitality businesses catering to hunters.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not expected to materially affect State or local operations or finances. While specific dollar estimates for increased revenue or expenditures are unavailable, the bill may have a minor positive economic impact on small businesses in Worcester County through increased hunter spending and potential reductions in agricultural crop damage.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary implementing agency, the Department of Natural Resources is authorized by the bill to allow Sunday hunting in Worcester County and to manage the designation of public lands for such activities. The department is also responsible for regulating hunting safety zones.
The fiscal note indicates that the bill may affect businesses such as farms by potentially reducing deer populations and subsequent crop damage in Worcester County.

The Council is responsible for advocating for rural interests and addressing challenges in Maryland's rural counties, such as Worcester County. The expansion of hunting and its economic impact on local rural businesses falls within its scope of interest.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and policy coordination for all state agencies, including the Department of Natural Resources, which will implement the bill's provisions.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Natural Resources regarding the implementation and enforcement of hunting regulations and statutory changes.
The Judiciary may be involved in the adjudication of violations related to the new hunting time restrictions or safety zone regulations established by the bill.

While the Natural Resources Police (within DNR) are the primary enforcers, the Maryland State Police provides support to allied law enforcement agencies and enforces state laws, which may include safety regulations related to the discharge of firearms.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits any person from setting on fire or burning oriented strand board (OSB) for the purpose of fire-fighting training. It establishes administrative penalties of up to $500 for a first violation and civil penalties of up to $1,000 for subsequent violations.
qa_results.stakeholders: Fire departments, fire fighters, fire marshals, local environmental health agencies, and manufacturers or sellers of oriented strand board.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, State general fund expenditures increase by $57,100 in FY 2024 to hire one contractual environmental compliance specialist within the Maryland Department of the Environment for outreach, investigation, and enforcement. This cost is expected to continue through FY 2026. Local governments may experience increased costs to purchase alternative training materials; for instance, Prince George's County estimates an annual increase of $15,000. Potential minimal State and local costs may also arise from disposing of noncompliant materials. Penalty revenues are not expected to have a significant impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill adds a new section to the Environment Article and identifies the Department of the Environment (MDE) as the primary agency for enforcement, outreach, and investigations, necessitating a new contractual environmental compliance specialist position.
