As the state's primary law enforcement agency, its officers reside in jurisdictions across the state and are directly impacted by the definitions and credit limits set by local governments.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: GOCPP is the primary coordinating body for public safety policy and advises the Governor on strategies affecting the recruitment and retention of public safety personnel.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires civil commission appointees to coordinate with the clerk of the circuit court to schedule the administration of the oath of office and the presentation of the commission. It also adjusts the frequency of reporting from the clerks to the Secretary of State regarding individuals who have taken the oath from at least once a month to regularly, and it repeals obsolete provisions regarding taxes on commissions.
qa_results.stakeholders: Appointees for civil commissions, Clerks of the Circuit Courts, and the Secretary of State
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no state or local fiscal impact because it codifies existing practice and repeals obsolete statutory requirements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly regulates the duties of the clerks of the circuit courts, who fall under the judicial branch. The Administrative Office of the Courts provides operational and legal support to these courts and their clerks, who are responsible for administering oaths of office and reporting to the Secretary of State.

The Secretary of State is the primary recipient of the reports from the clerks regarding officials who have taken the oath of office. The agency is also responsible for delivering civil commissions to the clerks and serves as the custodian of the state's official records, including these commissions.

The bill concerns civil commissions issued by the Governor. The Governor's Office is responsible for overseen the executive branch and appointing officials to various positions that require these commissions.

The Office of Appointments, within the Executive Department, manages the appointment process for the boards and commissions for which the Governor issues these civil commissions. The bill's changes to how appointees interact with clerks directly affects the individuals processed by this office.

The bill repeals an obsolete provision requiring clerks of the circuit court to pay money received for taxes on commissions to the State Treasurer. The Treasurer's Office was historically the recipient of these funds and was consulted for the fiscal note.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill establishes the Apprenticeship 2030 Commission to examine and provide recommendations for expanding registered apprenticeships in high-demand industry sectors, with the goal of reaching 60,000 apprentices by 2030 and ensuring 45% of high school graduates complete an apprenticeship. It also mandates that the Maryland Apprenticeship and Training Council and its consultants include representation from Asian, Black, and Latino individuals.
qa_results.responsible_party: Department of Legislative Services and Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland high school students, unemployed and underemployed adults, employers in high-demand sectors, and members of the Asian, Black, and Latino communities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $36,900 in fiscal 2024 for the Maryland Department of Labor to hire a half-time contractual policy analyst to support the Apprenticeship 2030 Commission. The Department of Legislative Services provides staffing using existing resources, although additional costs may be incurred if external consultants are needed. There is no fiscal impact on local governments or state revenues.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor is responsible for appointing members to the Maryland Apprenticeship and Training Council and the new Apprenticeship 2030 Commission, as well as jointly selecting the Commission's chair.

DBM oversees the state budget and personnel; the bill results in a general fund expenditure increase of $36,900 for a new contractual position at the Maryland Department of Labor to support the commission.
The Secretary of Commerce (or designee) is a mandated member of the Apprenticeship 2030 Commission, which is tasked with identifying and addressing skill shortages in high-demand industry sectors.

The State Superintendent of Schools is a mandated member of the Commission. The bill specifically addresses the Blueprint for Maryland's Future goal of 45% of high school graduates completing a registered apprenticeship.

The Department of Labor is the primary agency impacted. The Apprenticeship and Training Council is housed within its Division of Workforce Development and Adult Learning. The Secretary of Labor is a Commission member, and the department receives dedicated funding for a new staff position to support the Commission.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates a new temporary body, the Apprenticeship 2030 Commission, and expands the representation requirements for the existing Maryland Apprenticeship and Training Council.

The Chair of the Governor’s Workforce Development Board is a member of the Commission. Additionally, the GWDB houses the Career and Technical Education (CTE) Committee, whose policies regarding youth apprenticeships control in the event of a conflict with the new Commission.

The Secretary of Higher Education is a mandated member of the Commission, which is specifically tasked with exploring 'degree apprenticeships' in fields requiring higher education such as teaching and healthcare.
The Office of Appointments will assist the Governor in recruiting and vetting candidates for the diverse representation required for the Council and the employer representatives on the Commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the public notice requirements for real property tax increases by requiring counties and municipalities to provide notice specifically when they intend to increase the actual real property tax rate, rather than when the rate exceeds the 'constant yield' rate. Additionally, it requires property assessment notices and tax bills to include clear disclosures explaining that an increase in property assessment may result in a higher tax bill even if the tax rate itself does not increase.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT), the Attorney General, and local taxing authorities (counties and municipal corporations)
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland real property owners and local government entities including counties and municipalities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no impact on State finances. Local expenditures may decrease due to reduced public notice requirements, as fewer jurisdictions may be required to place newspaper advertisements compared to the previous constant yield standards. However, local expenditures may increase slightly in fiscal 2023 to update property tax bill formats to comply with new disclosure requirements. Local revenues are not affected, and specific dollar estimates are unavailable.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is relevant as the head of the executive branch which includes the primary agency responsible for assessments (SDAT) and the Office of the Attorney General. The Governor also signed this bill into law.

The bill specifies that the Department of Assessments and Taxation must notify local taxing authorities of any changes in estimated property assessments resulting from actions or decisions of the Maryland Tax Court.
The bill explicitly mandates the Attorney General to investigate reports of taxing authorities that violate real property tax notice requirements and to institute legal action to ensure compliance.

The bill specifies that the Department of Assessments and Taxation must notify local taxing authorities of any changes in estimated property assessments resulting from actions or decisions of the Property Tax Assessment Appeals Board.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill shifts the responsibility for reporting student Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) data from local school boards to the Maryland Longitudinal Data System (MLDS) Center. It requires the Maryland Higher Education Commission (MHEC) to provide the MLDS Center with access to FAFSA data, which the Center will then use to report completion rates and related statistics to the General Assembly annually through 2028.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland high school students, local boards of education, and the Maryland Higher Education Commission.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not expected to have a material impact on State finances, as both the MLDS Center and the Maryland Higher Education Commission can fulfill the requirements using existing resources. Local school system expenditures may decrease minimally due to the elimination of FAFSA reporting requirements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill repeals the requirement for local county boards of education to report FAFSA data, which are overseen by this department, and the Maryland Longitudinal Data System matches FAFSA data against student data provided by this department.

As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the Maryland Higher Education Commission and the Maryland State Department of Education, which are both integral to the data sharing and reporting processes established by this bill.
The bill explicitly requires the commission to provide the Maryland Longitudinal Data System Center with access to student FAFSA data from federal and state financial aid systems.
The bill explicitly requires the commission to provide the Maryland Longitudinal Data System Center with access to student FAFSA data from federal and state financial aid systems.
The bill centralizes FAFSA reporting within this agency, requiring it to collect data from MHEC and submit annual reports to the General Assembly.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the maximum time members of the State Police Retirement System (SPRS) and the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System (LEOPS) can participate in the Deferred Retirement Option Program (DROP) from five to seven years. It also extends the maximum eligibility service limit for the program from 30 to 32 years and allows current DROP participants to elect to extend their participation.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System
qa_results.stakeholders: Members of the State Police Retirement System (SPRS) and the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System (LEOPS), including State troopers and local law enforcement officers in participating governmental units.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, State pension contributions (general and special funds) are expected to decrease by $400,000 in FY 2025, with annual savings increasing slightly in subsequent years through FY 2028. Total State pension liabilities are estimated to decrease by $2,780,000 because the retirement system's assumed investment return (6.8%) is higher than the interest paid on DROP accounts (4%). Local government pension contributions are also expected to decrease by approximately $150,000 annually beginning in FY 2025.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Retirement Agency is the primary entity responsible for the operation and management of the State Retirement and Pension System, which includes the State Police Retirement System and the Law Enforcement Officers' Pension System. This bill directly impacts the administration of the Deferred Retirement Option Program (DROP) managed by the agency.

The bill specifically alters the retirement and DROP eligibility rules for the State Police Retirement System. The Secretary of State Police and the agency's Director of Human Resources are required to accept binding letters of resignation and acknowledge participation extensions.

The State Treasurer acts as the chief financial officer and typically chairs or holds a leadership position on the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System. The office is responsible for the management and protection of state funds, which are impacted by the actuarial savings resulting from this bill.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency for the state, DBM is responsible for the state's budget execution and managing state employee benefits. The bill has a direct fiscal impact on state pension contributions (general and special funds) which DBM oversees.
The Department of Natural Resources employs the Natural Resources Police, whose officers are typically members of the Law Enforcement Officers' Pension System (LEOPS) affected by this bill.
MDOT oversees the Maryland Transportation Authority (MDTA), which employs a significant number of law enforcement officers who are members of LEOPS and therefore impacted by the DROP program changes.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The agency employs various law enforcement personnel, such as Fire Marshals and investigators, who participate in the Law Enforcement Officers' Pension System (LEOPS).

The OAG serves as legal counsel to the State Retirement and Pension System's Board of Trustees and the various state agencies whose personnel are affected by these statutory changes to retirement law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows eligible electricity customer-generators and community solar subscribers to accrue net excess generation credits indefinitely instead of being restricted to a 12-month accrual period. It requires the Public Service Commission to establish a method for calculating the value of these credits and removes the provision that previously limited community solar credits to 200% of a subscriber's baseline annual usage.
qa_results.stakeholders: Eligible customer-generators (owners of solar, wind, biomass, and fuel cell facilities), community solar system subscribers, electric companies, and small businesses.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Public Service Commission can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. There is no impact on State or local revenues or expenditures. Small businesses that utilize net metering may experience a meaningful benefit by carrying forward excess generation indefinitely to offset future energy costs, though the broader impact on electricity rates is expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates the Public Service Commission to establish methods for calculating the value of accrued net excess generation and to oversee changes to net energy metering and virtual net energy metering regulations.

The Office of the People's Counsel represents residential utility consumers in proceedings before the Public Service Commission, including those required by this bill to determine valuation and billing protocols for net excess generation.

The Maryland Energy Administration promotes renewable energy and energy efficiency. The bill's changes to the financial incentives and accrual rules for solar and other customer-generated electricity directly align with and impact the agency's policy goals.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Public Service Commission and other state agencies regarding the implementation and enforcement of the Public Utilities Article affected by this bill.

The Department of General Services manages state-owned facilities and leads sustainability initiatives. As the state operates its own renewable energy facilities, the changes to net excess generation accrual could affect the state's energy utility accounts.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the State Board of Social Work Examiners to issue conditional and temporary licenses for bachelor and master social work applicants who have met all education and experience requirements except for passing the required examination. A conditional license may be issued if an administrative delay or unforeseen event prevents the examination from being available for at least 15 business days. The bill also mandates annual reporting by the Board on the status of examination availability and the issuance of these licenses.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Social Work Examiners
qa_results.stakeholders: Applicants for bachelor and master social work licenses, the State Board of Social Work Examiners, and social work supervisors.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State Board of Social Work Examiners special fund revenues and expenditures may increase by an indeterminate amount should the board issue conditional licenses, potentially charging a $75 fee per license. The FY 2024 budget included $75,000 in general funds for board operations contingent on the enactment of this bill to assist with implementation and other board costs. Local fiscal impact is none.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Social Work Examiners is a regulatory board within the Maryland Department of Health. The bill directly mandates the Board's authority to issue conditional and temporary licenses and requires it to report specific data to the General Assembly.

The Department of Human Services is a major employer of social workers and was listed as an information source in the fiscal note. Changes to the availability of conditional or temporary licenses directly impact the department's ability to hire and retain essential workforce staff.

The fiscal note indicates that the bill has an indeterminate impact on special fund revenues and expenditures. DBM oversees state agency budgeting and personnel, including the allocation of general funds contingent on the enactment of this bill.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and its boards. The agency will be responsible for providing legal guidance on the implementation and regulation of the new conditional and temporary license categories.

As an agency that adjudicates contested cases for state regulatory bodies, OAH would be responsible for hearing appeals regarding the denial or revocation of the conditional and temporary licenses established by this bill.

The bill includes specific provisions for the renewal of temporary social work licenses for service members or military spouses, aligning with the department's advocacy and support mission for military families.
The bill grants the Board the authority to renew a temporary license specifically upon the Governor's declaration of a state of emergency by executive order or proclamation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of Health to develop and implement a public awareness campaign to encourage the use of mental health advance directives. Additionally, the Behavioral Health Administration and the Maryland Health Care Commission must study how first responders and behavioral health crisis providers can access the statewide advance directives database when responding to emergencies.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health, Behavioral Health Administration, and Maryland Health Care Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Recipients of mental health services and their families, health care providers, first responders, and various state and local health and public safety agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: General fund expenditures increase by at least $140,700 in FY 2024 for the Maryland Department of Health to hire one administrative program manager and procure campaign materials. Ongoing expenditures for staffing and operations are estimated between $87,400 and $100,400 annually through FY 2028. The required study and report will be completed using existing resources, and there is no material impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill mandates the Maryland Department of Health to develop and implement a public awareness campaign for mental health advance directives. Its Behavioral Health Administration is also tasked with jointly studying database access for crisis providers.

The Commission is explicitly required to jointly study the feasibility and implementation of access to the mental health advance directives database for first responders and behavioral health crisis providers.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly names this department as an agency that must be included in and support the public awareness campaign outreach efforts regarding mental health advance directives.
The Maryland State Police are specifically identified in the bill as an agency required to participate in and support the public awareness and outreach campaign for mental health advance directives.
The bill (referencing the Department of Veterans Affairs) identifies this agency as a required partner for outreach and awareness initiatives related to mental health advance directives.
The bill mandates outreach to academic institutions, which are overseen and coordinated by the Higher Education Commission.
As an academic institution, Morgan State University is identified in the bill as a target entity for outreach and participation in the mental health advance directive public awareness campaign.
The University System of Maryland's member academic institutions are listed as partners for the mandated public awareness campaign outreach efforts.
As a state academic institution, the college is included in the list of entities expected to participate in the mental health advance directive outreach initiatives.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Governor to include an annual appropriation of $500,000 in the state budget for fiscal years 2025 through 2028 to fund the Prince George's County Reentry Employment Incentive Program. The program provides financial incentives to businesses that hire qualifying formerly incarcerated residents of the county.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George's County Office of the County Executive
qa_results.stakeholders: Formerly incarcerated residents of Prince George's County, commercial and nonprofit businesses in Prince George's County, and the Prince George's County government.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures increase by $500,000 annually from fiscal 2025 through 2028 due to the mandated appropriation. Prince George’s County revenues and expenditures will increase by an equivalent $500,000 annually during this period as the funds are received and distributed to the county's reentry employment program.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates the Governor to include a specific appropriation of $500,000 for the Prince George’s County Reentry Employment Incentive Program in the annual budget bill for fiscal years 2025 through 2028.
As the agency responsible for developing the state budget and supervising its execution, DBM is the primary body tasked with implementing the mandated appropriation required by the bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill focuses on reentry employment for individuals who were previously confined for criminal convictions. This program aligns with the department's mission to reduce recidivism and manage justice-involved individuals.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office coordinates state public safety strategies and manages funds to reduce crime and recidivism, making a reentry employment incentive program highly relevant to its policy goals.
As the state's chief financial officer, the Comptroller is responsible for the distribution of state funds and the payment of state bills, including the mandated grant to Prince George's County.

The Department of Labor oversees workforce development and adult learning programs; the reentry program incentivizes the employment of individuals, which falls under the broader scope of the agency's labor and economic growth mission.

The board sets the statewide vision for workforce development and coordinates solutions to ensure residents, including those facing employment barriers like former incarceration, have pathways to prosperity.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the Maryland Aviation Commission from 9 to 13 members and requires the commission to develop policies to mitigate the negative impacts of the aviation and airport industries on local communities. It mandates that four of the new members be recommended by the Anne Arundel and Howard county Senate delegations, requires new members to receive orientation on their duties, and subjects the commission to enhanced Open Meetings Act requirements, including live video streaming and specific online agenda posting timelines.
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents in communities near airports (particularly in Anne Arundel and Howard counties), airport employees and their representatives, local governments, and the air carrier, concessionaire, and support services industries.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State Transportation Trust Fund expenditures may increase minimally to meet the bill's enhanced Open Meetings Act requirements, such as providing live video streaming and maintaining archived recordings. Other requirements, including the expansion of the commission and additional reporting, are expected to be handled using existing budgeted resources. There is no impact on State or local revenues.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Aviation Commission governs and directs the Maryland Aviation Administration, developing management policies and approving major capital projects and regulations for state-owned airports managed by the MAA.

The Secretary of Transportation serves as the Chairman of the Maryland Aviation Commission, and the MAA is a modal administration within MDOT. The bill significantly alters the commission's structure and duties.
The Governor appoints the majority of the commission members and receives approvals for major capital projects from the commission prior to legislative submission.

This office assists the Governor in recruiting, vetting, and recommending candidates for state commissions. The bill increases the number of appointees and mandates specific recommendations from local delegations and industry experience.
The Secretary of Commerce is designated as a nonvoting ex officio member of the Maryland Aviation Commission.
The commission's annual report, including its expenditure estimates, is subject to review by DBM. DBM also oversees state personnel resources and benefits.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Aviation Commission is a permanent executive commission governed by the provisions of this bill regarding its composition, orientation, and reporting requirements.
As the chief legal officer for state agencies and boards, the OAG provides legal guidance on implementing new commission policies, composition changes, and Open Meetings Act compliance.
The bill requires the commission to establish policies to mitigate negative impacts on healthy and livable communities and to consider health impacts related to airport infrastructure.
The bill subjects the Maryland Aviation Commission to enhanced Open Meetings Act requirements, including live video streaming and online publication of agendas and minutes.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill clarifies the formula for calculating the annual mandated General Fund appropriation for the Maryland State Arts Council (MSAC) beginning in fiscal 2025. It defines the 'general fund growth adjustment' and specifies how revenues from the State admissions and amusement tax are factored into the Council's funding formula, effectively codifying current budgetary practices.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Budget and Management and the Maryland State Arts Council
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland arts organizations, local arts agencies, individual artists, public school students, and the general public benefiting from cultural arts programs.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill clarifies an existing mandated appropriation for the Maryland State Arts Council starting in fiscal 2025. According to the fiscal note, the bill has no effect on State expenditures or revenues as it aligns statutory language with the Department of Budget and Management's current interpretation of the funding mandate. No local fiscal impact is expected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires the Governor to include a specific mandated appropriation amount for the Maryland State Arts Council in the annual budget bill submitted to the General Assembly.

The Comptroller is responsible for distributing the Admissions and Amusement tax revenues mentioned in the bill and chairs the Board of Revenue Estimates, whose revenue projections are the basis for the bill's General Fund growth adjustment calculation.

The State Treasurer is a member of the Board of Revenue Estimates, whose report provides the revenue projection data used to calculate the General Fund growth adjustment for the Council's mandated funding.
As the state's principal budget agency, DBM is responsible for calculating and implementing the mandated appropriation formula specified in the bill for inclusion in the annual state budget.

The bill clarifies the funding formula for the Maryland State Arts Council and mentions the Maryland E-Nnovation Initiative Fund and the Special Fund for Preservation of Cultural Arts, all of which are programs or units overseen by the Department of Commerce.

The bill specifies the distribution of tax revenue to the Calvert County Youth Recreational Opportunities Fund, which is established under the Natural Resources Article and falls under the department's general domain of recreation and conservation.
The bill details the distribution of State admissions and amusement tax revenue to the Maryland Stadium Authority and funds it manages, such as the Michael Erin Busch Sports Fund.
The bill explicitly grants the Legislative Auditor the authority to conduct reviews or audits of any recipient of a grant from the Maryland State Arts Council.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Task Force to Study Crime Classification, which is tasked with recommending a method for classifying crimes and civil offenses under Maryland law and creating a logical categorization of all existing state violations.
qa_results.stakeholders: State legislators, legal professionals, law enforcement, and individuals subject to Maryland's criminal and civil laws.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by at least $177,400 in FY 2024 only for the Office of the Attorney General to staff the task force. This cost covers the hiring of contractual personnel, including one full-time attorney and one part-time paralegal. Any expense reimbursements for task force members are expected to be minimal and absorbable with existing resources. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Attorney General (OAG) is the primary agency responsible for providing staff for the task force. Additionally, the Attorney General (or their designee) is a mandated member of the task force. The fiscal note indicates that OAG will incur approximately $177,400 in costs for contractual personnel to fulfill these requirements.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill explicitly creates the Task Force to Study Crime Classification, which is an entity that falls under this administrative category of temporary bodies established for special studies and recommendations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Public Defender (or their designee) is a mandated member of the task force. The task force's focus on classifying and categorizing all crimes and civil offenses in Maryland directly impacts the agency's work in defending indigent individuals within the criminal justice system.

The task force is required to report its findings and recommendations to the Governor. As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the broader policy implications of crime classification and criminal justice reform.
The bill involves a significant fiscal impact of approximately $177,400 for personnel, which is managed and overseen by DBM as the state's principal fiscal and personnel agency.

The categorization and classification of offenses (e.g., felony vs. misdemeanor or civil vs. criminal) significantly impact judicial branch operations, jurisdiction, and the management of legal proceedings.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: Crime classification determines the severity of penalties and sentences, which directly affects the management of the state's correctional facilities, pretrial detention centers, and parole and probation services.
As the primary state law enforcement agency, the classification of offenses into criminal or civil categories directly impacts MSP's enforcement activities, reporting, and roadside safety operations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As Maryland's primary coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies, this agency has a high level of subject-matter interest in the classification and categorization of crimes, regardless of its removal from the formal task force membership in the final bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE) to conduct a statewide recycling needs assessment through an independent consultant and establishes a Producer Responsibility Advisory Council to develop recommendations for a producer responsibility program for packaging materials. It further mandates that MDE approve a single producer responsibility organization to represent producers and expands the definition of 'organics recycling' to include compostable packaging materials.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of the Environment (Office of Recycling)
qa_results.stakeholders: Producers of packaging materials, local governments, recycling and organics recycling processors, waste management companies, and Maryland residents.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures increase by $532,100 in FY 2024 and $12,100 in FY 2025. These costs primarily cover the procurement of an independent consultant to conduct the statewide recycling needs assessment ($500,000) and a part-time contractual position within MDE to staff the advisory council and oversee the assessment. While the bill mentions fees from producer responsibility organizations to cover costs, the immediate fiscal impact is noted as general fund expenditures. State revenues are not affected, and local governments are expected to consult using existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for environmental protection, MDE's Office of Recycling is responsible for hiring a consultant to conduct the statewide recycling needs assessment, staffing the new Producer Responsibility Plan Advisory Council, and approving the producer responsibility organizations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Producer Responsibility Plan Advisory Council, a formal body of appointed stakeholders tasked with evaluating and making recommendations on the establishment and implementation of a producer responsibility program for packaging materials.

MES is specifically named in the current law referenced by the bill as a coordinating body for recycling reports and studies. Additionally, the agency's operational role in managing state recycling infrastructure is directly impacted by the bill's assessment and infrastructure evaluations.

The bill requires the statewide recycling needs assessment to evaluate worker conditions, wages, and benefits at recycling facilities, as well as identify opportunities to increase employment in the industry, which falls under this agency's workforce oversight.

The assessment mandated by the bill must evaluate economic opportunities, business growth, and recommendations for incentives to stimulate the state's recycling industry, aligning with Commerce's mission to attract and retain businesses.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The recycling needs assessment must specifically evaluate economic and participation opportunities for women and minority-owned businesses and individuals within the recycling system.
The Comptroller is responsible for accounting for the State Recycling Trust Fund, which will receive registration fees and administrative penalties established by the bill.
The Treasurer is the custodian of the State Recycling Trust Fund, which is modified by the bill to include a separate account for producer responsibility organization fees and penalties.

This agency is responsible for overseeing the fiscal impact described in the fiscal note, including general fund expenditures for a new contractual employee and the costs associated with the independent consultant.
The OAG will provide legal counsel to MDE regarding the drafting of regulations, program oversight, and the enforcement of administrative penalties against producers.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch's implementation of this policy, receives the required reports from MDE and the Advisory Council, and manages related policy coordination.
The bill aligns with this multi-agency initiative's goals of sustainable development and reducing the state's carbon footprint through improved resource conservation and waste reduction strategies.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits a person from knowingly or intentionally accessing and viewing visual representations (including computer-generated images) of a child under the age of 16 engaged in sexual conduct, sadomasochistic abuse, or sexual excitement. It expands existing child pornography possession laws to include these specific acts, applying the same penalties and affirmative defenses.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary and the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals who access or view child pornography, law enforcement agencies, and the Maryland court and correctional systems.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill results in a minimal increase in State and local general fund revenues and expenditures due to the expanded application of existing criminal penalties. First-time offenders are subject to a misdemeanor conviction with up to 5 years imprisonment and/or a $2,500 fine, while repeat offenders face a felony conviction with up to 10 years imprisonment and/or a $10,000 fine. The Fiscal Note indicates that while the number of new convictions is expected to be minimal, the average cost per inmate in State facilities is approximately $4,970 per month.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The department is responsible for the incarceration and supervision of individuals convicted under the bill's expanded criminal provisions. The fiscal note explicitly mentions increased expenditures for state correctional facilities and reimbursements to local detention centers.

This agency supports the judicial branch, which will adjudicate the new and expanded criminal cases. The fiscal note identifies the Judiciary as a primary source and notes a minimal impact on court operations and revenue from fines.

The office is responsible for prosecuting offenses related to the integrity of state officials and may investigate misconduct, though this specific criminal law more broadly impacts law enforcement and State's Attorneys.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The OPD is mandated to provide legal representation for indigent defendants charged under the bill's new criminal prohibitions. The fiscal note lists them as a key stakeholder and source of information.

As the state's primary law enforcement agency, the State Police are responsible for enforcing criminal laws, including those related to child exploitation and the investigation of child pornography crimes.

The department is Maryland's primary social service provider and protector of children from abuse and neglect. The bill addresses the sexual exploitation of children, which directly intersects with the agency's mission to protect vulnerable youth.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office serves as the state's coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies, directly advising the Governor on policies like those established in this bill to combat child pornography.

The OAG represents the state in criminal appeals, including those resulting from convictions under child pornography statutes, and provides legal counsel to state agencies on criminal law enforcement.
The Comptroller is responsible for collecting state revenues, including general fund revenues from fines imposed by the District Court for violations of this bill.

DBM oversees state budget execution and fiscal planning, including the anticipated minimal increase in general fund expenditures for incarceration and county reimbursements mentioned in the fiscal note.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This entity includes the Maryland State Commission on Criminal Sentencing Policy, which is explicitly mentioned in the fiscal note as a source of sentencing data related to child pornography offenses.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and is responsible for enforcing state laws and proposing the budget that supports correctional and judicial operations affected by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program to include domestic partners of state employees and retirees. It mandates that terms denoting a spousal relationship in the context of the program's governing statutes be construed to include parties in a domestic partnership, thereby granting them and their dependents eligibility for health, life, and dental benefits equivalent to those provided to spouses.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland State employees, retirees, and their domestic partners and dependents.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State expenditures for the Department of Budget and Management will increase by an indeterminate amount beginning in fiscal 2024 to provide health and welfare benefits to newly eligible domestic partners and their dependents. The exact fiscal impact cannot be reliably estimated because the number of individuals who will enroll under these new provisions is unknown. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DBM is the primary agency responsible for the administration and development of the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program. The bill directly requires DBM to update contribution tables, eligibility rules, and internal systems to accommodate domestic partners.

As the state's chief financial officer and manager of the Central Payroll Bureau, the Comptroller's office must implement post-tax payroll deductions and calculate imputed income for domestic partner benefits to ensure compliance with IRS W-2 reporting.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, and the bill mandates that the health program encompass all units within the executive branch, including those with independent personnel systems.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including DBM, regarding the statutory interpretation of the new provisions and the legal requirements for establishing domestic partnerships.

The bill explicitly includes state retirees in the expanded eligibility for health benefits; the Retirement Agency manages communications and support services for the retiree population impacted by this change.

The Judicial branch is explicitly encompassed by the bill's provisions. As the central support agency for the judiciary, the AOC is responsible for the operational and financial administration of benefits for its employees.

The bill applies to units with independent personnel systems, specifically including large entities like the University System of Maryland, which must comply with the expanded benefit requirements for its faculty and staff.
Morgan State University operates an independent personnel system from the USM and is explicitly required to follow the updated health and welfare benefit provisions for domestic partnerships.
As an independent state college with its own personnel system, Saint Mary's College is encompassed by the bill's requirement to include domestic partners in the state health program.
MDOT manages various modal administrations with unique personnel requirements that are included in the scope of the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program.

MDTA is an independent state agency with its own personnel and police force that operates within the state's health benefits program and is affected by the expansion of spousal terms to include domestic partners.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires a resident member of a pass-through entity (PTE) to add back to their Maryland adjusted gross income the amount of any income tax credit claimed for taxes paid by the PTE to another state, provided those taxes were deductible in determining the PTE's income under the federal Internal Revenue Code. The measure is designed to align the state tax treatment of out-of-state PTE taxes with the treatment of Maryland-specific PTE taxes.
qa_results.stakeholders: Resident members of pass-through entities, including shareholders of S corporations, partners in partnerships, and members of limited liability companies (LLCs).
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund revenues and local income tax revenues may increase beginning in fiscal 2024. The specific financial impact is indeterminate because it depends on the tax elections of out-of-state and multi-state pass-through entities and their respective tax treatments in other jurisdictions. Expenditures are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the primary agency responsible for tax collection and enforcement. As the agency that requested this bill, it will be responsible for implementing the new addition modification, updating tax forms, and collecting the resulting revenues from pass-through entity members.

The Department of Budget and Management is responsible for revenue estimation and long-term fiscal planning. This bill is projected to increase general fund revenues beginning in fiscal 2024, which directly impacts the state's budget projections and execution managed by this agency.

The Maryland Tax Court adjudicates disputes between taxpayers and the Comptroller. Changes to the tax code, specifically new addition modifications like the one proposed in this bill, can lead to factual or legal disputes that the Court would be responsible for resolving.

As the state's chief financial agency responsible for receiving and depositing state funds, the Treasurer's Office is impacted by the increase in tax revenues collected and distributed to the general fund.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and proposes the annual state budget. The fiscal impact of this bill on the general fund affects the financial resources available for the Governor's policy initiatives and budget proposals.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Comptroller, regarding the interpretation and enforcement of tax laws. They may be involved in defending the state's position in cases where the application of this new tax modification is contested.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Comptroller, acting as the Administrator of abandoned property, to determine the commercial value of non-monetary abandoned property within one year of delivery. It authorizes the Comptroller to dispose of property that lacks commercial value by returning it to the holder, destroying it, or using other disposal methods instead of conducting a mandatory sale. Additionally, the bill replaces the requirement to publish sale notices in newspapers with a requirement to provide 'reasonable notice' to the public.
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners and holders of non-monetary abandoned property, the Comptroller's Office, and the general public.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local finances. However, the Comptroller's Office may realize cost efficiencies due to an expected reduction in storage, maintenance, and cataloging costs for items that have no commercial value and can now be disposed of rather than stored indefinitely for sale.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly designates the Comptroller, as the Administrator of abandoned property, with the responsibility to value and dispose of nonmonetary abandoned property. This significantly changes the agency's operational procedures for handling unclaimed assets.

The fiscal note indicates that the state may realize cost efficiencies, particularly in reduced storage costs, due to the new disposal authorities. DBM is responsible for supervising budget execution and estimating revenues affected by these efficiencies.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Comptroller and would be responsible for interpreting the new disposal authorities and the immunity provision (Section 17-316(F)) protecting the State from legal actions related to these transactions.
As the head of the executive branch, the Governor oversees the Comptroller's operations and officially signed the bill into law (Chapter 19).

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill makes a technical correction to the Maryland income tax law by updating an incorrect cross-reference to the federal Internal Revenue Code regarding the Work Opportunity Tax Credit. The correction ensures the State's nonrefundable income tax credit for employers correctly aligns with federal law.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland employers, including not-for-profit entities, who hire individuals from targeted groups that face significant barriers to employment.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is technical in nature and has no impact on State or local finances. Both state and local revenues and expenditures remain unchanged.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the state's chief tax collector and the agency that requested this bill. The bill corrects a technical error in the tax code related to the federal Work Opportunity Tax Credit, which the Comptroller's Office is responsible for administering at the state level.

The federal Work Opportunity Tax Credit requires certification from a designated state workforce agency. The Department of Labor typically handles these workforce certifications for targeted employment groups, making the correct statutory alignment of this credit relevant to their operational context.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, the Department of Budget and Management oversees revenue estimates and budget execution. While the fiscal note indicates no impact because the bill is technical, DBM monitors all changes to the Maryland tax code for fiscal policy consistency.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and signs legislation into law. This bill was introduced as a departmental request from an executive agency under the administration's purview.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel and reviews all legislation for legal form and sufficiency. This technical correction ensures that the Maryland Code correctly references the federal Internal Revenue Code.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the eligibility requirements for the Maryland child and dependent care tax credit by limiting the credit specifically to resident taxpayers, thereby excluding nonresident taxpayers from claiming it.
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland resident and nonresident taxpayers who claim the child and dependent care tax credit
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund revenues are estimated to increase by approximately $332,000 annually beginning in fiscal 2024 due to the elimination of the credit for nonresident taxpayers. There is no estimated impact on local government finances or State expenditures.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the primary agency responsible for administering the state's income tax laws and collecting revenues. This departmental bill was introduced at the Comptroller's request and directly impacts the agency's duties in managing and enforcing eligibility for the child and dependent care tax credit.

The Department of Budget and Management is responsible for revenue estimation and managing the state's fiscal planning. This bill has a direct impact on the general fund, with estimated revenue increases of $332,000 annually starting in FY 2024, which DBM must incorporate into the state budget.

The Maryland Tax Court is the administrative body that hears appeals from the final decisions of taxing authorities like the Comptroller. It would be the venue for adjudicating disputes regarding the denial of the tax credit based on the residency requirements established by this bill.

The State Treasurer is responsible for receiving and depositing state funds. As this bill is projected to increase state general fund revenues by over $300,000 per year, the Treasurer's Office will manage these additional deposits.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Comptroller and the state government. They would be responsible for interpreting the legal definitions of 'resident' and 'taxpayer' under the Tax - General Article as modified by this legislation.
The Governor oversees the executive branch and the state budget. This bill affects state revenues and tax policy, which are central to the Governor's fiscal and administrative oversight.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the mandated annual appropriation for the National Capital Strategic Economic Development Fund from $7,000,000 to $12,000,000 beginning in fiscal year 2025. It also updates the geographical boundaries for eligible community enhancement projects in Montgomery and Prince George’s counties, making existing usage requirements permanent and repealing a provision that would have restricted the fund's use starting in fiscal year 2024.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
qa_results.stakeholders: Sustainable communities in Montgomery and Prince George’s counties, local government agencies, housing authorities, community development organizations, and small businesses involved in development activities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Beginning in fiscal year 2025, State expenditures (either general funds or general obligation bonds) will increase by $5,000,000 annually to meet the new mandated appropriation level of $12,000,000. This results in a commensurate increase in special fund revenues and expenditures for the National Capital Strategic Economic Development Fund. Local government revenues and expenditures may increase to the extent they receive additional financial assistance from the fund.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for community development and the administrator of the National Capital Strategic Economic Development Fund, the department is responsible for managing the fund, designating 'sustainable communities,' and overseeing the distribution of the increased appropriations for community enhancement projects.

The Governor is explicitly required by the bill to include the mandated $12 million annual appropriation in the state budget beginning in fiscal 2025 and oversees the executive branch's implementation of the economic development strategy.
This agency is responsible for developing the state budget and managing the fiscal impact of the $5 million annual increase in mandated appropriations required by the bill.

The agency provides data analysis and policy guidance for land use and is heavily involved in the definition and recommendation of 'sustainable communities' and 'priority funding areas' which determine project eligibility under the bill.
The Treasurer manages the state's funds and General Obligation bond issuances, which the fiscal note indicates are a likely source for the $5 million increase in funding for the development fund.
The Board, composed of the Governor, Comptroller, and Treasurer, must approve the issuance of State bonds and significant expenditures related to the capital projects funded by this bill.
The Comptroller monitors state revenues and expenditures and serves as a member of the Board of Public Works, which oversees the fiscal allocation of the fund's resources.

As the state's primary economic development agency, Commerce has a secondary interest in programs like the National Capital Strategic Economic Development Fund that aim to stimulate regional job creation and business retention.
This multi-agency initiative coordinates the planning visions and sustainable development policies that underpin the eligibility criteria for the projects funded by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes procedures for sheriff's sales of real property, including requiring judgment creditors to provide notice to record owners and subordinate lienholders. It specifies that a sheriff's sale extinguishes subordinate liens and allows sheriffs to use assessment records instead of appraisals for real property. Additionally, it authorizes the Judiciary to transfer up to 12,000,000 dollars from its general fund appropriation to the Circuit Court Real Property Records Improvement Fund.
qa_results.stakeholders: Judgment creditors, property owners, subordinate lienholders, condominium and homeowners associations, and local governments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Special fund revenues increase by up to 12,000,000 dollars in fiscal 2023, while general fund revenues decrease by the same amount. This results from the bill allowing the Judiciary to transfer funds to the Circuit Court Real Property Records Improvement Fund that would otherwise revert to the general fund.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Judiciary by authorizing a transfer of up to $12 million from its General Fund appropriation to the Circuit Court Real Property Records Improvement Fund. It also establishes new procedural requirements for sheriff's sales, including the filing of affidavits with the court and adherence to rules adopted by the Supreme Court of Maryland.

The bill has a notable fiscal impact by authorizing the transfer of $12 million that would otherwise revert to the State's General Fund. DBM is responsible for supervising budget execution and fiscal planning.
As the state's chief financial officer, the Comptroller oversees the collection and distribution of state funds, including the movement of appropriations between general and special funds.

The bill provides significant funding to the Circuit Court Real Property Records Improvement Fund, which is used to support and preserve land records, a core interest and responsibility of the Maryland State Archives.

The legislation modifies the enforcement of liens and procedures for real property sales, including specific provisions for homeowners associations and local government liens related to property maintenance and nuisance violations.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies and the Judiciary regarding real property law, the extinguishment of subordinate liens, and the statutory requirements for judgment executions.
The bill's changes to sheriff's sales and subordinate interest extinguishment may impact small businesses that serve as judgment creditors or hold secondary interests in real property.
The Treasurer is responsible for the management and distribution of state funds and sits on the Board of Public Works, which oversees significant fiscal transfers and state property interests.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill expands the investigative and prosecutorial authority of the Independent Investigations Division (IID) within the Office of the Attorney General (OAG). Specifically, it grants the Attorney General exclusive authority to prosecute criminal offenses discovered during investigations of police-involved incidents resulting in death or life-threatening injury. It also allows State's Attorneys to refer serious bodily injury cases to the IID and requires the division to submit annual reports on its activities to the Governor and General Assembly.
qa_results.stakeholders: Police officers, law enforcement agencies, State's Attorneys, and the general public.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $548,900 in FY 2024 for the Office of the Attorney General to hire four additional staff members and cover operational costs. Expenditures are projected to increase annually, reaching $729,700 by FY 2028. Local fiscal impact is expected to be minimal as resources are redirected within State's Attorney's offices.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Office of the Attorney General by expanding the investigative jurisdiction of its Independent Investigations Division (IID) and granting the Attorney General exclusive authority to prosecute certain criminal offenses discovered during these investigations.

As Maryland's primary law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police is subject to the bill's notification and cooperation requirements. Furthermore, the bill explicitly authorizes the IID to detail MSP officers to assist with investigations of police misconduct.

The bill requires the IID to submit an annual report of its activities to the Governor. Additionally, the Governor is mandated to include funding in the state budget sufficient for the division's operation.

The bill has a significant fiscal impact, requiring an increase in general fund expenditures ($548,900 in FY 2024) for staffing and operations. DBM, as the state's principal fiscal agency, is responsible for overseeing these expenditures and the associated personnel management.

The bill involves the use of grand juries, issuance of subpoenas, and potential injunctive relief in court. The AOC provides the operational and legal support necessary for the judiciary to handle these expanded prosecutorial and investigative proceedings.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The expansion of the Attorney General's prosecutorial authority over police-involved incidents will directly impact the caseload and defense strategies of the OPD, which represents indigent defendants in criminal proceedings.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the state's coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategy, GOCPP is impacted by changes to the investigation and prosecution of police misconduct, which are central to its mission of reducing crime and improving public safety policy.

The Office of the State Prosecutor investigates criminal offenses related to the integrity of state officials. The IID's expanded jurisdiction over police misconduct may lead to overlapping areas of inquiry or require coordination between the two independent offices.

MDOT oversees several law enforcement entities, including the Maryland Transit Administration Police and the Maryland Aviation Administration's police force at BWI, all of which are subject to the bill's requirements to notify the IID of certain incidents.
The MDTA maintains its own nationally accredited police force, which must comply with the bill's mandates for notifying the IID of police-involved incidents and cooperating with investigations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The DPSCS employs law enforcement and correctional personnel who are subject to the bill's notification and cooperation requirements regarding incidents resulting in death or serious injury.

The DNR's Natural Resources Police must comply with the bill's requirements for reporting incidents to the IID and providing evidence during state investigations of police-involved deaths or injuries.
Member institutions within the USM operate their own police departments, which are law enforcement agencies required to adhere to the notification and cooperation protocols established by the bill.

Morgan State University operates its own police department, making it subject to the bill's requirements for reporting police-involved incidents to the Attorney General's Independent Investigations Division.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS regulates the state's EMS network; because the bill concerns incidents resulting in death or injuries likely to result in death, the protocols for medical reporting and prehospital care within their system are relevant to the IID's investigations.
MCSS oversees school safety personnel and resources; incidents involving school police officers that result in serious injury or death would be subject to investigation by the IID under this bill.

The OLA conducts fiscal compliance and performance audits of state agencies; the expansion of the OAG's IID and its associated funding will be subject to future audits by this office to ensure financial accountability and legal compliance.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates Maryland law by replacing the term 'inmate' with 'incarcerated individual' in all statutes, executive orders, rules, and regulations. It also directs the publisher of the Annotated Code of Maryland to update cross-references and terminology to reflect this change.
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals detained or confined in Maryland correctional facilities and State officials or units responsible for maintaining legal records and policies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no state or local fiscal impact, as the changes are technical in nature and do not affect governmental finances or operations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency responsible for the state's correctional facilities and the supervision of individuals within them, this bill directly affects their core terminology, official documentation, and operational policies.
This agency manages youth in residential facilities and juvenile justice systems; the bill's requirement to update terminology in all state documents applies to their operations and records.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Public Defender represents indigent individuals in the justice system; the bill's mandate to rename 'inmates' to 'incarcerated individuals' affects their legal filings, case records, and communications.
The judiciary must update court rules, legal forms, and terminology in judicial proceedings to align with the new statutory definition.
As the chief legal representative for the state and its agencies, the OAG must ensure all legal advice, contracts, and regulations reflect the updated terminology.
The Department of Health provides behavioral and public health services to individuals within correctional facilities and must update its regulatory and service documents accordingly.
The State Police interact with individuals entering or being transported between correctional facilities and must update enforcement records and internal policies.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies, this agency is responsible for policy development that must now use the updated terminology.
This agency adjudicates contested cases for correctional and juvenile services; its administrative law judges and legal documents will be required to use the new term.
This agency interacts with individuals in correctional facilities regarding child support, foster care, and other social services, requiring updates to case management documents.
The Department of Labor administers workforce development and adult learning programs within correctional facilities, which will require terminology updates in program materials.
The Department oversees educational programs for incarcerated populations and must update its policies and certification documents.
The bill specifically mandates that Executive Orders and documents created by any official or unit of the state must adopt the new terminology.
As the custodian of state records and executive orders, the Secretary of State is responsible for the official transition of these terms in the state's permanent records.
The Archives preserve state executive, legislative, and judicial records; the renaming of 'inmates' in all future government documents affects their archival collection and categorization.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Carroll County Commissioners to borrow up to $65.6 million by issuing general obligation bonds to finance various public projects, including school renovations, road improvements, water and sewer infrastructure, and loans for volunteer fire department equipment and facilities.
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents, students, and emergency service providers in Carroll County
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Carroll County receives up to $65,600,000 in bond proceeds for capital projects. Debt service expenditures are estimated to increase by approximately $4,400,000 annually over a 20-year period, based on a 2.97% interest rate. There is no state fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is responsible for overseeing the executive branch and signing legislation, including local bond authorizations such as this one for Carroll County.
The bonds and the interest they generate are exempt from state and local taxation; the Comptroller is the state's principal tax collector and administrator of state tax laws.
The bill authorizes borrowing for the acquisition of agricultural land and woodland preservation easements, which is a core part of the agency's mission to protect environmental resources.
A significant portion of the bond funds ($21.2 million) is specifically earmarked for public school infrastructure projects, including HVAC and roof replacements.
The bill authorizes loans to volunteer fire departments for fire or emergency-related equipment and facilities, aligning with the agency's role in emergency preparedness and jurisdiction support.
The bond authorization includes financing for the construction and improvement of public health and social services facilities in Carroll County.
The bill explicitly allows for bond proceeds to be used for the development of buildings and facilities related to social services.
The bill funds conservation and open space projects, including stormwater facility renovations and woodland preservation easements.
The agency promotes sustainable growth and manages historic and cultural programs; the bill involves land use planning through agricultural and woodland preservation.
The bond proceeds are authorized for water and sewer infrastructure, landfills, and watershed assessment and improvement (NPDES) projects.
The bill authorizes the county to finance public works projects such as roads, bridges, and pavement management programs.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: A major portion of the bond authorization ($21.7 million) is designated for public safety, including a new headquarters for the Sheriff's Office.
The bill authorizes bond proceeds to be used for the construction and development of buildings and facilities at Carroll Community College.
The agency oversees the planning and funding of public school construction and capital improvements, which constitute a major expenditure category under this bill.
The bill identifies libraries as public facilities that may be constructed, improved, or developed using the authorized bond proceeds.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill renames and expands the scope of the Black Bear Damage Reimbursement Fund to include grants for nonlethal bear damage prevention and conflict reduction projects. It increases the maximum annual reimbursement for bear-related damages from $3,000 to $5,000, establishes a $500 minimum reimbursement threshold, and authorizes a discretionary annual state appropriation of $50,000 to support the fund's activities.
qa_results.stakeholders: Farmers, landowners, local governments, businesses, nonprofit organizations, tribes, and higher education institutions in Maryland's black bear regions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures increase by $145,600 in FY 2024, which includes a $50,000 discretionary appropriation to the fund and $95,637 for administrative costs, including one natural resources technician and a vehicle. Special fund revenues and expenditures increase by $50,000 annually. Local governments and small businesses, especially farmers, may see a financial benefit through grants or increased damage reimbursements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency responsible for managing the Black Bear Damage Prevention and Reimbursement Fund, DNR is tasked with establishing the new grant program, accepting grant requests, determining project eligibility, and adopting regulations to carry out the bill's provisions.

The bill involves a potential annual appropriation to the fund and significant administrative costs for DNR, including the hiring of a new natural resources technician, which falls under DBM's oversight of state personnel and the budget.

The bill directly impacts the agricultural community by providing grants and reimbursements for damages to crops, beehives, livestock, and poultry, and references definitions within the Agriculture Article.

The bill identifies institutions of higher education as eligible grant recipients and specifically requires that damage amounts for reimbursement be determined by an extension agent of the University of Maryland Cooperative Extension Service.
The bill explicitly states that the State Treasurer shall hold the Black Bear Damage Prevention and Reimbursement Fund.
The bill explicitly states that the State Comptroller shall account for the fund.
The bill authorizes the Governor to include a discretionary appropriation of at least $50,000 to the fund in the annual budget bill.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Natural Resources and would likely assist in the drafting of the required regulations for the expanded fund and grant program.

As an advocate for rural interests and housed within the Department of Agriculture, the Council is relevant because the bill addresses issues primarily affecting rural counties where black bear conflicts occur.
As a public institution of higher education, Morgan State University is identified in the bill as an entity eligible to apply for grants from the fund.
As a public institution of higher education, St. Mary's College of Maryland is identified in the bill as an entity eligible to apply for grants from the fund.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes Class 4 limited winery license holders to prepare, sell, and serve a wider range of food items, specifically including sandwiches and other light fare. It extends the standard hours for on-premises consumption from 6:00 p.m. to 10:00 p.m. daily and removes the requirement that wineries provide 14 days' notice to the State before holding promotional events. Additionally, wineries may serve an unrestricted menu if they are licensed as food establishments and maintain certain food-to-alcohol sales ratios.
qa_results.stakeholders: Class 4 limited winery license holders, small business wineries, and winery consumers
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government operations or finances. However, the fiscal note indicates a meaningful impact on small businesses, as Class 4 limited winery licensees may see an increase in revenues resulting from expanded food service authorizations and extended operating hours.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments (Agriculture, Health, and Commerce) tasked with implementing the bill's provisions and coordinates the policy initiatives related to the state's winery industry.

The Comptroller regulates the alcohol industry in Maryland. This bill directly alters administrative requirements for winery license holders under the Comptroller's purview, including the repeal of a 14-day notice requirement for promotional events and the oversight of product storage and warehouse access.

The Secretary of Agriculture is specifically authorized by the bill to grant annual exemptions to the requirement that 51% of a winery's ingredients be grown in Maryland and must adopt regulations for these exemptions.

The bill allows wineries to expand their food service operations provided they are licensed as food establishments under Title 21 of the Health - General Article, which is administered and enforced by the Maryland Department of Health.

The bill impacts the economic viability and operational scope of Maryland wineries, which are significant small businesses and contributors to the state's tourism and agricultural economy promoted by the Department of Commerce.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
