qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals and businesses applying for or holding alcoholic beverages licenses in Anne Arundel County, specifically partnerships, corporations, clubs, and limited liability companies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill has no impact on State finances and is not expected to materially affect Anne Arundel County operations or finances. Small business impact is expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This category includes the Board of License Commissioners for Anne Arundel County, a state-appointed regulatory body responsible for directly implementing and enforcing the modified residency requirements for alcoholic beverages license applicants within the county.

As the state's chief regulator of the alcohol industry, the Comptroller provides the standardized application forms referenced in the bill that must be updated to reflect the new state-level residency criteria for Anne Arundel County.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, boards, and commissions, including local liquor boards, regarding the interpretation and application of statutory changes to the Alcoholic Beverages Article.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the Maryland income tax definition of a 'captive REIT' to exclude entities owned or controlled by certain qualified foreign entities and specific trusts owned by listed Australian property trusts. As a result, these entities are no longer required to add back the federal dividends paid deduction to their Maryland modified income, which may reduce their corporate income tax liability.
qa_results.stakeholders: Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs), foreign corporations and trusts, Australian property trusts, and Maryland state and local governments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund, Higher Education Investment Fund (HEIF), and Transportation Trust Fund (TTF) revenues may decrease by an indeterminate amount starting in fiscal 2024. Local highway user revenues and corresponding TTF expenditures may also decrease. Specific dollar estimates are unavailable as the Comptroller’s Office requires a thorough examination of individual corporate structures to determine the full impact on corporate income tax revenues.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller is the state's chief tax collector and is responsible for enforcing tax laws. This bill directly alters the definition of 'captive REIT' within the tax code, which impacts the Maryland income tax addition modification that the Comptroller administers.

DBM is responsible for estimating revenues and fiscal planning. The bill's fiscal note indicates an indeterminate decrease in general fund revenues, which DBM must monitor and account for in the state budget.

The fiscal note specifically mentions a decrease in Transportation Trust Fund (TTF) revenues resulting from this bill. MDOT oversees the administrations funded by the TTF and manages the state's transportation network capital grants.

The Maryland Tax Court adjudicates disputes between taxpayers and the Comptroller regarding income tax assessments. Altering definitions of corporate entities (like captive REITs) frequently leads to litigation that this court would hear.

The bill impacts revenues for the Higher Education Investment Fund (HEIF). As the regulatory and coordinating board for higher education, the commission is impacted by changes in its primary funding streams.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Comptroller, and represents the state in tax-related litigation and interpretation of the Tax - General Article.

qa_results.bill_summary: This emergency bill requires contractors operating State-owned nursing homes to promptly notify the Governor, the overseeing State agency, and the General Assembly of any deficiencies or enforcement actions issued by monitoring agencies. Within 30 days of receiving a citation or fine, contractors must provide detailed documentation, including deficiency statements, plans of correction, and survey-related communications. Additionally, the Maryland Department of Health is required to submit an annual report summarizing all federal and State survey activities and enforcement actions at contractor-operated State-owned nursing homes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and contractors operating State-owned nursing homes
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of State-owned nursing homes, private contractors operating these facilities, the Maryland Department of Health, the Maryland Department of Veterans Affairs, the Governor, and the Maryland General Assembly.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Health can implement the bill's reporting and notification requirements using existing budgeted resources. There is no impact on State revenues or local government finances, and no additional funding is mandated.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires contractors operating State-owned nursing homes to promptly provide notice of deficiencies or enforcement actions directly to the Governor. It also requires the Department of Health to submit annual reports on these activities to the Governor.

The bill defines the Office of Health Care Quality (within MDH) as a monitoring agency. MDH is mandated to prepare and submit an annual report to the Governor and General Assembly regarding survey activities and enforcement actions at State-owned nursing homes operated by contractors.

This department (formerly the Department of Veterans Affairs) oversees the Charlotte Hall Veterans Home, which the fiscal note identifies as a State-owned nursing home operated by a private contractor and thus directly subject to the bill's notice and reporting requirements.
